<div id="http.uri">
<section id="section-4.2.1">
          <h4 id="name-http-uri-scheme">
<a href="#section-4.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">4.2.1. </a><a href="#name-http-uri-scheme" class="section-name selfRef">http URI Scheme</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-http-uri-scheme-56" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-uri-scheme-http-57" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-4.2.1-1">
   The "http" URI scheme is hereby defined for minting identifiers within the
   hierarchical namespace governed by a potential HTTP origin server
   listening for TCP (<span>[<a href="#TCP" class="xref">TCP</a>]</span>) connections on a given port.<a href="#section-4.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-http-uri-58" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-4.2.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  http-URI = "http" "://" authority path-abempty [ "?" query ]
</pre><a href="#section-4.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-4.2.1-3">
   The origin server for an "http" URI is identified by the
   <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">authority</a> component, which includes a host identifier
   (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.2.2" class="relref">Section 3.2.2</a></span>)
   and optional port number (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.2.3" class="relref">Section 3.2.3</a></span>).
   If the port subcomponent is empty or not given, TCP port 80 (the
   reserved port for WWW services) is the default.
   The origin determines who has the right to respond authoritatively to
   requests that target the identified resource, as defined in
   <a href="#http.origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.2</a>.<a href="#section-4.2.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.1-4">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate an "http" URI with an empty host identifier.
   A recipient that processes such a URI reference <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> reject it as invalid.<a href="#section-4.2.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.1-5">
   The hierarchical path component and optional query component identify the
   target resource within that origin server's namespace.<a href="#section-4.2.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="https.uri">
<section id="section-4.2.2">
          <h4 id="name-https-uri-scheme">
<a href="#section-4.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">4.2.2. </a><a href="#name-https-uri-scheme" class="section-name selfRef">https URI Scheme</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-https-uri-scheme-59" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-uri-scheme-https-60" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-secured-61" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-4.2.2-1">
   The "https" URI scheme is hereby defined for minting identifiers within the
   hierarchical namespace governed by a potential origin server listening for
   TCP connections on a given port and capable of establishing a TLS
   (<span>[<a href="#TLS13" class="xref">TLS13</a>]</span>) connection that has been secured for HTTP
   communication. In this context, "secured" specifically
   means that the server has been authenticated as acting on behalf of the
   identified authority and all HTTP communication with that server has
   confidentiality and integrity protection that is acceptable to both client
   and server.<a href="#section-4.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-https-uri-62" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-4.2.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  https-URI = "https" "://" authority path-abempty [ "?" query ]
</pre><a href="#section-4.2.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-4.2.2-3">
   The origin server for an "https" URI is identified by the
   <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">authority</a> component, which includes a host identifier
   (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.2.2" class="relref">Section 3.2.2</a></span>)
   and optional port number (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.2.3" class="relref">Section 3.2.3</a></span>).
   If the port subcomponent is empty or not given, TCP port 443
   (the reserved port for HTTP over TLS) is the default.
   The origin determines who has the right to respond authoritatively to
   requests that target the identified resource, as defined in
   <a href="#https.origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.3</a>.<a href="#section-4.2.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.2-4">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate an "https" URI with an empty host identifier.
   A recipient that processes such a URI reference <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> reject it as invalid.<a href="#section-4.2.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.2-5">
   The hierarchical path component and optional query component identify the
   target resource within that origin server's namespace.<a href="#section-4.2.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.2-6">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> ensure that its HTTP requests for an "https" resource are
   secured, prior to being communicated, and that it only accepts secured
   responses to those requests. Note that the definition of what cryptographic
   mechanisms are acceptable to client and server are usually negotiated and
   can change over time.<a href="#section-4.2.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.2-7">
   Resources made available via the "https" scheme have no shared identity
   with the "http" scheme. They are distinct origins with separate namespaces.
   However, extensions to HTTP that are defined as applying to all origins with
   the same host, such as the Cookie protocol <span>[<a href="#COOKIE" class="xref">COOKIE</a>]</span>,
   allow information set by one service to impact communication with other
   services within a matching group of host domains. Such extensions ought to
   be designed with great care to prevent information obtained from a secured
   connection being inadvertently exchanged within an unsecured context.<a href="#section-4.2.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="uri.comparison">
<section id="section-4.2.3">
          <h4 id="name-https-normalization-and-com">
<a href="#section-4.2.3" class="section-number selfRef">4.2.3. </a><a href="#name-https-normalization-and-com" class="section-name selfRef">http(s) Normalization and Comparison</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-4.2.3-1">
   URIs with an "http" or "https" scheme are normalized and compared according to the
   methods defined in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-6" class="relref">Section 6</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>, using
   the defaults described above for each scheme.<a href="#section-4.2.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.3-2">
   HTTP does not require the use of a specific method for determining
   equivalence. For example, a cache key might be compared as a simple
   string, after syntax-based normalization, or after scheme-based
   normalization.<a href="#section-4.2.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.3-3">
   Scheme-based normalization (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-6.2.3" class="relref">Section 6.2.3</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>) of "http" and "https" URIs involves the following
   additional rules:<a href="#section-4.2.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-4.2.3-4.1">If the port is equal to the default port for a scheme, the normal form
   is to omit the port subcomponent.<a href="#section-4.2.3-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-4.2.3-4.2">When not being used as the target of an OPTIONS request, an empty path
   component is equivalent to an absolute path of "/", so the normal form is
   to provide a path of "/" instead.<a href="#section-4.2.3-4.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-4.2.3-4.3">The scheme and host are case-insensitive and normally provided in
   lowercase; all other components are compared in a case-sensitive
   manner.<a href="#section-4.2.3-4.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-4.2.3-4.4">Characters other than those in the "reserved" set are equivalent to
   their percent-encoded octets: the normal form is to not encode them (see
   Sections <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.1" class="relref">2.1</a> and <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.2" class="relref">2.2</a> of <span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-4.2.3-4.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-4.2.3-5">
   For example, the following three URIs are equivalent:<a href="#section-4.2.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-4.2.3-6">
<pre>
   http://example.com:80/~smith/home.html
   http://EXAMPLE.com/%7Esmith/home.html
   http://EXAMPLE.com:/%7esmith/home.html
</pre><a href="#section-4.2.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-4.2.3-7">
   Two HTTP URIs that are equivalent after normalization (using any method)
   can be assumed to identify the same resource, and any HTTP component <span class="bcp14">MAY</span>
   perform normalization. As a result, distinct resources <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> be
   identified by HTTP URIs that are equivalent after normalization (using any
   method defined in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-6.2" class="relref">Section 6.2</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-4.2.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="http.userinfo">
<section id="section-4.2.4">
          <h4 id="name-deprecation-of-userinfo-in-">
<a href="#section-4.2.4" class="section-number selfRef">4.2.4. </a><a href="#name-deprecation-of-userinfo-in-" class="section-name selfRef">Deprecation of userinfo in http(s) URIs</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-4.2.4-1">
   The URI generic syntax for authority also includes a userinfo subcomponent
   (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.2.1" class="relref">Section 3.2.1</a></span>) for including user
   authentication information in the URI. In that subcomponent, the
   use of the format "user:password" is deprecated.<a href="#section-4.2.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.4-2">
   Some implementations make use of the userinfo component for internal
   configuration of authentication information, such as within command
   invocation options, configuration files, or bookmark lists, even
   though such usage might expose a user identifier or password.<a href="#section-4.2.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.4-3">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate the userinfo subcomponent (and its "@"
   delimiter) when an "http" or "https" URI reference is generated within a
   message as a target URI or field value.<a href="#section-4.2.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.2.4-4">
   Before making use of an "http" or "https" URI reference received from an untrusted
   source, a recipient <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> parse for userinfo and treat its presence as
   an error; it is likely being used to obscure the authority for the sake of
   phishing attacks.<a href="#section-4.2.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="uri.fragment.identifiers">
<section id="section-4.2.5">
          <h4 id="name-https-references-with-fragm">
<a href="#section-4.2.5" class="section-number selfRef">4.2.5. </a><a href="#name-https-references-with-fragm" class="section-name selfRef">http(s) References with Fragment Identifiers</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fragment-identifiers-63" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-4.2.5-1">
   Fragment identifiers allow for indirect identification
   of a secondary resource, independent of the URI scheme, as defined in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.5" class="relref">Section 3.5</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>.
   Some protocol elements that refer to a URI allow inclusion of a fragment,
   while others do not. They are distinguished by use of the ABNF rule for
   elements where fragment is allowed; otherwise, a specific rule that excludes
   fragments is used.<a href="#section-4.2.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-4.2.5-2">
            <p id="section-4.2.5-2.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> The fragment identifier component is not part of the scheme
    definition for a URI scheme (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-4.3" class="relref">Section 4.3</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>),
    thus does not appear in the ABNF definitions for the "http" and "https"
    URI schemes above.<a href="#section-4.2.5-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="authoritative.access">
<section id="section-4.3">
        <h3 id="name-authoritative-access">
<a href="#section-4.3" class="section-number selfRef">4.3. </a><a href="#name-authoritative-access" class="section-name selfRef">Authoritative Access</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-4.3-1">
   Authoritative access refers to dereferencing a given identifier,
   for the sake of access to the identified resource, in a way that the client
   believes is authoritative (controlled by the resource owner). The process
   for determining whether access is granted is defined by the URI scheme and often uses
   data within the URI components, such as the authority component when
   the generic syntax is used. However, authoritative access is not limited to
   the identified mechanism.<a href="#section-4.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3-2">
               <a href="#origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.1</a> defines the concept of an origin as an aid to
   such uses, and the subsequent subsections explain how to establish that a
   peer has the authority to represent an origin.<a href="#section-4.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3-3">
   See <a href="#establishing.authority" class="xref">Section 17.1</a> for security considerations
   related to establishing authority.<a href="#section-4.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="origin">
<section id="section-4.3.1">
          <h4 id="name-uri-origin">
<a href="#section-4.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">4.3.1. </a><a href="#name-uri-origin" class="section-name selfRef">URI Origin</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-origin-64" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-uri-origin-65" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-4.3.1-1">
   The "origin" for a given URI is the triple of scheme, host,
   and port after normalizing the scheme and host to lowercase and
   normalizing the port to remove any leading zeros. If port is elided from
   the URI, the default port for that scheme is used. For example, the URI<a href="#section-4.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-4.3.1-2">
<pre>
   https://Example.Com/happy.js
</pre><a href="#section-4.3.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-4.3.1-3">
   would have the origin<a href="#section-4.3.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-4.3.1-4">
<pre>
   { "https", "example.com", "443" }
</pre><a href="#section-4.3.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-4.3.1-5">
   which can also be described as the normalized URI prefix with port always
   present:<a href="#section-4.3.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-4.3.1-6">
<pre>
   https://example.com:443
</pre><a href="#section-4.3.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-4.3.1-7">
   Each origin defines its own namespace and controls how identifiers
   within that namespace are mapped to resources. In turn, how the origin
   responds to valid requests, consistently over time, determines the
   semantics that users will associate with a URI, and the usefulness of
   those semantics is what ultimately transforms these mechanisms into a
   resource for users to reference and access in the future.<a href="#section-4.3.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.1-8">
   Two origins are distinct if they differ in scheme, host, or port. Even
   when it can be verified that the same entity controls two distinct origins,
   the two namespaces under those origins are distinct unless explicitly
   aliased by a server authoritative for that origin.<a href="#section-4.3.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.1-9">
   Origin is also used within HTML and related Web protocols, beyond the
   scope of this document, as described in <span>[<a href="#RFC6454" class="xref">RFC6454</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-4.3.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="http.origin">
<section id="section-4.3.2">
          <h4 id="name-http-origins">
<a href="#section-4.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">4.3.2. </a><a href="#name-http-origins" class="section-name selfRef">http Origins</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-4.3.2-1">
   Although HTTP is independent of the transport protocol, the "http" scheme
   (<a href="#http.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.1</a>) is specific to associating authority with
   whomever controls the origin
   server listening for TCP connections on the indicated port of whatever
   host is identified within the authority component. This is a very weak
   sense of authority because it depends on both client-specific name
   resolution mechanisms and communication that might not be secured from
   an on-path attacker. Nevertheless, it is a sufficient minimum for
   binding "http" identifiers to an origin server for consistent resolution
   within a trusted environment.<a href="#section-4.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.2-2">
   If the host identifier is provided as an IP address, the origin server is
   the listener (if any) on the indicated TCP port at that IP address.
   If host is a registered name, the registered name is an indirect identifier
   for use with a name resolution service, such as DNS, to find an address for
   an appropriate origin server.<a href="#section-4.3.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.2-3">
   When an "http" URI is used within a context that calls for access to the
   indicated resource, a client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> attempt access by resolving the host
   identifier to an IP address, establishing a TCP connection to that
   address on the indicated port, and sending over that connection an HTTP
   request message containing a request target that matches the client's
   target URI (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>).<a href="#section-4.3.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.2-4">
   If the server responds to such a request with a non-interim HTTP response
   message, as described in <a href="#status.codes" class="xref">Section 15</a>, then that response
   is considered an authoritative answer to the client's request.<a href="#section-4.3.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.2-5">
   Note, however, that the above is not the only means for obtaining an
   authoritative response, nor does it imply that an authoritative response
   is always necessary (see <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).
   For example, the Alt-Svc header field <span>[<a href="#ALTSVC" class="xref">ALTSVC</a>]</span> allows an
   origin server to identify other services that are also authoritative for
   that origin. Access to "http" identified resources might also be provided
   by protocols outside the scope of this document.<a href="#section-4.3.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="https.origin">
<section id="section-4.3.3">
          <h4 id="name-https-origins">
<a href="#section-4.3.3" class="section-number selfRef">4.3.3. </a><a href="#name-https-origins" class="section-name selfRef">https Origins</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-4.3.3-1">
   The "https" scheme (<a href="#https.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.2</a>) associates authority based
   on the ability of a server to use the private key corresponding to a
   certificate that the client considers to be trustworthy for the identified
   origin server. The client usually relies upon a chain of trust, conveyed
   from some prearranged or configured trust anchor, to deem a certificate
   trustworthy (<a href="#https.verify" class="xref">Section 4.3.4</a>).<a href="#section-4.3.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-2">
   In HTTP/1.1 and earlier, a client will only attribute authority to a server
   when they are communicating over a successfully established and secured
   connection specifically to that URI origin's host. The connection
   establishment and certificate verification are used as proof of authority.<a href="#section-4.3.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-3">
   In HTTP/2 and HTTP/3, a client will attribute authority to a server when
   they are communicating over a successfully established and secured
   connection if the URI origin's host matches any of the hosts present in the
   server's certificate and the client believes that it could open a connection
   to that host for that URI. In practice, a client will make a DNS query to
   check that the origin's host contains the same server IP address as the
   established connection. This restriction can be removed by the origin server
   sending an equivalent ORIGIN frame <span>[<a href="#RFC8336" class="xref">RFC8336</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-4.3.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-4">
   The request target's host and port value are passed within each HTTP
   request, identifying the origin and distinguishing it from other namespaces
   that might be controlled by the same server (<a href="#field.host" class="xref">Section 7.2</a>).
   It is the origin's responsibility to ensure that any services provided with
   control over its certificate's private key are equally responsible for
   managing the corresponding "https" namespaces or at least prepared to
   reject requests that appear to have been misdirected
   (<a href="#routing.reject" class="xref">Section 7.4</a>).<a href="#section-4.3.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-5">
   An origin server might be unwilling to process requests for certain target
   URIs even when they have the authority to do so. For example, when a host
   operates distinct services on different ports (e.g., 443 and 8000), checking
   the target URI at the origin server is necessary (even after the connection
   has been secured) because a network attacker might cause connections for one
   port to be received at some other port. Failing to check the target URI
   might allow such an attacker to replace a response to one target URI
   (e.g., "https://example.com/foo") with a seemingly authoritative response
   from the other port (e.g., "https://example.com:8000/foo").<a href="#section-4.3.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-6">
   Note that the "https" scheme does not rely on TCP and the connected port
   number for associating authority, since both are outside the secured
   communication and thus cannot be trusted as definitive. Hence, the HTTP
   communication might take place over any channel that has been secured,
   as defined in <a href="#https.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.2</a>, including protocols that don't
   use TCP.<a href="#section-4.3.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-7">
   When an "https" URI is used within a context that calls for access to
   the indicated resource, a client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> attempt access by resolving the
   host identifier to an IP address, establishing a TCP connection to that
   address on the indicated port, securing the connection end-to-end by
   successfully initiating TLS over TCP with confidentiality and integrity
   protection, and sending over that connection an HTTP request message
   containing a request target that matches the client's target URI
   (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>).<a href="#section-4.3.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-8">
   If the server responds to such a request with a non-interim HTTP response
   message, as described in <a href="#status.codes" class="xref">Section 15</a>, then that response
   is considered an authoritative answer to the client's request.<a href="#section-4.3.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.3-9">
   Note, however, that the above is not the only means for obtaining an
   authoritative response, nor does it imply that an authoritative response
   is always necessary (see <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-4.3.3-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="https.verify">
<section id="section-4.3.4">
          <h4 id="name-https-certificate-verificat">
<a href="#section-4.3.4" class="section-number selfRef">4.3.4. </a><a href="#name-https-certificate-verificat" class="section-name selfRef">https Certificate Verification</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-4.3.4-1">
   To establish a <a href="#https.uri" class="xref">secured</a> connection to dereference a URI,
   a client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> verify that the service's identity is an acceptable
   match for the URI's origin server. Certificate verification is used to
   prevent server impersonation by an on-path attacker or by an attacker
   that controls name resolution. This process requires that a client be
   configured with a set of trust anchors.<a href="#section-4.3.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.4-2">
   In general, a client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> verify the service identity using the
   verification process defined in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6125#section-6" class="relref">Section 6</a> of [<a href="#RFC6125" class="xref">RFC6125</a>]</span>. The client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> construct
   a reference identity from the service's host: if the host is a literal IP address
   (<a href="#https.ip-id" class="xref">Section 4.3.5</a>), the reference identity is an IP-ID, otherwise
   the host is a name and the reference identity is a DNS-ID.<a href="#section-4.3.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.4-3">
   A reference identity of type CN-ID <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> be used by clients.  As noted
   in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6125#section-6.2.1" class="relref">Section 6.2.1</a> of [<a href="#RFC6125" class="xref">RFC6125</a>]</span>, a reference
   identity of type CN-ID might be used by older clients.<a href="#section-4.3.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.4-4">
   A client might be specially configured to accept an alternative form of
   server identity verification. For example, a client might be connecting
   to a server whose address and hostname are dynamic, with an expectation that
   the service will present a specific certificate (or a certificate matching
   some externally defined reference identity) rather than one matching the
   target URI's origin.<a href="#section-4.3.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.4-5">
   In special cases, it might be appropriate for
   a client to simply ignore the server's identity, but it must be
   understood that this leaves a connection open to active attack.<a href="#section-4.3.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.4-6">
   If the certificate is not valid for the target URI's origin,
   a user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> either obtain confirmation from the user
   before proceeding (see <a href="#user.agent" class="xref">Section 3.5</a>) or
   terminate the connection with a bad certificate error. Automated
   clients <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> log the error to an appropriate audit log (if available)
   and <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> terminate the connection (with a bad certificate error).
   Automated clients <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> provide a configuration setting that disables
   this check, but <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> provide a setting which enables it.<a href="#section-4.3.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="https.ip-id">
<section id="section-4.3.5">
          <h4 id="name-ip-id-reference-identity">
<a href="#section-4.3.5" class="section-number selfRef">4.3.5. </a><a href="#name-ip-id-reference-identity" class="section-name selfRef">IP-ID Reference Identity</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-4.3.5-1">
   A server that is identified using an IP address literal in the "host" field
   of an "https" URI has a reference identity of type IP-ID.  An IP version 4
   address uses the "IPv4address" ABNF rule, and an IP version 6 address uses
   the "IP-literal" production with the "IPv6address" option; see
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.2.2" class="relref">Section 3.2.2</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>.  A reference identity of
   IP-ID contains the decoded bytes of the IP address.<a href="#section-4.3.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.5-2">
   An IP version 4 address is 4 octets, and an IP version 6 address is 16 octets.
   Use of IP-ID is not defined for any other IP version. The iPAddress
   choice in the certificate subjectAltName extension does not explicitly
   include the IP version and so relies on the length of the address to
   distinguish versions; see
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5280#section-4.2.1.6" class="relref">Section 4.2.1.6</a> of [<a href="#RFC5280" class="xref">RFC5280</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-4.3.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-4.3.5-3">
   A reference identity of type IP-ID matches if the address is identical to
   an iPAddress value of the subjectAltName extension of the certificate.<a href="#section-4.3.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="fields">
<section id="section-5">
      <h2 id="name-fields">
<a href="#section-5" class="section-number selfRef">5. </a><a href="#name-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Fields</a>
      </h2>
<span id="iref-field-66" class="iref"></span>
      <p id="section-5-1">
   HTTP uses "fields" to provide data in the form of extensible
   name/value pairs with a registered key namespace. Fields are sent and
   received within the header and trailer sections of messages
   (<a href="#message.abstraction" class="xref">Section 6</a>).<a href="#section-5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="fields.names">
<section id="section-5.1">
        <h3 id="name-field-names">
<a href="#section-5.1" class="section-number selfRef">5.1. </a><a href="#name-field-names" class="section-name selfRef">Field Names</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-5.1-1">
   A field name labels the corresponding field value as having the
   semantics defined by that name.  For example, the <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a>
   header field is defined in <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Section 6.6.1</a> as containing the
   origination timestamp for the message in which it appears.<a href="#section-5.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-field-name-67" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-5.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  field-name     = token
</pre><a href="#section-5.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.1-3">
   Field names are case-insensitive and ought to be registered within the
   "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Field Name Registry"; see <a href="#fields.registry" class="xref">Section 16.3.1</a>.<a href="#section-5.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.1-4">
   The interpretation of a field does not change between minor
   versions of the same major HTTP version, though the default behavior of a
   recipient in the absence of such a field can change. Unless specified
   otherwise, fields are defined for all versions of HTTP.
   In particular, the <a href="#field.host" class="xref">Host</a> and <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Connection</a>
   fields ought to be recognized by all HTTP implementations
   whether or not they advertise conformance with HTTP/1.1.<a href="#section-5.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.1-5">
   New fields can be introduced without changing the protocol version if
   their defined semantics allow them to be safely ignored by recipients
   that do not recognize them; see <a href="#fields.extensibility" class="xref">Section 16.3</a>.<a href="#section-5.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.1-6">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> forward unrecognized header fields unless the
   field name is listed in the <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Connection</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Section 7.6.1</a>) or the proxy is specifically
   configured to block, or otherwise transform, such fields.
   Other recipients <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> ignore unrecognized header and trailer fields.
   Adhering to these requirements allows HTTP's functionality to be extended
   without updating or removing deployed intermediaries.<a href="#section-5.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.lines">
<section id="section-5.2">
        <h3 id="name-field-lines-and-combined-fi">
<a href="#section-5.2" class="section-number selfRef">5.2. </a><a href="#name-field-lines-and-combined-fi" class="section-name selfRef">Field Lines and Combined Field Value</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-5.2-1">
               <span id="iref-field-line-68" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-field-name-69" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-field-line-value-70" class="iref"></span>
   Field sections are composed of any number of "field lines",
   each with a "field name" (see <a href="#fields.names" class="xref">Section 5.1</a>)
   identifying the field, and a "field line value" that conveys
   data for that instance of the field.<a href="#section-5.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.2-2">
               <span id="iref-field-value-71" class="iref"></span>
   When a field name is only present once in a section, the combined
   "field value" for that field consists of the corresponding
   field line value.
   When a field name is repeated within a section, its combined field value
   consists of the list of corresponding field line values within that section,
   concatenated in order, with each field line value separated by a comma.<a href="#section-5.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.2-3">
   For example, this section:<a href="#section-5.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-5.2-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Example-Field: Foo, Bar
Example-Field: Baz
</pre><a href="#section-5.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.2-5">
   contains two field lines, both with the field name "Example-Field". The
   first field line has a field line value of "Foo, Bar", while the second
   field line value is "Baz". The field value for "Example-Field" is the list
   "Foo, Bar, Baz".<a href="#section-5.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="fields.order">
<section id="section-5.3">
        <h3 id="name-field-order">
<a href="#section-5.3" class="section-number selfRef">5.3. </a><a href="#name-field-order" class="section-name selfRef">Field Order</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-5.3-1">
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> combine multiple field lines within a field section that
   have the same field name
   into one field line, without changing the semantics of the message, by
   appending each subsequent field line value to the initial field line value
   in order, separated by a comma (",") and optional whitespace
   (<a href="#whitespace" class="xref">OWS</a>, defined in <a href="#whitespace" class="xref">Section 5.6.3</a>).
   For consistency, use comma SP.<a href="#section-5.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.3-2">
   The order in which field lines with the
   same name are received is therefore significant to the interpretation of
   the field value; a proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> change the order of these field line
   values when forwarding a message.<a href="#section-5.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.3-3">
   This means that, aside from the well-known exception noted below, a sender
   <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate multiple field lines with the same name in a message
   (whether in the headers or trailers) or append a field line when a field
   line of the same name already exists in the message, unless that field's
   definition allows multiple field line values to be recombined as a
   comma-separated list (i.e., at least one alternative of the field's
   definition allows a comma-separated list, such as an ABNF rule of
   #(values) defined in <a href="#abnf.extension" class="xref">Section 5.6.1</a>).<a href="#section-5.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-5.3-4">
          <p id="section-5.3-4.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> In practice, the "Set-Cookie" header field (<span>[<a href="#COOKIE" class="xref">COOKIE</a>]</span>)
   often appears in a response message across multiple field lines and does not
   use the list syntax, violating the above requirements on multiple field lines
   with the same field name. Since it cannot be combined into a single field
   value, recipients ought to handle "Set-Cookie" as a special case while
   processing fields. (See Appendix A.2.3 of <span>[<a href="#Kri2001" class="xref">Kri2001</a>]</span> for
   details.)<a href="#section-5.3-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<p id="section-5.3-5">
   The order in which field lines with differing field names are received in a
   section is not significant. However, it is good practice to send header
   fields that contain additional control data first, such as
   <a href="#field.host" class="xref">Host</a> on requests and <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a> on responses, so
   that implementations can decide when not to handle a message as early as
   possible.<a href="#section-5.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.3-6">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> apply a request to the target resource until it
   receives the entire request header section, since later header field lines
   might include conditionals, authentication credentials, or deliberately
   misleading duplicate header fields that could impact request processing.<a href="#section-5.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="fields.limits">
<section id="section-5.4">
        <h3 id="name-field-limits">
<a href="#section-5.4" class="section-number selfRef">5.4. </a><a href="#name-field-limits" class="section-name selfRef">Field Limits</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-5.4-1">
   HTTP does not place a predefined limit on the length of each field line, field value,
   or on the length of a header or trailer section as a whole, as described in
   <a href="#conformance" class="xref">Section 2</a>. Various ad hoc limitations on individual
   lengths are found in practice, often depending on the specific
   field's semantics.<a href="#section-5.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.4-2">
   A server that receives a request header field line, field value, or set of
   fields larger than it wishes to process <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> respond with an appropriate
   <a href="#status.4xx" class="xref">4xx (Client Error)</a> status code. Ignoring such header fields
   would increase the server's vulnerability to request smuggling attacks
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-11.2" class="relref">Section 11.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-5.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.4-3">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> discard or truncate received field lines that are larger
   than the client wishes to process if the field semantics are such that the
   dropped value(s) can be safely ignored without changing the
   message framing or response semantics.<a href="#section-5.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="fields.values">
<section id="section-5.5">
        <h3 id="name-field-values">
<a href="#section-5.5" class="section-number selfRef">5.5. </a><a href="#name-field-values" class="section-name selfRef">Field Values</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-5.5-1">
   HTTP field values consist of a sequence of characters in a format defined
   by the field's grammar. Each field's grammar is usually defined using
   ABNF (<span>[<a href="#RFC5234" class="xref">RFC5234</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-5.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-field-value-72" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-grammar-field-vchar-73" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-grammar-field-content-74" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-grammar-obs-text-75" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-5.5-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  field-value    = *field-content
  field-content  = field-vchar
                   [ 1*( SP / HTAB / field-vchar ) field-vchar ]
  field-vchar    = VCHAR / obs-text
  obs-text       = %x80-FF
</pre><a href="#section-5.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.5-3">
   A field value does not include leading or trailing whitespace. When a
   specific version of HTTP allows such whitespace to appear in a message,
   a field parsing implementation <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> exclude such whitespace prior to
   evaluating the field value.<a href="#section-5.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-4">
   Field values are usually constrained to the range of US-ASCII characters
   <span>[<a href="#USASCII" class="xref">USASCII</a>]</span>.
   Fields needing a greater range of characters can use an encoding,
   such as the one defined in <span>[<a href="#RFC8187" class="xref">RFC8187</a>]</span>.
   Historically, HTTP allowed field content with text in the ISO-8859-1
   charset <span>[<a href="#ISO-8859-1" class="xref">ISO-8859-1</a>]</span>, supporting other charsets only
   through use of <span>[<a href="#RFC2047" class="xref">RFC2047</a>]</span> encoding.
   Specifications for newly defined fields <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> limit their values to
   visible US-ASCII octets (VCHAR), SP, and HTAB.
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> treat other allowed octets in field content
   (i.e., <a href="#fields.values" class="xref">obs-text</a>) as opaque data.<a href="#section-5.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-5">
   Field values containing CR, LF, or NUL characters are invalid and dangerous,
   due to the varying ways that implementations might parse and interpret
   those characters; a recipient of CR, LF, or NUL within a field value <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
   either reject the message or replace each of those characters with SP
   before further processing or forwarding of that message. Field values
   containing other CTL characters are also invalid; however,
   recipients <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> retain such characters for the sake of robustness when
   they appear within a safe context (e.g., an application-specific quoted
   string that will not be processed by any downstream HTTP parser).<a href="#section-5.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-6">
               <span id="iref-singleton-field-76" class="iref"></span>
   Fields that only anticipate a single member as the field value are
   referred to as "singleton fields".<a href="#section-5.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-7">
               <span id="iref-list-based-field-77" class="iref"></span>
   Fields that allow multiple members as the field value are referred to as
   "list-based fields". The list operator extension of
   <a href="#abnf.extension" class="xref">Section 5.6.1</a> is used as a common notation for defining
   field values that can contain multiple members.<a href="#section-5.5-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-8">
   Because commas (",") are used as the delimiter between members, they need
   to be treated with care if they are allowed as data within a member. This
   is true for both list-based and singleton fields, since a singleton field
   might be erroneously sent with multiple members and detecting such errors
   improves interoperability. Fields that expect to contain a
   comma within a member, such as within an <a href="#http.date" class="xref">HTTP-date</a> or
   <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">URI-reference</a>
   element, ought to be defined with delimiters around that element to
   distinguish any comma within that data from potential list separators.<a href="#section-5.5-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-9">
   For example, a textual date and a URI (either of which might contain a comma)
   could be safely carried in list-based field values like these:<a href="#section-5.5-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-5.5-10">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Example-URIs: "http://example.com/a.html,foo",
              "http://without-a-comma.example.com/"
Example-Dates: "Sat, 04 May 1996", "Wed, 14 Sep 2005"
</pre><a href="#section-5.5-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.5-11">
   Note that double-quote delimiters are almost always used with the
   quoted-string production (<a href="#quoted.strings" class="xref">Section 5.6.4</a>); using a different syntax inside double-quotes
   will likely cause unnecessary confusion.<a href="#section-5.5-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.5-12">
   Many fields (such as <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a>, defined in
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Section 8.3</a>) use a common syntax for parameters
   that allows both unquoted (token) and quoted (quoted-string) syntax for
   a parameter value (<a href="#parameter" class="xref">Section 5.6.6</a>). Use of common syntax
   allows recipients to reuse existing parser components. When allowing both
   forms, the meaning of a parameter value ought to be the same whether it
   was received as a token or a quoted string.<a href="#section-5.5-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-5.5-13">
          <p id="section-5.5-13.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> For defining field value syntax, this specification uses an ABNF
      rule named after the field name to define the allowed grammar for that
      field's value (after said value has been extracted from the underlying
      messaging syntax and multiple instances combined into a list).<a href="#section-5.5-13.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="fields.components">
<section id="section-5.6">
        <h3 id="name-common-rules-for-defining-f">
<a href="#section-5.6" class="section-number selfRef">5.6. </a><a href="#name-common-rules-for-defining-f" class="section-name selfRef">Common Rules for Defining Field Values</a>
        </h3>
<div id="abnf.extension">
<section id="section-5.6.1">
          <h4 id="name-lists-rule-abnf-extension">
<a href="#section-5.6.1" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.1. </a><a href="#name-lists-rule-abnf-extension" class="section-name selfRef">Lists (#rule ABNF Extension)</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-5.6.1-1">
   A #rule extension to the ABNF rules of <span>[<a href="#RFC5234" class="xref">RFC5234</a>]</span> is used to
   improve readability in the definitions of some list-based field values.<a href="#section-5.6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.1-2">
   A construct "#" is defined, similar to "*", for defining comma-delimited
   lists of elements. The full form is "<n>#<m>element" indicating
   at least <n> and at most <m> elements, each separated by a single
   comma (",") and optional whitespace (<a href="#whitespace" class="xref">OWS</a>,
   defined in <a href="#whitespace" class="xref">Section 5.6.3</a>).<a href="#section-5.6.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="abnf.extension.sender">
<section id="section-5.6.1.1">
            <h5 id="name-sender-requirements">
<a href="#section-5.6.1.1" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.1.1. </a><a href="#name-sender-requirements" class="section-name selfRef">Sender Requirements</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-5.6.1.1-1">
   In any production that uses the list construct, a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span>
   generate empty list elements. In other words, a sender has to generate
   lists that satisfy the following syntax:<a href="#section-5.6.1.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.1-2">
<pre>
  1#element => element *( OWS "," OWS element )
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.1.1-3">
   and:<a href="#section-5.6.1.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.1-4">
<pre>
  #element => [ 1#element ]
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.1.1-5">
   and for n >= 1 and m > 1:<a href="#section-5.6.1.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.1-6">
<pre>
  <n>#<m>element => element <n-1>*<m-1>( OWS "," OWS element )
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.1.1-7">
                     <a href="#collected.abnf" class="xref">Appendix A</a> shows the collected ABNF for senders
   after the list constructs have been expanded.<a href="#section-5.6.1.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="abnf.extension.recipient">
<section id="section-5.6.1.2">
            <h5 id="name-recipient-requirements">
<a href="#section-5.6.1.2" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.1.2. </a><a href="#name-recipient-requirements" class="section-name selfRef">Recipient Requirements</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-5.6.1.2-1">
   Empty elements do not contribute to the count of elements present.
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> parse and ignore
   a reasonable number of empty list elements: enough to handle common mistakes
   by senders that merge values, but not so much that they could be used as a
   denial-of-service mechanism. In other words, a recipient <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> accept lists
   that satisfy the following syntax:<a href="#section-5.6.1.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.2-2">
<pre>
  #element => [ element ] *( OWS "," OWS [ element ] )
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.1.2-3">
   Note that because of the potential presence of empty list elements, the
   RFC 5234 ABNF cannot enforce the cardinality of list elements, and
   consequently all cases are mapped as if there was no cardinality specified.<a href="#section-5.6.1.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.1.2-4">
   For example, given these ABNF productions:<a href="#section-5.6.1.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.2-5">
<pre>
  example-list      = 1#example-list-elmt
  example-list-elmt = token ; see Section 5.6.2
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.1.2-6">
   Then the following are valid values for example-list (not including the
   double quotes, which are present for delimitation only):<a href="#section-5.6.1.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.2-7">
<pre>
  "foo,bar"
  "foo ,bar,"
  "foo , ,bar,charlie"
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.1.2-8">
   In contrast, the following values would be invalid, since at least one
   non-empty element is required by the example-list production:<a href="#section-5.6.1.2-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.1.2-9">
<pre>
  ""
  ","
  ",   ,"
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.1.2-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="tokens">
<section id="section-5.6.2">
          <h4 id="name-tokens">
<a href="#section-5.6.2" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.2. </a><a href="#name-tokens" class="section-name selfRef">Tokens</a>
          </h4>
<div id="rule.token.separators">
<p id="section-5.6.2-1">
  
  
  Tokens are short textual identifiers that do not include whitespace or
  delimiters.<a href="#section-5.6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-token-78" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-tchar-79" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  token          = 1*tchar

  tchar          = "!" / "#" / "$" / "%" / "&" / "'" / "*"
                 / "+" / "-" / "." / "^" / "_" / "`" / "|" / "~"
                 / DIGIT / ALPHA
                 ; any VCHAR, except delimiters
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<div id="delimiters">
<p id="section-5.6.2-3">
                  <span id="iref-delimiters-80" class="iref"></span>
   Many HTTP field values are defined using common syntax
   components, separated by whitespace or specific delimiting characters.
   Delimiters are chosen from the set of US-ASCII visual characters not
   allowed in a <a href="#rule.token.separators" class="xref">token</a> (DQUOTE and "(),/:;<=>?@[\]{}").<a href="#section-5.6.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="whitespace">
<section id="section-5.6.3">
          <h4 id="name-whitespace">
<a href="#section-5.6.3" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.3. </a><a href="#name-whitespace" class="section-name selfRef">Whitespace</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-5.6.3-1">
   This specification uses three rules to denote the use of linear
   whitespace: OWS (optional whitespace), RWS (required whitespace), and
   BWS ("bad" whitespace).<a href="#section-5.6.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.3-2">
   The OWS rule is used where zero or more linear whitespace octets might
   appear. For protocol elements where optional whitespace is preferred to
   improve readability, a sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate the optional whitespace
   as a single SP; otherwise, a sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate optional
   whitespace except as needed to overwrite invalid or unwanted protocol
   elements during in-place message filtering.<a href="#section-5.6.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.3-3">
   The RWS rule is used when at least one linear whitespace octet is required
   to separate field tokens. A sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate RWS as a single SP.<a href="#section-5.6.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.3-4">
   OWS and RWS have the same semantics as a single SP. Any content known to
   be defined as OWS or RWS <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> be replaced with a single SP before
   interpreting it or forwarding the message downstream.<a href="#section-5.6.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.3-5">
   The BWS rule is used where the grammar allows optional whitespace only for
   historical reasons. A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate BWS in messages.
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> parse for such bad whitespace and remove it before
   interpreting the protocol element.<a href="#section-5.6.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.3-6">
   BWS has no semantics. Any content known to be
   defined as BWS <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> be removed before interpreting it or forwarding the
   message downstream.<a href="#section-5.6.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-ows-81" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-rws-82" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-bws-83" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.3-7">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  OWS            = *( SP / HTAB )
                 ; optional whitespace
  RWS            = 1*( SP / HTAB )
                 ; required whitespace
  BWS            = OWS
                 ; "bad" whitespace
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="quoted.strings">
<section id="section-5.6.4">
          <h4 id="name-quoted-strings">
<a href="#section-5.6.4" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.4. </a><a href="#name-quoted-strings" class="section-name selfRef">Quoted Strings</a>
          </h4>
<div id="rule.quoted-string">
<p id="section-5.6.4-1">
  
  
   A string of text is parsed as a single value if it is quoted using
   double-quote marks.<a href="#section-5.6.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-quoted-string-84" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-qdtext-85" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.4-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  quoted-string  = DQUOTE *( qdtext / quoted-pair ) DQUOTE
  qdtext         = HTAB / SP / %x21 / %x23-5B / %x5D-7E / obs-text
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<div id="rule.quoted-pair">
<p id="section-5.6.4-3">
  
   The backslash octet ("\") can be used as a single-octet
   quoting mechanism within quoted-string and comment constructs.
   Recipients that process the value of a quoted-string <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> handle a
   quoted-pair as if it were replaced by the octet following the backslash.<a href="#section-5.6.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-quoted-pair-86" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.4-4">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  quoted-pair    = "\" ( HTAB / SP / VCHAR / obs-text )
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.4-5">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate a quoted-pair in a quoted-string except
   where necessary to quote DQUOTE and backslash octets occurring within that
   string.
   A sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate a quoted-pair in a comment except
   where necessary to quote parentheses ["(" and ")"] and backslash octets
   occurring within that comment.<a href="#section-5.6.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="comments">
<section id="section-5.6.5">
          <h4 id="name-comments">
<a href="#section-5.6.5" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.5. </a><a href="#name-comments" class="section-name selfRef">Comments</a>
          </h4>
<div id="rule.comment">
<p id="section-5.6.5-1">
  
  
   Comments can be included in some HTTP fields by surrounding
   the comment text with parentheses. Comments are only allowed in
   fields containing "comment" as part of their field value definition.<a href="#section-5.6.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-comment-87" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-ctext-88" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.5-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  comment        = "(" *( ctext / quoted-pair / comment ) ")"
  ctext          = HTAB / SP / %x21-27 / %x2A-5B / %x5D-7E / obs-text
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="parameter">
<section id="section-5.6.6">
          <h4 id="name-parameters">
<a href="#section-5.6.6" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.6. </a><a href="#name-parameters" class="section-name selfRef">Parameters</a>
          </h4>
<div id="rule.parameter">
<p id="section-5.6.6-1">
   
   
   
   Parameters are instances of name/value pairs; they are often used in field
   values as a common syntax for appending auxiliary information to an item.
   Each parameter is usually delimited by an immediately preceding semicolon.<a href="#section-5.6.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-parameters-89" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-parameter-90" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-parameter-name-91" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-parameter-value-92" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.6-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  parameters      = *( OWS ";" OWS [ parameter ] )
  parameter       = parameter-name "=" parameter-value
  parameter-name  = token
  parameter-value = ( token / quoted-string )
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.6-3">
   Parameter names are case-insensitive. Parameter values might or might
   not be case-sensitive, depending on the semantics of the parameter
   name. Examples of parameters and some equivalent forms can be seen in
   media types (<a href="#media.type" class="xref">Section 8.3.1</a>) and the Accept header field
   (<a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Section 12.5.1</a>).<a href="#section-5.6.6-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.6-4">
   A parameter value that matches the <a href="#rule.token.separators" class="xref">token</a> production can be
   transmitted either as a token or within a quoted-string. The quoted and
   unquoted values are equivalent.<a href="#section-5.6.6-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-5.6.6-5">
            <p id="section-5.6.6-5.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Parameters do not allow whitespace (not even "bad" whitespace)
    around the "=" character.<a href="#section-5.6.6-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="http.date">
<section id="section-5.6.7">
          <h4 id="name-date-time-formats">
<a href="#section-5.6.7" class="section-number selfRef">5.6.7. </a><a href="#name-date-time-formats" class="section-name selfRef">Date/Time Formats</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-clock-93" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-5.6.7-1">
   Prior to 1995, there were three different formats commonly used by servers
   to communicate timestamps.  For compatibility with old implementations, all
   three are defined here. The preferred format is a fixed-length and
   single-zone subset of the date and time specification used by the
   Internet Message Format <span>[<a href="#RFC5322" class="xref">RFC5322</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-5.6.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-http-date-94" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.7-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  HTTP-date    = IMF-fixdate / obs-date
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.7-3">
   An example of the preferred format is<a href="#section-5.6.7-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.7-4">
<pre>
  Sun, 06 Nov 1994 08:49:37 GMT    ; IMF-fixdate
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.7-5">
   Examples of the two obsolete formats are<a href="#section-5.6.7-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-5.6.7-6">
<pre>
  Sunday, 06-Nov-94 08:49:37 GMT   ; obsolete RFC 850 format
  Sun Nov  6 08:49:37 1994         ; ANSI C's asctime() format
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.7-7">
   A recipient that parses a timestamp value in an HTTP field <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
   accept all three HTTP-date formats. When a sender generates a field
   that contains one or more timestamps defined as HTTP-date,
   the sender <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate those timestamps in the IMF-fixdate format.<a href="#section-5.6.7-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.7-8">
   An HTTP-date value represents time as an instance of Coordinated
   Universal Time (UTC). The first two formats indicate UTC by the
   three-letter abbreviation for Greenwich Mean Time, "GMT", a predecessor
   of the UTC name; values in the asctime format are assumed to be in UTC.<a href="#section-5.6.7-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.7-9">
   A "clock" is an implementation capable of providing a
   reasonable approximation of the current instant in UTC.
   A clock implementation ought to use NTP (<span>[<a href="#RFC5905" class="xref">RFC5905</a>]</span>),
   or some similar protocol, to synchronize with UTC.<a href="#section-5.6.7-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="preferred.date.format">
<p id="section-5.6.7-10">
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  Preferred format:<a href="#section-5.6.7-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-imf-fixdate-95" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-date1-96" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-time-of-day-97" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-hour-98" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-minute-99" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-second-100" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-day-name-101" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-day-name-l-102" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-day-103" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-month-104" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-year-105" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-gmt-106" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.7-11">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  IMF-fixdate  = day-name "," SP date1 SP time-of-day SP GMT
  ; fixed length/zone/capitalization subset of the format
  ; see Section 3.3 of [RFC5322]

  day-name     = %s"Mon" / %s"Tue" / %s"Wed"
               / %s"Thu" / %s"Fri" / %s"Sat" / %s"Sun"

  date1        = day SP month SP year
               ; e.g., 02 Jun 1982

  day          = 2DIGIT
  month        = %s"Jan" / %s"Feb" / %s"Mar" / %s"Apr"
               / %s"May" / %s"Jun" / %s"Jul" / %s"Aug"
               / %s"Sep" / %s"Oct" / %s"Nov" / %s"Dec"
  year         = 4DIGIT

  GMT          = %s"GMT"

  time-of-day  = hour ":" minute ":" second
               ; 00:00:00 - 23:59:60 (leap second)

  hour         = 2DIGIT
  minute       = 2DIGIT
  second       = 2DIGIT
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<div id="obsolete.date.formats">
<p id="section-5.6.7-12">
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  Obsolete formats:<a href="#section-5.6.7-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-obs-date-107" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.7-13">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  obs-date     = rfc850-date / asctime-date
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-rfc850-date-108" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.7-14">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  rfc850-date  = day-name-l "," SP date2 SP time-of-day SP GMT
  date2        = day "-" month "-" 2DIGIT
               ; e.g., 02-Jun-82

  day-name-l   = %s"Monday" / %s"Tuesday" / %s"Wednesday"
               / %s"Thursday" / %s"Friday" / %s"Saturday"
               / %s"Sunday"
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-asctime-date-109" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-5.6.7-15">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  asctime-date = day-name SP date3 SP time-of-day SP year
  date3        = month SP ( 2DIGIT / ( SP 1DIGIT ))
               ; e.g., Jun  2
</pre><a href="#section-5.6.7-15" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-5.6.7-16">
   HTTP-date is case sensitive. Note that <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2" class="relref">Section 4.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span> relaxes this for cache recipients.<a href="#section-5.6.7-16" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.7-17">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate additional whitespace in an HTTP-date beyond
   that specifically included as SP in the grammar.
   The semantics of <a href="#preferred.date.format" class="xref">day-name</a>, <a href="#preferred.date.format" class="xref">day</a>,
   <a href="#preferred.date.format" class="xref">month</a>, <a href="#preferred.date.format" class="xref">year</a>, and <a href="#preferred.date.format" class="xref">time-of-day</a>
   are the same as those defined for the Internet Message Format constructs
   with the corresponding name (<span>[<a href="#RFC5322" class="xref">RFC5322</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322#section-3.3" class="relref">Section 3.3</a></span>).<a href="#section-5.6.7-17" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.7-18">
   Recipients of a timestamp value in rfc850-date format, which uses a
   two-digit year, <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> interpret a timestamp that appears to be more
   than 50 years in the future as representing the most recent year in the
   past that had the same last two digits.<a href="#section-5.6.7-18" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-5.6.7-19">
   Recipients of timestamp values are encouraged to be robust in parsing
   timestamps unless otherwise restricted by the field definition.
   For example, messages are occasionally forwarded over HTTP from a non-HTTP
   source that might generate any of the date and time specifications defined
   by the Internet Message Format.<a href="#section-5.6.7-19" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-5.6.7-20">
            <p id="section-5.6.7-20.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> HTTP requirements for timestamp formats apply only
    to their usage within the protocol stream. Implementations are
    not required to use these formats for user presentation, request
    logging, etc.<a href="#section-5.6.7-20.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="message.abstraction">
<section id="section-6">
      <h2 id="name-message-abstraction">
<a href="#section-6" class="section-number selfRef">6. </a><a href="#name-message-abstraction" class="section-name selfRef">Message Abstraction</a>
      </h2>
<span id="iref-message-abstraction-110" class="iref"></span>
      <span id="iref-message-111" class="iref"></span>
      <span id="iref-self-descriptive-112" class="iref"></span>
      <p id="section-6-1">
   Each major version of HTTP defines its own syntax for communicating
   messages. This section defines an abstract data type for HTTP messages
   based on a generalization of those message characteristics, common structure,
   and capacity for conveying semantics. This abstraction is used to define
   requirements on senders and recipients that are independent of the HTTP
   version, such that a message in one version can be relayed through other
   versions without changing its meaning.<a href="#section-6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6-2">
   A "message" consists of the following:<a href="#section-6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-6-3.1">control data to describe and route the message,<a href="#section-6-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-6-3.2">a headers lookup table of name/value pairs for extending that control
      data and conveying additional information about the sender, message,
      content, or context,<a href="#section-6-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-6-3.3">a potentially unbounded stream of content, and<a href="#section-6-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-6-3.4">a trailers lookup table of name/value pairs for communicating information
      obtained while sending the content.<a href="#section-6-3.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
      </ul>
<p id="section-6-4">
   Framing and control data is sent first, followed by a header section
   containing fields for the headers table. When a message includes content,
   the content is sent after the header section, potentially followed by a
   trailer section that might contain fields for the trailers table.<a href="#section-6-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6-5">
   Messages are expected to be processed as a stream, wherein the purpose of
   that stream and its continued processing is revealed while being read.
   Hence, control data describes what the recipient needs to know immediately,
   header fields describe what needs to be known before receiving content,
   the content (when present) presumably contains what the recipient wants or
   needs to fulfill the message semantics, and trailer fields provide
   optional metadata that was unknown prior to sending the content.<a href="#section-6-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6-6">
   Messages are intended to be "self-descriptive":
   everything a recipient needs to know about the message can be determined by
   looking at the message itself, after decoding or reconstituting parts that
   have been compressed or elided in transit, without requiring an
   understanding of the sender's current application state (established via
   prior messages). However, a client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> retain knowledge of the request when
   parsing, interpreting, or caching a corresponding response. For example,
   responses to the <a href="#HEAD" class="xref">HEAD</a> method look just like the beginning of a
   response to <a href="#GET" class="xref">GET</a> but cannot be parsed in the same manner.<a href="#section-6-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6-7">
   Note that this message abstraction is a generalization across many versions
   of HTTP, including features that might not be found in some versions. For
   example, trailers were introduced within the HTTP/1.1 chunked transfer
   coding as a trailer section after the content. An equivalent feature is
   present in HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 within the header block that terminates each
   stream.<a href="#section-6-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="message.framing">
<section id="section-6.1">
        <h3 id="name-framing-and-completeness">
<a href="#section-6.1" class="section-number selfRef">6.1. </a><a href="#name-framing-and-completeness" class="section-name selfRef">Framing and Completeness</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-complete-113" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-incomplete-114" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-6.1-1">
   Message framing indicates how each message begins and ends, such that each
   message can be distinguished from other messages or noise on the same
   connection. Each major version of HTTP defines its own framing mechanism.<a href="#section-6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.1-2">
   HTTP/0.9 and early deployments of HTTP/1.0 used closure of the underlying
   connection to end a response. For backwards compatibility, this implicit
   framing is also allowed in HTTP/1.1. However, implicit framing can fail to
   distinguish an incomplete response if the connection closes early. For
   that reason, almost all modern implementations use explicit framing in
   the form of length-delimited sequences of message data.<a href="#section-6.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.1-3">
   A message is considered "complete" when all of the octets
   indicated by its framing are available. Note that,
   when no explicit framing is used, a response message that is ended
   by the underlying connection's close is considered complete even though it
   might be indistinguishable from an incomplete response, unless a
   transport-level error indicates that it is not complete.<a href="#section-6.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="message.control.data">
<section id="section-6.2">
        <h3 id="name-control-data">
<a href="#section-6.2" class="section-number selfRef">6.2. </a><a href="#name-control-data" class="section-name selfRef">Control Data</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-control-data-115" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-6.2-1">
   Messages start with control data that describe its primary purpose. Request
   message control data includes a request method (<a href="#methods" class="xref">Section 9</a>),
   request target (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>), and protocol version
   (<a href="#protocol.version" class="xref">Section 2.5</a>). Response message control data includes
   a status code (<a href="#status.codes" class="xref">Section 15</a>), optional reason phrase, and
   protocol version.<a href="#section-6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-2">
   In HTTP/1.1 (<span>[<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) and earlier, control data is sent
   as the first line of a message. In HTTP/2 (<span>[<a href="#HTTP2" class="xref">HTTP/2</a>]</span>) and
   HTTP/3 (<span>[<a href="#HTTP3" class="xref">HTTP/3</a>]</span>), control data is sent as pseudo-header
   fields with a reserved name prefix (e.g., ":authority").<a href="#section-6.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-3">
   Every HTTP message has a protocol version. Depending on the version in use,
   it might be identified within the message explicitly or inferred by the
   connection over which the message is received. Recipients use that version
   information to determine limitations or potential for later communication
   with that sender.<a href="#section-6.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-4">
   When a message is forwarded by an intermediary, the protocol version is
   updated to reflect the version used by that intermediary.
   The <a href="#field.via" class="xref">Via</a> header field (<a href="#field.via" class="xref">Section 7.6.3</a>) is used to
   communicate upstream protocol information within a forwarded message.<a href="#section-6.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-5">
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a request version equal to the highest
   version to which the client is conformant and
   whose major version is no higher than the highest version supported
   by the server, if this is known.  A client <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a
   version to which it is not conformant.<a href="#section-6.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-6">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a lower request version if it is known that
   the server incorrectly implements the HTTP specification, but only
   after the client has attempted at least one normal request and determined
   from the response status code or header fields (e.g., <a href="#field.server" class="xref">Server</a>) that
   the server improperly handles higher request versions.<a href="#section-6.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-7">
   A server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a response version equal to the highest version to
   which the server is conformant that has a major version less than or equal
   to the one received in the request.
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a version to which it is not conformant.
   A server can send a <a href="#status.505" class="xref">505 (HTTP Version Not Supported)</a>
   response if it wishes, for any reason, to refuse service of the client's
   major protocol version.<a href="#section-6.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.2-8">
   A recipient that receives a message with a major version number that it
   implements and a minor version number higher than what it implements
   <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> process the message as if it
