   were in the highest minor version within that major version to which the
   recipient is conformant. A recipient can assume that a message with a
   higher minor version, when sent to a recipient that has not yet indicated
   support for that higher version, is sufficiently backwards-compatible to be
   safely processed by any implementation of the same major version.<a href="#section-6.2-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="header.fields">
<section id="section-6.3">
        <h3 id="name-header-fields">
<a href="#section-6.3" class="section-number selfRef">6.3. </a><a href="#name-header-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Header Fields</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-header-section-116" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-field-117" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-6.3-1">
   Fields (<a href="#fields" class="xref">Section 5</a>) that are sent or received before the content
   are referred to as "header fields" (or just "headers", colloquially).<a href="#section-6.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.3-2">
   The "header section" of a message consists of a sequence of
   header field lines. Each header field might modify or extend message
   semantics, describe the sender, define the content, or provide additional
   context.<a href="#section-6.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-6.3-3">
          <p id="section-6.3-3.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> We refer to named fields specifically as a "header field" when they
    are only allowed to be sent in the header section.<a href="#section-6.3-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="content">
<section id="section-6.4">
        <h3 id="name-content">
<a href="#section-6.4" class="section-number selfRef">6.4. </a><a href="#name-content" class="section-name selfRef">Content</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-content-118" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-6.4-1">
   HTTP messages often transfer a complete or partial representation as the
   message "content": a stream of octets sent after the header
   section, as delineated by the message framing.<a href="#section-6.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4-2">
   This abstract definition of content reflects the data after it has been
   extracted from the message framing. For example, an HTTP/1.1 message body
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-6" class="relref">Section 6</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) might consist of a stream of data encoded
   with the chunked transfer coding -- a sequence of data chunks, one
   zero-length chunk, and a trailer section -- whereas
   the content of that same message
   includes only the data stream after the transfer coding has been decoded;
   it does not include the chunk lengths, chunked framing syntax, nor the
   trailer fields (<a href="#trailer.fields" class="xref">Section 6.5</a>).<a href="#section-6.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-6.4-3">
          <p id="section-6.4-3.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> Some field names have a "Content-" prefix. This is an informal
      convention; while some of these fields refer to the content of the
      message, as defined above, others are scoped to the selected representation
      (<a href="#representations" class="xref">Section 3.2</a>). See the individual field's
      definition to disambiguate.<a href="#section-6.4-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<div id="content.semantics">
<section id="section-6.4.1">
          <h4 id="name-content-semantics">
<a href="#section-6.4.1" class="section-number selfRef">6.4.1. </a><a href="#name-content-semantics" class="section-name selfRef">Content Semantics</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-6.4.1-1">
   The purpose of content in a request is defined by the method semantics
   (<a href="#methods" class="xref">Section 9</a>).<a href="#section-6.4.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-2">
   For example, a representation in the content of a PUT request
   (<a href="#PUT" class="xref">Section 9.3.4</a>) represents the desired state of the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> after the request is successfully applied,
   whereas a representation in the content of a POST request
   (<a href="#POST" class="xref">Section 9.3.3</a>) represents information to be processed by the
   target resource.<a href="#section-6.4.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-3">
   In a response, the content's purpose is defined by the request method,
   response status code (<a href="#status.codes" class="xref">Section 15</a>), and response
   fields describing that content.
   For example, the content of a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response to GET
   (<a href="#GET" class="xref">Section 9.3.1</a>) represents the current state of the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>, as observed at the time of the message
   origination date (<a href="#field.date" class="xref">Section 6.6.1</a>), whereas the content of
   the same status code in a response to POST might represent either the
   processing result or the new state of the target resource after applying
   the processing.<a href="#section-6.4.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-4">
   The content of a <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a> response to GET
   contains either a single part of the selected representation or a
   multipart message body containing multiple parts of that representation,
   as described in <a href="#status.206" class="xref">Section 15.3.7</a>.<a href="#section-6.4.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-5">
   Response messages with an error status code usually contain content that
   represents the error condition, such that the content describes the
   error state and what steps are suggested for resolving it.<a href="#section-6.4.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-6">
   Responses to the HEAD request method (<a href="#HEAD" class="xref">Section 9.3.2</a>) never include
   content; the associated response header fields indicate only
   what their values would have been if the request method had been GET
   (<a href="#GET" class="xref">Section 9.3.1</a>).<a href="#section-6.4.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-7">
                  <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a> responses to a CONNECT request method
   (<a href="#CONNECT" class="xref">Section 9.3.6</a>) switch the connection to tunnel mode instead of
   having content.<a href="#section-6.4.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-8">
   All <a href="#status.1xx" class="xref">1xx (Informational)</a>, <a href="#status.204" class="xref">204 (No Content)</a>, and
   <a href="#status.304" class="xref">304 (Not Modified)</a> responses do not include content.<a href="#section-6.4.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.1-9">
   All other responses do include content, although that content
   might be of zero length.<a href="#section-6.4.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="identifying.content">
<section id="section-6.4.2">
          <h4 id="name-identifying-content">
<a href="#section-6.4.2" class="section-number selfRef">6.4.2. </a><a href="#name-identifying-content" class="section-name selfRef">Identifying Content</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-6.4.2-1">
   When a complete or partial representation is transferred as message
   content, it is often desirable for the sender to supply, or the recipient
   to determine, an identifier for a resource corresponding to that specific
   representation. For example, a client making a GET request on a resource
   for "the current weather report" might want an identifier specific to the
   content returned (e.g., "weather report for Laguna Beach at 20210720T1711").
   This can be useful for sharing or bookmarking content from resources that
   are expected to have changing representations over time.<a href="#section-6.4.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.4.2-2">
   For a request message:<a href="#section-6.4.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-6.4.2-3.1">If the request has a <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a> header field,
       then the sender asserts that the content is a representation of the
       resource identified by the Content-Location field value. However,
       such an assertion cannot be trusted unless it can be verified by
       other means (not defined by this specification). The information
       might still be useful for revision history links.<a href="#section-6.4.2-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-6.4.2-3.2">Otherwise, the content is unidentified by HTTP, but a more specific
       identifier might be supplied within the content itself.<a href="#section-6.4.2-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-6.4.2-4">
   For a response message, the following rules are applied in order until a
   match is found:<a href="#section-6.4.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-6.4.2-5">
                  <li id="section-6.4.2-5.1">If the request method is HEAD or the response status code is
       <a href="#status.204" class="xref">204 (No Content)</a> or <a href="#status.304" class="xref">304 (Not Modified)</a>,
       there is no content in the response.<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-6.4.2-5.2">If the request method is GET and the response status code is
       <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a>,
       the content is a representation of the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>).<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-6.4.2-5.3">If the request method is GET and the response status code is
       <a href="#status.203" class="xref">203 (Non-Authoritative Information)</a>, the content is
       a potentially modified or enhanced representation of the
       <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> as provided by an intermediary.<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-6.4.2-5.4">If the request method is GET and the response status code is
       <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a>,
       the content is one or more parts of a representation of the
       target resource.<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-6.4.2-5.5">If the response has a <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a> header field
       and its field value is a reference to the same URI as the target URI,
       the content is a representation of the target resource.<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-6.4.2-5.6">If the response has a <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a> header field
       and its field value is a reference to a URI different from the
       target URI, then the sender asserts that the content is a
       representation of the resource identified by the Content-Location
       field value. However, such an assertion cannot be trusted unless
       it can be verified by other means (not defined by this specification).<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-6.4.2-5.7">Otherwise, the content is unidentified by HTTP, but a more specific
       identifier might be supplied within the content itself.<a href="#section-6.4.2-5.7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ol>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="trailer.fields">
<section id="section-6.5">
        <h3 id="name-trailer-fields">
<a href="#section-6.5" class="section-number selfRef">6.5. </a><a href="#name-trailer-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Trailer Fields</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-trailer-section-119" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-trailer-fields-120" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-trailers-121" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-6.5-1">
   Fields (<a href="#fields" class="xref">Section 5</a>) that are located within a
   "trailer section" are referred to as "trailer fields"
   (or just "trailers", colloquially).
   Trailer fields can be useful for supplying message integrity checks, digital
   signatures, delivery metrics, or post-processing status information.<a href="#section-6.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5-2">
   Trailer fields ought to be processed and stored separately from the fields
   in the header section to avoid contradicting message semantics known at
   the time the header section was complete. The presence or absence of
   certain header fields might impact choices made for the routing or
   processing of the message as a whole before the trailers are received;
   those choices cannot be unmade by the later discovery of trailer fields.<a href="#section-6.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="trailers.limitations">
<section id="section-6.5.1">
          <h4 id="name-limitations-on-use-of-trail">
<a href="#section-6.5.1" class="section-number selfRef">6.5.1. </a><a href="#name-limitations-on-use-of-trail" class="section-name selfRef">Limitations on Use of Trailers</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-6.5.1-1">
   A trailer section is only possible when supported by the version
   of HTTP in use and enabled by an explicit framing mechanism.
   For example, the chunked transfer coding in HTTP/1.1 allows a trailer section to be
   sent after the content (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-7.1.2" class="relref">Section 7.1.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-6.5.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5.1-2">
   Many fields cannot be processed outside the header section because
   their evaluation is necessary prior to receiving the content, such as
   those that describe message framing, routing, authentication,
   request modifiers, response controls, or content format.
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a trailer field unless the sender knows the
   corresponding header field name's definition permits the field to be sent
   in trailers.<a href="#section-6.5.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5.1-3">
   Trailer fields can be difficult to process by intermediaries that forward
   messages from one protocol version to another. If the entire message can be
   buffered in transit, some intermediaries could merge trailer fields into
   the header section (as appropriate) before it is forwarded. However, in
   most cases, the trailers are simply discarded.
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> merge a trailer field into a header section unless
   the recipient understands the corresponding header field definition and
   that definition explicitly permits and defines how trailer field values
   can be safely merged.<a href="#section-6.5.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5.1-4">
   The presence of the keyword "trailers" in the TE header field (<a href="#field.te" class="xref">Section 10.1.4</a>) of a request indicates that the client is willing to
   accept trailer fields, on behalf of itself and any downstream clients. For
   requests from an intermediary, this implies that all
   downstream clients are willing to accept trailer fields in the forwarded
   response. Note that the presence of "trailers" does not mean that the
   client(s) will process any particular trailer field in the response; only
   that the trailer section(s) will not be dropped by any of the clients.<a href="#section-6.5.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5.1-5">
   Because of the potential for trailer fields to be discarded in transit, a
   server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate trailer fields that it believes are necessary
   for the user agent to receive.<a href="#section-6.5.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="trailers.processing">
<section id="section-6.5.2">
          <h4 id="name-processing-trailer-fields">
<a href="#section-6.5.2" class="section-number selfRef">6.5.2. </a><a href="#name-processing-trailer-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Processing Trailer Fields</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-6.5.2-1">
   The "Trailer" header field (<a href="#field.trailer" class="xref">Section 6.6.2</a>) can be sent
   to indicate fields likely to be sent in the trailer section, which allows
   recipients to prepare for their receipt before processing the content.
   For example, this could be useful if a field name indicates that a dynamic
   checksum should be calculated as the content is received and then
   immediately checked upon receipt of the trailer field value.<a href="#section-6.5.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5.2-2">
   Like header fields, trailer fields with the same name are processed in the
   order received; multiple trailer field lines with the same name have the
   equivalent semantics as appending the multiple values as a list of members.
   Trailer fields that might be generated more than once during a message
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> be defined as a list-based field even if each member value is only
   processed once per field line received.<a href="#section-6.5.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.5.2-3">
   At the end of a message, a recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> treat the set of received
   trailer fields as a data structure of name/value pairs, similar to (but
   separate from) the header fields. Additional processing expectations, if
   any, can be defined within the field specification for a field intended
   for use in trailers.<a href="#section-6.5.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="message.metadata">
<section id="section-6.6">
        <h3 id="name-message-metadata">
<a href="#section-6.6" class="section-number selfRef">6.6. </a><a href="#name-message-metadata" class="section-name selfRef">Message Metadata</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-6.6-1">
   Fields that describe the message itself, such as when and how the
   message has been generated, can appear in both requests and responses.<a href="#section-6.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="field.date">
<section id="section-6.6.1">
          <h4 id="name-date">
<a href="#section-6.6.1" class="section-number selfRef">6.6.1. </a><a href="#name-date" class="section-name selfRef">Date</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-date-122" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-date-123" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-date-header-field-124" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-6.6.1-1">
   The "Date" header field represents the date and time at which
   the message was originated, having the same semantics as the Origination
   Date Field (orig-date) defined in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322#section-3.6.1" class="relref">Section 3.6.1</a> of [<a href="#RFC5322" class="xref">RFC5322</a>]</span>.
   The field value is an HTTP-date, as defined in <a href="#http.date" class="xref">Section 5.6.7</a>.<a href="#section-6.6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-date-125" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-6.6.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Date = HTTP-date
</pre><a href="#section-6.6.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-6.6.1-3">
   An example is<a href="#section-6.6.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-6.6.1-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Date: Tue, 15 Nov 1994 08:12:31 GMT
</pre><a href="#section-6.6.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-6.6.1-5">
   A sender that generates a Date header field <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate its
   field value as the best available approximation of the date and time of
   message generation. In theory, the date ought to represent the moment just
   before generating the message content. In practice, a sender can generate
   the date value at any time during message origination.<a href="#section-6.6.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.1-6">
   An origin server with a clock (as defined in
   <a href="#http.date" class="xref">Section 5.6.7</a>) <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate a Date header field in
   all <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a>, <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a>,
   and <a href="#status.4xx" class="xref">4xx (Client Error)</a> responses,
   and <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> generate a Date header field in
   <a href="#status.1xx" class="xref">1xx (Informational)</a> and
   <a href="#status.5xx" class="xref">5xx (Server Error)</a> responses.<a href="#section-6.6.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.1-7">
   An origin server without a clock <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a Date header field.<a href="#section-6.6.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.1-8">
   A recipient with a clock that receives a response message without a Date
   header field <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> record the time it was received and append a
   corresponding Date header field to the message's header section if it is
   cached or forwarded downstream.<a href="#section-6.6.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.1-9">
   A recipient with a clock that receives a response with an invalid Date
   header field value <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> replace that value with the time that
   response was received.<a href="#section-6.6.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.1-10">
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a Date header field in a request, though generally
   will not do so unless it is believed to convey useful information to the
   server. For example, custom applications of HTTP might convey a Date if
   the server is expected to adjust its interpretation of the user's request
   based on differences between the user agent and server clocks.<a href="#section-6.6.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.trailer">
<section id="section-6.6.2">
          <h4 id="name-trailer">
<a href="#section-6.6.2" class="section-number selfRef">6.6.2. </a><a href="#name-trailer" class="section-name selfRef">Trailer</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-trailer-126" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-trailer-127" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-trailer-header-field-128" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-6.6.2-1">
   The "Trailer" header field provides a list of field names that the sender
   anticipates sending as trailer fields within that message. This allows a
   recipient to prepare for receipt of the indicated metadata before it starts
   processing the content.<a href="#section-6.6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-trailer-129" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-field-name-130" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-6.6.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Trailer = #field-name
</pre><a href="#section-6.6.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-6.6.2-3">
   For example, a sender might indicate that a signature will
   be computed as the content is being streamed and provide the final
   signature as a trailer field. This allows a recipient to perform the same
   check on the fly as it receives the content.<a href="#section-6.6.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.2-4">
   A sender that intends to generate one or more trailer fields in a message
   <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a <a href="#field.trailer" class="xref">Trailer</a> header field in the header
   section of that message to indicate which fields might be present in the
   trailers.<a href="#section-6.6.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-6.6.2-5">
   If an intermediary discards the trailer section in transit, the
   <a href="#field.trailer" class="xref">Trailer</a> field could provide a hint of what metadata
   was lost, though there is no guarantee that a sender of Trailer
   will always follow through by sending the named fields.<a href="#section-6.6.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="routing">
<section id="section-7">
      <h2 id="name-routing-http-messages">
<a href="#section-7" class="section-number selfRef">7. </a><a href="#name-routing-http-messages" class="section-name selfRef">Routing HTTP Messages</a>
      </h2>
<p id="section-7-1">
   HTTP request message routing is determined by each client based on the
   target resource, the client's proxy configuration, and
   establishment or reuse of an inbound connection.  The corresponding
   response routing follows the same connection chain back to the client.<a href="#section-7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="target.resource">
<section id="section-7.1">
        <h3 id="name-determining-the-target-reso">
<a href="#section-7.1" class="section-number selfRef">7.1. </a><a href="#name-determining-the-target-reso" class="section-name selfRef">Determining the Target Resource</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-target-resource-131" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-target-uri-132" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-request-target-133" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-7.1-1">
   Although HTTP is used in a wide variety of applications, most clients rely
   on the same resource identification mechanism and configuration techniques
   as general-purpose Web browsers. Even when communication options are
   hard-coded in a client's configuration, we can think of their combined
   effect as a URI reference (<a href="#uri.references" class="xref">Section 4.1</a>).<a href="#section-7.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.1-2">
   A URI reference is resolved to its absolute form in order to obtain the
   "target URI". The target URI excludes the reference's
   fragment component, if any, since fragment identifiers are reserved for
   client-side processing (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.5" class="relref">Section 3.5</a></span>).<a href="#section-7.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.1-3">
   To perform an action on a "target resource", the client sends
   a request message containing enough components of its parsed target URI to
   enable recipients to identify that same resource. For historical reasons,
   the parsed target URI components, collectively referred to as the
   "request target", are sent within the message control data
   and the <a href="#field.host" class="xref">Host</a> header field (<a href="#field.host" class="xref">Section 7.2</a>).<a href="#section-7.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.1-4">
   There are two unusual cases for which the request target components are in
   a method-specific form:<a href="#section-7.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-7.1-5.1">
      For CONNECT (<a href="#CONNECT" class="xref">Section 9.3.6</a>), the request target is the host
      name and port number of the tunnel destination, separated by a colon.<a href="#section-7.1-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li class="normal" id="section-7.1-5.2">
      For OPTIONS (<a href="#OPTIONS" class="xref">Section 9.3.7</a>), the request target can be a
      single asterisk ("*").<a href="#section-7.1-5.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        </ul>
<p id="section-7.1-6">
   See the respective method definitions for details. These forms <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span>
   be used with other methods.<a href="#section-7.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.1-7">
   Upon receipt of a client's request, a server reconstructs the target URI
   from the received components in accordance with their local configuration
   and incoming connection context. This reconstruction is specific to each
   major protocol version. For example,
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-3.3" class="relref">Section 3.3</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span> defines how a server
   determines the target URI of an HTTP/1.1 request.<a href="#section-7.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="effective.request.uri">
<aside id="section-7.1-8">
          <p id="section-7.1-8.1">
                  <span id="iref-effective-request-uri-134" class="iref"></span>
            <strong>Note:</strong> Previous specifications defined the recomposed target URI as a
    distinct concept, the "effective request URI".<a href="#section-7.1-8.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.host">
<section id="section-7.2">
        <h3 id="name-host-and-authority">
<a href="#section-7.2" class="section-number selfRef">7.2. </a><a href="#name-host-and-authority" class="section-name selfRef">Host and :authority</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-host-135" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-host-136" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-host-header-field-137" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-7.2-1">
   The "Host" header field in a request provides the host and port
   information from the target URI, enabling the origin
   server to distinguish among resources while servicing requests
   for multiple host names.<a href="#section-7.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.2-2">
   In HTTP/2 <span>[<a href="#HTTP2" class="xref">HTTP/2</a>]</span> and HTTP/3 <span>[<a href="#HTTP3" class="xref">HTTP/3</a>]</span>, the
   Host header field is, in some cases, supplanted by the ":authority"
   pseudo-header field of a request's control data.<a href="#section-7.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-host-138" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-7.2-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Host = uri-host [ ":" port ] ; Section 4
</pre><a href="#section-7.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.2-4">
   The target URI's authority information is critical for handling a
   request. A user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate a Host header field in a request
   unless it sends that information as an ":authority" pseudo-header field.
   A user agent that sends Host <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send it as the first field in the
   header section of a request.<a href="#section-7.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.2-5">
   For example, a GET request to the origin server for
   <http://www.example.org/pub/WWW/> would begin with:<a href="#section-7.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.2-6">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">GET /pub/WWW/ HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.org
</pre><a href="#section-7.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.2-7">
   Since the host and port information acts as an application-level routing
   mechanism, it is a frequent target for malware seeking to poison
   a shared cache or redirect a request to an unintended server.
   An interception proxy is particularly vulnerable if it relies on
   the host and port information for redirecting requests to internal
   servers, or for use as a cache key in a shared cache, without
   first verifying that the intercepted connection is targeting a
   valid IP address for that host.<a href="#section-7.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="routing.inbound">
<section id="section-7.3">
        <h3 id="name-routing-inbound-requests">
<a href="#section-7.3" class="section-number selfRef">7.3. </a><a href="#name-routing-inbound-requests" class="section-name selfRef">Routing Inbound Requests</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-7.3-1">
   Once the target URI and its origin are determined, a client decides whether
   a network request is necessary to accomplish the desired semantics and,
   if so, where that request is to be directed.<a href="#section-7.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="routing.cache">
<section id="section-7.3.1">
          <h4 id="name-to-a-cache">
<a href="#section-7.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">7.3.1. </a><a href="#name-to-a-cache" class="section-name selfRef">To a Cache</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-7.3.1-1">
   If the client has a cache <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span> and the request can be
   satisfied by it, then the request is
   usually directed there first.<a href="#section-7.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="routing.proxy">
<section id="section-7.3.2">
          <h4 id="name-to-a-proxy">
<a href="#section-7.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">7.3.2. </a><a href="#name-to-a-proxy" class="section-name selfRef">To a Proxy</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-7.3.2-1">
   If the request is not satisfied by a cache, then a typical client will
   check its configuration to determine whether a proxy is to be used to
   satisfy the request.  Proxy configuration is implementation-dependent,
   but is often based on URI prefix matching, selective authority matching,
   or both, and the proxy itself is usually identified by an "http" or
   "https" URI.<a href="#section-7.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.3.2-2">
   If an "http" or "https" proxy is applicable, the client connects
   inbound by establishing (or reusing) a connection to that proxy and
   then sending it an HTTP request message containing a request target
   that matches the client's target URI.<a href="#section-7.3.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="routing.origin">
<section id="section-7.3.3">
          <h4 id="name-to-the-origin">
<a href="#section-7.3.3" class="section-number selfRef">7.3.3. </a><a href="#name-to-the-origin" class="section-name selfRef">To the Origin</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-7.3.3-1">
   If no proxy is applicable, a typical client will invoke a handler
   routine (specific to the target URI's scheme) to obtain access to the
   identified resource. How that is accomplished is dependent on the
   target URI scheme and defined by its associated specification.<a href="#section-7.3.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.3.3-2">
                  <a href="#http.origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.2</a> defines how to obtain access to an
   "http" resource by establishing (or reusing) an inbound connection to
   the identified origin server and then sending it an HTTP request message
   containing a request target that matches the client's target URI.<a href="#section-7.3.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.3.3-3">
                  <a href="#https.origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.3</a> defines how to obtain access to an
   "https" resource by establishing (or reusing) an inbound secured
   connection to an origin server that is authoritative for the identified
   origin and then sending it an HTTP request message containing a request
   target that matches the client's target URI.<a href="#section-7.3.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="routing.reject">
<section id="section-7.4">
        <h3 id="name-rejecting-misdirected-reque">
<a href="#section-7.4" class="section-number selfRef">7.4. </a><a href="#name-rejecting-misdirected-reque" class="section-name selfRef">Rejecting Misdirected Requests</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-7.4-1">
   Once a request is received by a server and parsed sufficiently to determine
   its target URI, the server decides whether to process the request itself,
   forward the request to another server, redirect the client to a different
   resource, respond with an error, or drop the connection. This decision can
   be influenced by anything about the request or connection context, but is
   specifically directed at whether the server has been configured to process
   requests for that target URI and whether the connection context is
   appropriate for that request.<a href="#section-7.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.4-2">
   For example, a request might have been misdirected,
   deliberately or accidentally, such that the information within a received
   <a href="#field.host" class="xref">Host</a> header field differs from the connection's host or port.
   If the connection is from a trusted gateway, such inconsistency might
   be expected; otherwise, it might indicate an attempt to bypass security
   filters, trick the server into delivering non-public content, or poison a
   cache. See <a href="#security.considerations" class="xref">Section 17</a> for security
   considerations regarding message routing.<a href="#section-7.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.4-3">
   Unless the connection is from a trusted gateway,
   an origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> reject a request if any scheme-specific requirements
   for the target URI are not met. In particular,
   a request for an "https" resource <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> be rejected unless it has been
   received over a connection that has been secured via a certificate
   valid for that target URI's origin, as defined by <a href="#https.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.2</a>.<a href="#section-7.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.4-4">
   The <a href="#status.421" class="xref">421 (Misdirected Request)</a> status code in a response
   indicates that the origin server has rejected the request because it
   appears to have been misdirected (<a href="#status.421" class="xref">Section 15.5.20</a>).<a href="#section-7.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="response.correlation">
<section id="section-7.5">
        <h3 id="name-response-correlation">
<a href="#section-7.5" class="section-number selfRef">7.5. </a><a href="#name-response-correlation" class="section-name selfRef">Response Correlation</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-7.5-1">
   A connection might be used for multiple request/response exchanges. The
   mechanism used to correlate between request and response messages is
   version dependent; some versions of HTTP use implicit ordering of
   messages, while others use an explicit identifier.<a href="#section-7.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.5-2">
   All responses, regardless of the status code (including <a href="#final.interim" class="xref">interim</a>
   responses) can be sent at any time after a request is received, even if the
   request is not yet complete. A response can complete before its
   corresponding request is complete (<a href="#message.framing" class="xref">Section 6.1</a>). Likewise, clients are not expected
   to wait any specific amount of time for a response. Clients
   (including intermediaries) might abandon a request if the response is not
   received within a reasonable period of time.<a href="#section-7.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.5-3">
   A client that receives a response while it is still sending the associated
   request <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> continue sending that request unless it receives
   an explicit indication to the contrary (see, e.g., <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-9.5" class="relref">Section 9.5</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span> and <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9113#section-6.4" class="relref">Section 6.4</a> of [<a href="#HTTP2" class="xref">HTTP/2</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-7.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="message.forwarding">
<section id="section-7.6">
        <h3 id="name-message-forwarding">
<a href="#section-7.6" class="section-number selfRef">7.6. </a><a href="#name-message-forwarding" class="section-name selfRef">Message Forwarding</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-7.6-1">
   As described in <a href="#intermediaries" class="xref">Section 3.7</a>, intermediaries can serve
   a variety of roles in the processing of HTTP requests and responses.
   Some intermediaries are used to improve performance or availability.
   Others are used for access control or to filter content.
   Since an HTTP stream has characteristics similar to a pipe-and-filter
   architecture, there are no inherent limits to the extent an intermediary
   can enhance (or interfere) with either direction of the stream.<a href="#section-7.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6-2">
   Intermediaries are expected to forward messages even when protocol elements
   are not recognized (e.g., new methods, status codes, or field names) since that
   preserves extensibility for downstream recipients.<a href="#section-7.6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6-3">
   An intermediary not acting as a tunnel <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> implement the
   <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Connection</a> header field, as specified in
   <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Section 7.6.1</a>, and exclude fields from being forwarded
   that are only intended for the incoming connection.<a href="#section-7.6-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6-4">
   An intermediary <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> forward a message to itself unless it is
   protected from an infinite request loop. In general, an intermediary ought
   to recognize its own server names, including any aliases, local variations,
   or literal IP addresses, and respond to such requests directly.<a href="#section-7.6-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6-5">
   An HTTP message can be parsed as a stream for incremental processing or
   forwarding downstream.
   However, senders and recipients cannot rely on incremental
   delivery of partial messages, since some implementations will buffer or
   delay message forwarding for the sake of network efficiency, security
   checks, or content transformations.<a href="#section-7.6-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="field.connection">
<section id="section-7.6.1">
          <h4 id="name-connection">
<a href="#section-7.6.1" class="section-number selfRef">7.6.1. </a><a href="#name-connection" class="section-name selfRef">Connection</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-connection-139" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-connection-14" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-connection-header-field-141" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-connection-142" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-connection-option-1" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-7.6.1-1">
   The "Connection" header field allows the sender to list desired
   control options for the current connection.<a href="#section-7.6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.6.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Connection        = #connection-option
  connection-option = token
</pre><a href="#section-7.6.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.6.1-3">
   Connection options are case-insensitive.<a href="#section-7.6.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.1-4">
   When a field aside from Connection is used to supply control
   information for or about the current connection, the sender <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> list
   the corresponding field name within the Connection header field.
   Note that some versions of HTTP prohibit the use of fields for such
   information, and therefore do not allow the Connection field.<a href="#section-7.6.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.1-5">
   Intermediaries <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> parse a received Connection
   header field before a message is forwarded and, for each
   connection-option in this field, remove any header or trailer field(s) from
   the message with the same name as the connection-option, and then
   remove the Connection header field itself (or replace it with the
   intermediary's own control options for the forwarded message).<a href="#section-7.6.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.1-6">
   Hence, the Connection header field provides a declarative way of
   distinguishing fields that are only intended for the
   immediate recipient ("hop-by-hop") from those fields that are
   intended for all recipients on the chain ("end-to-end"), enabling the
   message to be self-descriptive and allowing future connection-specific
   extensions to be deployed without fear that they will be blindly
   forwarded by older intermediaries.<a href="#section-7.6.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.1-7">
   Furthermore, intermediaries <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> remove or replace fields
   that are known to require removal before forwarding, whether or not they appear as a
   connection-option, after applying those fields' semantics. This includes but is not limited to:<a href="#section-7.6.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-7.6.1-8.1">Proxy-Connection (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#appendix-C.2.2" class="relref">Appendix C.2.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>)<a href="#section-7.6.1-8.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-7.6.1-8.2">Keep-Alive (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2068#section-19.7.1" class="relref">Section 19.7.1</a> of [<a href="#RFC2068" class="xref">RFC2068</a>]</span>)<a href="#section-7.6.1-8.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-7.6.1-8.3">TE (<a href="#field.te" class="xref">Section 10.1.4</a>)<a href="#section-7.6.1-8.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-7.6.1-8.4">Transfer-Encoding (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-6.1" class="relref">Section 6.1</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>)<a href="#section-7.6.1-8.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-7.6.1-8.5">Upgrade (<a href="#field.upgrade" class="xref">Section 7.8</a>)<a href="#section-7.6.1-8.5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-7.6.1-9">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a connection option corresponding to a
   field that is intended for all recipients of the content.
   For example, Cache-Control is never appropriate as a
   connection option (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2" class="relref">Section 5.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-7.6.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.1-10">
   Connection options do not always correspond to a field
   present in the message, since a connection-specific field
   might not be needed if there are no parameters associated with a
   connection option. In contrast, a connection-specific field
   received without a corresponding connection option usually indicates
   that the field has been improperly forwarded by an intermediary and
   ought to be ignored by the recipient.<a href="#section-7.6.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.1-11">
   When defining a new connection option that does not correspond to a field,
   specification authors ought to reserve the corresponding field name
   anyway in order to avoid later collisions. Such reserved field names are
   registered in the "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Field Name Registry"
   (<a href="#fields.registry" class="xref">Section 16.3.1</a>).<a href="#section-7.6.1-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.max-forwards">
<section id="section-7.6.2">
          <h4 id="name-max-forwards">
<a href="#section-7.6.2" class="section-number selfRef">7.6.2. </a><a href="#name-max-forwards" class="section-name selfRef">Max-Forwards</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-max-forwards-144" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-max-forwards-" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-max-forwards-header-field-1" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-7.6.2-1">
   The "Max-Forwards" header field provides a mechanism with the
   TRACE (<a href="#TRACE" class="xref">Section 9.3.8</a>) and OPTIONS (<a href="#OPTIONS" class="xref">Section 9.3.7</a>)
   request methods to limit the number of times that the request is forwarded by
   proxies. This can be useful when the client is attempting to
   trace a request that appears to be failing or looping mid-chain.<a href="#section-7.6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-max-forwards-147" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-7.6.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Max-Forwards = 1*DIGIT
</pre><a href="#section-7.6.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.6.2-3">
   The Max-Forwards value is a decimal integer indicating the remaining
   number of times this request message can be forwarded.<a href="#section-7.6.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.2-4">
   Each intermediary that receives a TRACE or OPTIONS request containing a
   Max-Forwards header field <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> check and update its value prior to
   forwarding the request. If the received value is zero (0), the intermediary
   <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> forward the request; instead, the intermediary <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> respond as
   the final recipient. If the received Max-Forwards value is greater than
   zero, the intermediary <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate an updated Max-Forwards field in the
   forwarded message with a field value that is the lesser of a) the received
   value decremented by one (1) or b) the recipient's maximum supported value
   for Max-Forwards.<a href="#section-7.6.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.2-5">
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> ignore a Max-Forwards header field received with any
   other request methods.<a href="#section-7.6.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.via">
<section id="section-7.6.3">
          <h4 id="name-via">
<a href="#section-7.6.3" class="section-number selfRef">7.6.3. </a><a href="#name-via" class="section-name selfRef">Via</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-via-148" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-via-149" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-via-header-field-150" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-7.6.3-1">
   The "Via" header field indicates the presence of intermediate protocols and
   recipients between the user agent and the server (on requests) or between
   the origin server and the client (on responses), similar to the
   "Received" header field in email
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322#section-3.6.7" class="relref">Section 3.6.7</a> of [<a href="#RFC5322" class="xref">RFC5322</a>]</span>).
   Via can be used for tracking message forwards,
   avoiding request loops, and identifying the protocol capabilities of
   senders along the request/response chain.<a href="#section-7.6.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-via-151" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-received-protocol-1" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-protocol-name-153" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-protocol-version-15" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-received-by-155" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-pseudonym-156" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-7.6.3-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Via = #( received-protocol RWS received-by [ RWS comment ] )

  received-protocol = [ protocol-name "/" ] protocol-version
                    ; see Section 7.8
  received-by       = pseudonym [ ":" port ]
  pseudonym         = token
</pre><a href="#section-7.6.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.6.3-3">
   Each member of the Via field value represents a proxy or gateway that has
   forwarded the message. Each intermediary appends its own information
   about how the message was received, such that the end result is ordered
   according to the sequence of forwarding recipients.<a href="#section-7.6.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.3-4">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send an appropriate Via header field, as described below, in
   each message that it forwards.
   An HTTP-to-HTTP gateway <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send an appropriate Via header field in
   each inbound request message and <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a Via header field in
   forwarded response messages.<a href="#section-7.6.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.3-5">
   For each intermediary, the received-protocol indicates the protocol and
   protocol version used by the upstream sender of the message. Hence, the
   Via field value records the advertised protocol capabilities of the
   request/response chain such that they remain visible to downstream
   recipients; this can be useful for determining what backwards-incompatible
   features might be safe to use in response, or within a later request, as
   described in <a href="#protocol.version" class="xref">Section 2.5</a>. For brevity, the protocol-name
   is omitted when the received protocol is HTTP.<a href="#section-7.6.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.3-6">
   The received-by portion is normally the host and optional
   port number of a recipient server or client that subsequently forwarded the
   message.
   However, if the real host is considered to be sensitive information, a
   sender <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> replace it with a pseudonym. If a port is not provided,
   a recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> interpret that as meaning it was received on the default
   port, if any, for the received-protocol.<a href="#section-7.6.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.3-7">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> generate comments to identify the
   software of each recipient, analogous to the <a href="#field.user-agent" class="xref">User-Agent</a> and
   <a href="#field.server" class="xref">Server</a> header fields. However, comments in Via
   are optional, and a recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> remove them prior to forwarding the
   message.<a href="#section-7.6.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.3-8">
   For example, a request message could be sent from an HTTP/1.0 user
   agent to an internal proxy code-named "fred", which uses HTTP/1.1 to
   forward the request to a public proxy at p.example.net, which completes
   the request by forwarding it to the origin server at www.example.com.
   The request received by www.example.com would then have the following
   Via header field:<a href="#section-7.6.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.6.3-9">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Via: 1.0 fred, 1.1 p.example.net
</pre><a href="#section-7.6.3-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.6.3-10">
   An intermediary used as a portal through a network firewall
   <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> forward the names and ports of hosts within the firewall
   region unless it is explicitly enabled to do so. If not enabled, such an
   intermediary <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> replace each received-by host of any host behind the
   firewall by an appropriate pseudonym for that host.<a href="#section-7.6.3-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.6.3-11">
   An intermediary <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> combine an ordered subsequence of Via header
   field list members into a single member if the entries have identical
   received-protocol values. For example,<a href="#section-7.6.3-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.6.3-12">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Via: 1.0 ricky, 1.1 ethel, 1.1 fred, 1.0 lucy
</pre><a href="#section-7.6.3-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.6.3-13">
  could be collapsed to<a href="#section-7.6.3-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.6.3-14">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Via: 1.0 ricky, 1.1 mertz, 1.0 lucy
</pre><a href="#section-7.6.3-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.6.3-15">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> combine multiple list members unless they are all
   under the same organizational control and the hosts have already been
   replaced by pseudonyms. A sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> combine members that
   have different received-protocol values.<a href="#section-7.6.3-15" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="message.transformations">
<section id="section-7.7">
        <h3 id="name-message-transformations">
<a href="#section-7.7" class="section-number selfRef">7.7. </a><a href="#name-message-transformations" class="section-name selfRef">Message Transformations</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-transforming-proxy-157" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-non-transforming-proxy-158" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-7.7-1">
   Some intermediaries include features for transforming messages and their
   content. A proxy might, for example, convert between image formats in
   order to save cache space or to reduce the amount of traffic on a slow
   link. However, operational problems might occur when these transformations
   are applied to content intended for critical applications, such as medical
   imaging or scientific data analysis, particularly when integrity checks or
   digital signatures are used to ensure that the content received is
   identical to the original.<a href="#section-7.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.7-2">
   An HTTP-to-HTTP proxy is called a "transforming proxy"
   if it is designed or configured to modify messages in a semantically
   meaningful way (i.e., modifications, beyond those required by normal
   HTTP processing, that change the message in a way that would be
   significant to the original sender or potentially significant to
   downstream recipients).  For example, a transforming proxy might be
   acting as a shared annotation server (modifying responses to include
   references to a local annotation database), a malware filter, a
   format transcoder, or a privacy filter. Such transformations are presumed
   to be desired by whichever client (or client organization) chose the
   proxy.<a href="#section-7.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.7-3">
   If a proxy receives a target URI with a host name that is not a
   fully qualified domain name, it <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> add its own domain to the host name
   it received when forwarding the request.  A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> change the
   host name if the target URI contains a fully qualified domain name.<a href="#section-7.7-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.7-4">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> modify the "absolute-path" and "query" parts of the
   received target URI when forwarding it to the next inbound server except
   as required by that forwarding protocol. For example, a proxy forwarding
   a request to an origin server via HTTP/1.1 will replace an empty path with
   "/" (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-3.2.1" class="relref">Section 3.2.1</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) or "*" (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-3.2.4" class="relref">Section 3.2.4</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>),
   depending on the request method.<a href="#section-7.7-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.7-5">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> transform the content (<a href="#content" class="xref">Section 6.4</a>) of a
   response message that contains a no-transform cache directive
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2.2.6" class="relref">Section 5.2.2.6</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>). Note that this
   does not apply to message transformations that do not change the content,
   such as the addition or removal of transfer codings
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-7" class="relref">Section 7</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-7.7-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.7-6">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> transform the content of a message
   that does not contain a no-transform cache directive.
   A proxy that transforms the content of a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response
   can inform downstream recipients that a transformation has been
   applied by changing the response status code to
   <a href="#status.203" class="xref">203 (Non-Authoritative Information)</a> (<a href="#status.203" class="xref">Section 15.3.4</a>).<a href="#section-7.7-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.7-7">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> modify header fields that provide information about
   the endpoints of the communication chain, the resource state, or the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> (other than the content) unless the field's
   definition specifically allows such modification or the modification is
   deemed necessary for privacy or security.<a href="#section-7.7-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.upgrade">
<section id="section-7.8">
        <h3 id="name-upgrade">
<a href="#section-7.8" class="section-number selfRef">7.8. </a><a href="#name-upgrade" class="section-name selfRef">Upgrade</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-upgrade-159" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-upgrade-160" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-upgrade-header-field-161" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-7.8-1">
   The "Upgrade" header field is intended to provide a simple mechanism
   for transitioning from HTTP/1.1 to some other protocol on the same
   connection.<a href="#section-7.8-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-2">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a list of protocol names in the Upgrade header field
   of a request to invite the server to switch to one or more of the named
   protocols, in order of descending preference, before sending
   the final response. A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> ignore a received Upgrade header field
   if it wishes to continue using the current protocol on that connection.
   Upgrade cannot be used to insist on a protocol change.<a href="#section-7.8-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-upgrade-162" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-7.8-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Upgrade          = #protocol

  protocol         = protocol-name ["/" protocol-version]
  protocol-name    = token
  protocol-version = token
</pre><a href="#section-7.8-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.8-4">
   Although protocol names are registered with a preferred case,
   recipients <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> use case-insensitive comparison when matching each
   protocol-name to supported protocols.<a href="#section-7.8-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-5">
   A server that sends a <a href="#status.101" class="xref">101 (Switching Protocols)</a> response
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send an Upgrade header field to indicate the new protocol(s) to
   which the connection is being switched; if multiple protocol layers are
   being switched, the sender <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> list the protocols in layer-ascending
   order. A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> switch to a protocol that was not indicated by
   the client in the corresponding request's Upgrade header field.
   A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> choose to ignore the order of preference indicated by the
   client and select the new protocol(s) based on other factors, such as the
   nature of the request or the current load on the server.<a href="#section-7.8-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-6">
   A server that sends a <a href="#status.426" class="xref">426 (Upgrade Required)</a> response
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send an Upgrade header field to indicate the acceptable protocols,
   in order of descending preference.<a href="#section-7.8-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-7">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send an Upgrade header field in any other response to
   advertise that it implements support for upgrading to the listed protocols,
   in order of descending preference, when appropriate for a future request.<a href="#section-7.8-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-8">
   The following is a hypothetical example sent by a client:<a href="#section-7.8-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.8-9">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">GET /hello HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
Connection: upgrade
Upgrade: websocket, IRC/6.9, RTA/x11

</pre><a href="#section-7.8-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.8-10">
   The capabilities and nature of the
   application-level communication after the protocol change is entirely
   dependent upon the new protocol(s) chosen. However, immediately after
   sending the <a href="#status.101" class="xref">101 (Switching Protocols)</a> response, the server is expected to continue responding to
   the original request as if it had received its equivalent within the new
   protocol (i.e., the server still has an outstanding request to satisfy
   after the protocol has been changed, and is expected to do so without
   requiring the request to be repeated).<a href="#section-7.8-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-11">
   For example, if the Upgrade header field is received in a GET request
   and the server decides to switch protocols, it first responds
   with a <a href="#status.101" class="xref">101 (Switching Protocols)</a> message in HTTP/1.1 and
   then immediately follows that with the new protocol's equivalent of a
   response to a GET on the target resource.  This allows a connection to be
   upgraded to protocols with the same semantics as HTTP without the
   latency cost of an additional round trip.  A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> switch
   protocols unless the received message semantics can be honored by the new
   protocol; an OPTIONS request can be honored by any protocol.<a href="#section-7.8-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-12">
   The following is an example response to the above hypothetical request:<a href="#section-7.8-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-7.8-13">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Connection: upgrade
Upgrade: websocket

[... data stream switches to websocket with an appropriate response
(as defined by new protocol) to the "GET /hello" request ...]
</pre><a href="#section-7.8-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-7.8-14">
   A sender of Upgrade <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> also send an "Upgrade" connection option in the
   <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Connection</a> header field (<a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Section 7.6.1</a>)
   to inform intermediaries not to forward this field.
   A server that receives an Upgrade header field in an HTTP/1.0 request
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> ignore that Upgrade field.<a href="#section-7.8-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-15">
   A client cannot begin using an upgraded protocol on the connection until
   it has completely sent the request message (i.e., the client can't change
   the protocol it is sending in the middle of a message).
   If a server receives both an Upgrade and an <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Expect</a> header field
   with the "100-continue" expectation (<a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Section 10.1.1</a>), the
   server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send a <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response before sending
   a <a href="#status.101" class="xref">101 (Switching Protocols)</a> response.<a href="#section-7.8-15" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-16">
   The Upgrade header field only applies to switching protocols on top of the
   existing connection; it cannot be used to switch the underlying connection
   (transport) protocol, nor to switch the existing communication to a
   different connection. For those purposes, it is more appropriate to use a
   <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a> response (<a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">Section 15.4</a>).<a href="#section-7.8-16" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-7.8-17">
   This specification only defines the protocol name "HTTP" for use by
   the family of Hypertext Transfer Protocols, as defined by the HTTP
   version rules of <a href="#protocol.version" class="xref">Section 2.5</a> and future updates to this
   specification. Additional protocol names ought to be registered using the
   registration procedure defined in <a href="#upgrade.token.registry" class="xref">Section 16.7</a>.<a href="#section-7.8-17" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="representation.data.and.metadata">
<section id="section-8">
      <h2 id="name-representation-data-and-met">
<a href="#section-8" class="section-number selfRef">8. </a><a href="#name-representation-data-and-met" class="section-name selfRef">Representation Data and Metadata</a>
      </h2>
<div id="representation.data">
<section id="section-8.1">
        <h3 id="name-representation-data">
<a href="#section-8.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.1. </a><a href="#name-representation-data" class="section-name selfRef">Representation Data</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-8.1-1">
   The representation data associated with an HTTP message is
   either provided as the content of the message or
   referred to by the message semantics and the target
   URI.  The representation data is in a format and encoding defined by
   the representation metadata header fields.<a href="#section-8.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.1-2">
   The data type of the representation data is determined via the header fields
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> and <a href="#field.content-encoding" class="xref">Content-Encoding</a>.
   These define a two-layer, ordered encoding model:<a href="#section-8.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-8.1-3">
<pre>
  representation-data := Content-Encoding( Content-Type( data ) )
</pre><a href="#section-8.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="representation.metadata">
<section id="section-8.2">
        <h3 id="name-representation-metadata">
<a href="#section-8.2" class="section-number selfRef">8.2. </a><a href="#name-representation-metadata" class="section-name selfRef">Representation Metadata</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-8.2-1">
   Representation header fields provide metadata about the representation.
   When a message includes content, the representation header fields
   describe how to interpret that data. In a response to a HEAD request, the
   representation header fields describe the representation data that would
   have been enclosed in the content if the same request had been a GET.<a href="#section-8.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.content-type">
<section id="section-8.3">
        <h3 id="name-content-type">
<a href="#section-8.3" class="section-number selfRef">8.3. </a><a href="#name-content-type" class="section-name selfRef">Content-Type</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-content-type-163" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-content-type-" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-content-type-header-field-1" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-8.3-1">
   The "Content-Type" header field indicates the media type of the
   associated representation: either the representation enclosed in
   the message content or the <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>, as determined by the
   message semantics.  The indicated media type defines both the data format
   and how that data is intended to be processed by a recipient, within the
   scope of the received message semantics, after any content codings
   indicated by <a href="#field.content-encoding" class="xref">Content-Encoding</a> are decoded.<a href="#section-8.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-content-type-166" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-8.3-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Content-Type = media-type
</pre><a href="#section-8.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.3-3">
   Media types are defined in <a href="#media.type" class="xref">Section 8.3.1</a>. An example of the
   field is<a href="#section-8.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.3-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Content-Type: text/html; charset=ISO-8859-4
</pre><a href="#section-8.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.3-5">
   A sender that generates a message containing content <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span>
   generate a Content-Type header field in that message unless the intended
   media type of the enclosed representation is unknown to the sender.
   If a Content-Type header field is not present, the recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> either
   assume a media type of
   "application/octet-stream" (<span>[<a href="#RFC2046" class="xref">RFC2046</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2046#section-4.5.1" class="relref">Section 4.5.1</a></span>)
   or examine the data to determine its type.<a href="#section-8.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.3-6">
   In practice, resource owners do not always properly configure their origin
   server to provide the correct Content-Type for a given representation.
   Some user agents examine the content and, in certain cases,
   override the received type (for example, see <span>[<a href="#Sniffing" class="xref">Sniffing</a>]</span>).
   This "MIME sniffing" risks drawing incorrect conclusions about the data,
   which might expose the user to additional security risks
   (e.g., "privilege escalation").
   Furthermore, distinct media types often share a common data format,
   differing only in how the data is intended to be processed, which is
   impossible to distinguish by inspecting the data alone.
   When sniffing is implemented, implementers are encouraged to provide a
   means for the user to disable it.<a href="#section-8.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.3-7">
   Although Content-Type is defined as a singleton field, it is
   sometimes incorrectly generated multiple times, resulting in a combined
   field value that appears to be a list.
   Recipients often attempt to handle this error by using the last
   syntactically valid member of the list, leading to potential
   interoperability and security issues if different implementations
   have different error handling behaviors.<a href="#section-8.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="media.type">
<section id="section-8.3.1">
          <h4 id="name-media-type">
<a href="#section-8.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.3.1. </a><a href="#name-media-type" class="section-name selfRef">Media Type</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-8.3.1-1">
   HTTP uses media types <span>[<a href="#RFC2046" class="xref">RFC2046</a>]</span> in the
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> (<a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Section 8.3</a>)
   and <a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Accept</a> (<a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Section 12.5.1</a>) header fields in
   order to provide open and extensible data typing and type negotiation.
   Media types define both a data format and various processing models:
   how to process that data in accordance with the message context.<a href="#section-8.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-media-type-167" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-type-168" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-subtype-169" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-8.3.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  media-type = type "/" subtype parameters
  type       = token
  subtype    = token
</pre><a href="#section-8.3.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.3.1-3">
   The type and subtype tokens are case-insensitive.<a href="#section-8.3.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.3.1-4">
   The type/subtype <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> be followed by semicolon-delimited parameters
   (<a href="#parameter" class="xref">Section 5.6.6</a>) in the form of name/value pairs.
   The presence or absence of a parameter might be significant to the
   processing of a media type, depending on its definition within the media
   type registry.
   Parameter values might or might not be case-sensitive, depending on the
   semantics of the parameter name.<a href="#section-8.3.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.3.1-5">
   For example, the following media types are equivalent in describing HTML
   text data encoded in the UTF-8 character encoding scheme, but the first is
   preferred for consistency (the "charset" parameter value is defined as
   being case-insensitive in <span>[<a href="#RFC2046" class="xref">RFC2046</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2046#section-4.1.2" class="relref">Section 4.1.2</a></span>):<a href="#section-8.3.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-8.3.1-6">
<pre>
  text/html;charset=utf-8
  Text/HTML;Charset="utf-8"
  text/html; charset="utf-8"
  text/html;charset=UTF-8
</pre><a href="#section-8.3.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.3.1-7">
   Media types ought to be registered with IANA according to the
   procedures defined in <span>[<a href="#BCP13" class="xref">BCP13</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-8.3.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="charset">
<section id="section-8.3.2">
          <h4 id="name-charset">
<a href="#section-8.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">8.3.2. </a><a href="#name-charset" class="section-name selfRef">Charset</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-8.3.2-1">
   HTTP uses "charset" names to indicate or negotiate the
   character encoding scheme (<span>[<a href="#RFC6365" class="xref">RFC6365</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6365#section-2" class="relref">Section 2</a></span>)
   of a textual representation. In the fields defined by this document,
   charset names appear either in parameters (<a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a>),
   or, for <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a>, in the form of a plain <a href="#rule.token.separators" class="xref">token</a>.
   In both cases, charset names are matched case-insensitively.<a href="#section-8.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.3.2-2">
   Charset names ought to be registered in the IANA "Character Sets" registry
   (<span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/character-sets">https://www.iana.org/assignments/character-sets</a>></span>)
   according to the procedures defined in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2978#section-2" class="relref">Section 2</a> of [<a href="#RFC2978" class="xref">RFC2978</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-8.3.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-8.3.2-3">
            <p id="section-8.3.2-3.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> In theory, charset names are defined by the "mime-charset" ABNF
