    rule defined in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2978#section-2.3" class="relref">Section 2.3</a> of [<a href="#RFC2978" class="xref">RFC2978</a>]</span> (as
    corrected in <span>[<a href="#Err1912" class="xref">Err1912</a>]</span>). That rule allows two characters
    that are not included in "token" ("{" and "}"), but no charset name
    registered at the time of this writing includes braces
    (see <span>[<a href="#Err5433" class="xref">Err5433</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-8.3.2-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="multipart.types">
<section id="section-8.3.3">
          <h4 id="name-multipart-types">
<a href="#section-8.3.3" class="section-number selfRef">8.3.3. </a><a href="#name-multipart-types" class="section-name selfRef">Multipart Types</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-8.3.3-1">
   MIME provides for a number of "multipart" types -- encapsulations of
   one or more representations within a single message body. All multipart
   types share a common syntax, as defined in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2046#section-5.1.1" class="relref">Section 5.1.1</a> of [<a href="#RFC2046" class="xref">RFC2046</a>]</span>,
   and include a boundary parameter as part of the media type
   value. The message body is itself a protocol element; a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
   generate only CRLF to represent line breaks between body parts.<a href="#section-8.3.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.3.3-2">
   HTTP message framing does not use the multipart boundary as an indicator
   of message body length, though it might be used by implementations that
   generate or process the content. For example, the "multipart/form-data"
   type is often used for carrying form data in a request, as described in
   <span>[<a href="#RFC7578" class="xref">RFC7578</a>]</span>, and the "multipart/byteranges" type is defined
   by this specification for use in some <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a>
   responses (see <a href="#status.206" class="xref">Section 15.3.7</a>).<a href="#section-8.3.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.content-encoding">
<section id="section-8.4">
        <h3 id="name-content-encoding">
<a href="#section-8.4" class="section-number selfRef">8.4. </a><a href="#name-content-encoding" class="section-name selfRef">Content-Encoding</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-content-encoding-170" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-content-encod" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-content-encoding-header-fie" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-8.4-1">
   The "Content-Encoding" header field indicates what content codings
   have been applied to the representation, beyond those inherent in the media
   type, and thus what decoding mechanisms have to be applied in order to
   obtain data in the media type referenced by the <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a>
   header field.
   Content-Encoding is primarily used to allow a representation's data to be
   compressed without losing the identity of its underlying media type.<a href="#section-8.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-content-encoding-17" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-8.4-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Content-Encoding = #content-coding
</pre><a href="#section-8.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.4-3">
   An example of its use is<a href="#section-8.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.4-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Content-Encoding: gzip
</pre><a href="#section-8.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.4-5">
   If one or more encodings have been applied to a representation, the sender
   that applied the encodings <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate a Content-Encoding header field
   that lists the content codings in the order in which they were applied.
   Note that the coding named "identity" is reserved for its special role
   in <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a> and thus <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> be included.<a href="#section-8.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.4-6">
   Additional information about the encoding parameters can be provided
   by other header fields not defined by this specification.<a href="#section-8.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.4-7">
   Unlike Transfer-Encoding (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-6.1" class="relref">Section 6.1</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>), the codings listed
   in Content-Encoding are a characteristic of the representation; the
   representation is defined in terms of the coded form, and all other
   metadata about the representation is about the coded form unless otherwise
   noted in the metadata definition. Typically, the representation is only
   decoded just prior to rendering or analogous usage.<a href="#section-8.4-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.4-8">
   If the media type includes an inherent encoding, such as a data format
   that is always compressed, then that encoding would not be restated in
   Content-Encoding even if it happens to be the same algorithm as one
   of the content codings.  Such a content coding would only be listed if,
   for some bizarre reason, it is applied a second time to form the
   representation.  Likewise, an origin server might choose to publish the
   same data as multiple representations that differ only in whether
   the coding is defined as part of <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> or
   Content-Encoding, since some user agents will behave differently in their
   handling of each response (e.g., open a "Save as ..." dialog instead of
   automatic decompression and rendering of content).<a href="#section-8.4-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.4-9">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> respond with a status code of
   <a href="#status.415" class="xref">415 (Unsupported Media Type)</a> if a representation in the
   request message has a content coding that is not acceptable.<a href="#section-8.4-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="content.codings">
<section id="section-8.4.1">
          <h4 id="name-content-codings">
<a href="#section-8.4.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.4.1. </a><a href="#name-content-codings" class="section-name selfRef">Content Codings</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-content-coding-174" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-compress-content-coding-175" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-x-compress-content-coding-1" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-deflate-content-coding-177" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-gzip-content-coding-178" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-x-gzip-content-coding-179" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-8.4.1-1">
   Content coding values indicate an encoding transformation that has
   been or can be applied to a representation. Content codings are primarily
   used to allow a representation to be compressed or otherwise usefully
   transformed without losing the identity of its underlying media type
   and without loss of information. Frequently, the representation is stored
   in coded form, transmitted directly, and only decoded by the final recipient.<a href="#section-8.4.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-content-coding-180" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-8.4.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  content-coding   = token
</pre><a href="#section-8.4.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.4.1-3">
   All content codings are case-insensitive and ought to be registered
   within the "HTTP Content Coding Registry", as described in
   <a href="#content.coding.extensibility" class="xref">Section 16.6</a><a href="#section-8.4.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.4.1-4">
   Content-coding values are used in the
   <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a> (<a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Section 12.5.3</a>)
   and <a href="#field.content-encoding" class="xref">Content-Encoding</a> (<a href="#field.content-encoding" class="xref">Section 8.4</a>)
   header fields.<a href="#section-8.4.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="compress.coding">
<section id="section-8.4.1.1">
            <h5 id="name-compress-coding">
<a href="#section-8.4.1.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.4.1.1. </a><a href="#name-compress-coding" class="section-name selfRef">Compress Coding</a>
            </h5>
<span id="iref-compress-coding-format-181" class="iref"></span>
            <p id="section-8.4.1.1-1">
   The "compress" coding is an adaptive Lempel-Ziv-Welch (LZW) coding
   <span>[<a href="#Welch" class="xref">Welch</a>]</span> that is commonly produced by the UNIX file
   compression program "compress".
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> consider "x-compress" to be equivalent to "compress".<a href="#section-8.4.1.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="deflate.coding">
<section id="section-8.4.1.2">
            <h5 id="name-deflate-coding">
<a href="#section-8.4.1.2" class="section-number selfRef">8.4.1.2. </a><a href="#name-deflate-coding" class="section-name selfRef">Deflate Coding</a>
            </h5>
<span id="iref-deflate-coding-format-182" class="iref"></span>
            <p id="section-8.4.1.2-1">
   The "deflate" coding is a "zlib" data format <span>[<a href="#RFC1950" class="xref">RFC1950</a>]</span>
   containing a "deflate" compressed data stream <span>[<a href="#RFC1951" class="xref">RFC1951</a>]</span>
   that uses a combination of the Lempel-Ziv (LZ77) compression algorithm and
   Huffman coding.<a href="#section-8.4.1.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-8.4.1.2-2">
              <p id="section-8.4.1.2-2.1">
                        <strong>Note:</strong> Some non-conformant implementations send the "deflate"
    compressed data without the zlib wrapper.<a href="#section-8.4.1.2-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="gzip.coding">
<section id="section-8.4.1.3">
            <h5 id="name-gzip-coding">
<a href="#section-8.4.1.3" class="section-number selfRef">8.4.1.3. </a><a href="#name-gzip-coding" class="section-name selfRef">Gzip Coding</a>
            </h5>
<span id="iref-gzip-coding-format-183" class="iref"></span>
            <p id="section-8.4.1.3-1">
   The "gzip" coding is an LZ77 coding with a 32-bit Cyclic Redundancy Check
   (CRC) that is commonly
   produced by the gzip file compression program <span>[<a href="#RFC1952" class="xref">RFC1952</a>]</span>.
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> consider "x-gzip" to be equivalent to "gzip".<a href="#section-8.4.1.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.content-language">
<section id="section-8.5">
        <h3 id="name-content-language">
<a href="#section-8.5" class="section-number selfRef">8.5. </a><a href="#name-content-language" class="section-name selfRef">Content-Language</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-content-language-184" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-content-langu" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-content-language-header-fie" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-8.5-1">
   The "Content-Language" header field describes the natural
   language(s) of the intended audience for the representation. Note that this might
   not be equivalent to all the languages used within the representation.<a href="#section-8.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-content-language-18" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-8.5-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Content-Language = #language-tag
</pre><a href="#section-8.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.5-3">
   Language tags are defined in <a href="#language.tags" class="xref">Section 8.5.1</a>. The primary purpose of
   Content-Language is to allow a user to identify and differentiate
   representations according to the users' own preferred language. Thus, if the
   content is intended only for a Danish-literate audience, the
   appropriate field is<a href="#section-8.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.5-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Content-Language: da
</pre><a href="#section-8.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.5-5">
   If no Content-Language is specified, the default is that the content
   is intended for all language audiences. This might mean that the
   sender does not consider it to be specific to any natural language,
   or that the sender does not know for which language it is intended.<a href="#section-8.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.5-6">
   Multiple languages <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> be listed for content that is intended for
   multiple audiences. For example, a rendition of the "Treaty of
   Waitangi", presented simultaneously in the original Maori and English
   versions, would call for<a href="#section-8.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.5-7">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Content-Language: mi, en
</pre><a href="#section-8.5-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.5-8">
   However, just because multiple languages are present within a representation
   does not mean that it is intended for multiple linguistic audiences.
   An example would be a beginner's language primer, such as "A First
   Lesson in Latin", which is clearly intended to be used by an
   English-literate audience. In this case, the Content-Language would
   properly only include "en".<a href="#section-8.5-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.5-9">
   Content-Language <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> be applied to any media type -- it is not
   limited to textual documents.<a href="#section-8.5-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="language.tags">
<section id="section-8.5.1">
          <h4 id="name-language-tags">
<a href="#section-8.5.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.5.1. </a><a href="#name-language-tags" class="section-name selfRef">Language Tags</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-8.5.1-1">
   A language tag, as defined in <span>[<a href="#RFC5646" class="xref">RFC5646</a>]</span>, identifies a
   natural language spoken, written, or otherwise conveyed by human beings for
   communication of information to other human beings. Computer languages are
   explicitly excluded.<a href="#section-8.5.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.5.1-2">
   HTTP uses language tags within the <a href="#field.accept-language" class="xref">Accept-Language</a> and
   <a href="#field.content-language" class="xref">Content-Language</a> header fields.
   <a href="#field.accept-language" class="xref">Accept-Language</a> uses the broader language-range production
   defined in <a href="#field.accept-language" class="xref">Section 12.5.4</a>, whereas
   <a href="#field.content-language" class="xref">Content-Language</a> uses the language-tag production defined
   below.<a href="#section-8.5.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-language-tag-188" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-8.5.1-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  language-tag = <Language-Tag, see [RFC5646], Section 2.1>
</pre><a href="#section-8.5.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.5.1-4">
   A language tag is a sequence of one or more case-insensitive subtags, each
   separated by a hyphen character ("-", %x2D).  In most cases, a language tag
   consists of a primary language subtag that identifies a broad family of
   related languages (e.g., "en" = English), which is optionally followed by a
   series of subtags that refine or narrow that language's range (e.g.,
   "en-CA" = the variety of English as communicated in Canada).
   Whitespace is not allowed within a language tag.
   Example tags include:<a href="#section-8.5.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div class="alignLeft art-text artwork" id="section-8.5.1-5">
<pre>
  fr, en-US, es-419, az-Arab, x-pig-latin, man-Nkoo-GN
</pre><a href="#section-8.5.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.5.1-6">
   See <span>[<a href="#RFC5646" class="xref">RFC5646</a>]</span> for further information.<a href="#section-8.5.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.content-length">
<section id="section-8.6">
        <h3 id="name-content-length">
<a href="#section-8.6" class="section-number selfRef">8.6. </a><a href="#name-content-length" class="section-name selfRef">Content-Length</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-content-length-189" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-content-lengt" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-content-length-header-field" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-8.6-1">
   The "Content-Length" header field indicates the associated representation's
   data length as a decimal non-negative integer number of octets.
   When transferring a representation as content, Content-Length refers
   specifically to the amount of data enclosed so that it can be used to
   delimit framing (e.g., <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-6.2" class="relref">Section 6.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).
   In other cases, Content-Length indicates the selected representation's
   current length, which can be used by recipients to estimate transfer time
   or to compare with previously stored representations.<a href="#section-8.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-content-length-192" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-8.6-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Content-Length = 1*DIGIT
</pre><a href="#section-8.6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.6-3">
   An example is<a href="#section-8.6-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.6-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Content-Length: 3495
</pre><a href="#section-8.6-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.6-5">
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send Content-Length in a request when the method
   defines a meaning for enclosed content and it is not sending
   Transfer-Encoding.
   For example, a user agent normally sends Content-Length in a POST request
   even when the value is 0 (indicating empty content).
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> send a
   Content-Length header field when the request message does not contain
   content and the method semantics do not anticipate such data.<a href="#section-8.6-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-6">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a Content-Length header field in a response to a HEAD
   request (<a href="#HEAD" class="xref">Section 9.3.2</a>); a server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send Content-Length in such a
   response unless its field value equals the decimal number of octets that
   would have been sent in the content of a response if the same
   request had used the GET method.<a href="#section-8.6-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-7">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a Content-Length header field in a
   <a href="#status.304" class="xref">304 (Not Modified)</a> response to a conditional GET request
   (<a href="#status.304" class="xref">Section 15.4.5</a>); a server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send Content-Length in such a
   response unless its field value equals the decimal number of octets that
   would have been sent in the content of a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a>
   response to the same request.<a href="#section-8.6-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-8">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a Content-Length header field in any response
   with a status code of
   <a href="#status.1xx" class="xref">1xx (Informational)</a> or <a href="#status.204" class="xref">204 (No Content)</a>.
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a Content-Length header field in any
   <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a> response to a CONNECT request (<a href="#CONNECT" class="xref">Section 9.3.6</a>).<a href="#section-8.6-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-9">
   Aside from the cases defined above, in the absence of Transfer-Encoding,
   an origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a Content-Length header field when the
   content size is known prior to sending the complete header section.
   This will allow downstream recipients to measure transfer progress,
   know when a received message is complete, and potentially reuse the
   connection for additional requests.<a href="#section-8.6-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-10">
   Any Content-Length field value greater than or equal to zero is valid.
   Since there is no predefined limit to the length of content, a
   recipient <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> anticipate potentially large decimal numerals and
   prevent parsing errors due to integer conversion overflows
   or precision loss due to integer conversion
   (<a href="#attack.protocol.element.length" class="xref">Section 17.5</a>).<a href="#section-8.6-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-11">
   Because Content-Length is used for message delimitation in HTTP/1.1,
   its field value can impact how the message is parsed by downstream
   recipients even when the immediate connection is not using HTTP/1.1.
   If the message is forwarded by a downstream intermediary, a Content-Length
   field value that is inconsistent with the received message framing might
   cause a security failure due to request smuggling or response splitting.<a href="#section-8.6-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-12">
   As a result, a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> forward a message with a
   Content-Length header field value that is known to be incorrect.<a href="#section-8.6-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.6-13">
   Likewise, a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> forward a message with a Content-Length
   header field value that does not match the ABNF above, with one exception:
   a recipient of a Content-Length header field value consisting of the same
   decimal value repeated as a comma-separated list (e.g,
   "Content-Length: 42, 42") <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> either reject the message as invalid or
   replace that invalid field value with a single instance of the decimal
   value, since this likely indicates that a duplicate was generated or
   combined by an upstream message processor.<a href="#section-8.6-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.content-location">
<section id="section-8.7">
        <h3 id="name-content-location">
<a href="#section-8.7" class="section-number selfRef">8.7. </a><a href="#name-content-location" class="section-name selfRef">Content-Location</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-fields-content-location-193" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-header-fields-content-locat" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-content-location-header-fie" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-8.7-1">
   The "Content-Location" header field references a URI that can be used
   as an identifier for a specific resource corresponding to the
   representation in this message's content.
   In other words, if one were to perform a GET request on this URI at the time
   of this message's generation, then a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response would
   contain the same representation that is enclosed as content in this message.<a href="#section-8.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-content-location-19" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-8.7-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Content-Location = absolute-URI / partial-URI
</pre><a href="#section-8.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.7-3">
   The field value is either an <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">absolute-URI</a> or a
   <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">partial-URI</a>. In the latter case (<a href="#uri" class="xref">Section 4</a>),
   the referenced URI is relative to the target URI
   (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-5" class="relref">Section 5</a></span>).<a href="#section-8.7-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.7-4">
   The Content-Location value is not a replacement for the target URI
   (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>).  It is representation metadata.
   It has the same syntax and semantics as the header field of the same name
   defined for MIME body parts in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2557#section-4" class="relref">Section 4</a> of [<a href="#RFC2557" class="xref">RFC2557</a>]</span>.
   However, its appearance in an HTTP message has some special implications
   for HTTP recipients.<a href="#section-8.7-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.7-5">
   If Content-Location is included in a <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a>
   response message and its value refers (after conversion to absolute form)
   to a URI that is the same as the target URI, then
   the recipient <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> consider the content to be a current representation of
   that resource at the time indicated by the message origination date.
   For a GET (<a href="#GET" class="xref">Section 9.3.1</a>) or HEAD (<a href="#HEAD" class="xref">Section 9.3.2</a>) request,
   this is the same as the default semantics when no Content-Location is
   provided by the server.
   For a state-changing request like PUT (<a href="#PUT" class="xref">Section 9.3.4</a>) or
   POST (<a href="#POST" class="xref">Section 9.3.3</a>), it implies that the server's response
   contains the new representation of that resource, thereby distinguishing it
   from representations that might only report about the action
   (e.g., "It worked!").
   This allows authoring applications to update their local copies without
   the need for a subsequent GET request.<a href="#section-8.7-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.7-6">
   If Content-Location is included in a <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a>
   response message and its field value refers to a URI that differs from the
   target URI, then the origin server claims that the URI
   is an identifier for a different resource corresponding to the enclosed
   representation. Such a claim can only be trusted if both identifiers share
   the same resource owner, which cannot be programmatically determined via
   HTTP.<a href="#section-8.7-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-8.7-7.1">For a response to a GET or HEAD request, this is an indication that the
     target URI refers to a resource that is subject to content
     negotiation and the Content-Location field value is a more specific
     identifier for the <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>.<a href="#section-8.7-7.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li class="normal" id="section-8.7-7.2">For a <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> response to a state-changing method,
     a Content-Location field value that is identical to the
     <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> field value indicates that this content is a
     current representation of the newly created resource.<a href="#section-8.7-7.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li class="normal" id="section-8.7-7.3">Otherwise, such a Content-Location indicates that this content is a
     representation reporting on the requested action's status and that the
     same report is available (for future access with GET) at the given URI.
     For example, a purchase transaction made via a POST request might
     include a receipt document as the content of the <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a>
     response; the Content-Location field value provides an identifier for
     retrieving a copy of that same receipt in the future.<a href="#section-8.7-7.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        </ul>
<p id="section-8.7-8">
   A user agent that sends Content-Location in a request message is stating
   that its value refers to where the user agent originally obtained the
   content of the enclosed representation (prior to any modifications made by
   that user agent).  In other words, the user agent is providing a back link
   to the source of the original representation.<a href="#section-8.7-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.7-9">
   An origin server that receives a Content-Location field in a request
   message <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> treat the information as transitory request context rather
   than as metadata to be saved verbatim as part of the representation.
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use that context to guide in processing the
   request or to save it for other uses, such as within source links or
   versioning metadata. However, an origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> use such context
   information to alter the request semantics.<a href="#section-8.7-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.7-10">
   For example, if a client makes a PUT request on a negotiated resource and
   the origin server accepts that PUT (without redirection), then the new
   state of that resource is expected to be consistent with the one
   representation supplied in that PUT; the Content-Location cannot be used as
   a form of reverse content selection identifier to update only one of the
   negotiated representations. If the user agent had wanted the latter
   semantics, it would have applied the PUT directly to the Content-Location
   URI.<a href="#section-8.7-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="response.validator">
<section id="section-8.8">
        <h3 id="name-validator-fields">
<a href="#section-8.8" class="section-number selfRef">8.8. </a><a href="#name-validator-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Validator Fields</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-metadata-197" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-validator-198" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-selected-representation-199" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-8.8-1">
   Resource metadata is referred to as a "validator" if it
   can be used within a precondition (<a href="#preconditions" class="xref">Section 13.1</a>) to
   make a conditional request (<a href="#conditional.requests" class="xref">Section 13</a>).
   Validator fields convey a current validator for the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>
   (<a href="#representations" class="xref">Section 3.2</a>).<a href="#section-8.8-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8-2">
   In responses to safe requests, validator fields describe the selected
   representation chosen by the origin server while handling the response.
   Note that, depending on the method and status code semantics, the
   selected representation for a given response is not
   necessarily the same as the representation enclosed as response content.<a href="#section-8.8-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8-3">
   In a successful response to a state-changing request, validator fields
   describe the new representation that has replaced the prior
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> as a result of processing the
   request.<a href="#section-8.8-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8-4">
   For example, an ETag field in a <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> response
   communicates the entity tag of the newly created resource's
   representation, so that the entity tag can be used as a validator in
   later conditional requests to prevent the "lost update" problem.<a href="#section-8.8-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8-5">
   This specification defines two forms of metadata that are commonly used
   to observe resource state and test for preconditions: modification dates
   (<a href="#field.last-modified" class="xref">Section 8.8.2</a>) and opaque entity tags
   (<a href="#field.etag" class="xref">Section 8.8.3</a>).
   Additional metadata that reflects resource state
   has been defined by various extensions of HTTP, such as Web Distributed
   Authoring and Versioning <span>[<a href="#WEBDAV" class="xref">WEBDAV</a>]</span>, that are beyond the
   scope of this specification.<a href="#section-8.8-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="weak.and.strong.validators">
<section id="section-8.8.1">
          <h4 id="name-weak-versus-strong">
<a href="#section-8.8.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.1. </a><a href="#name-weak-versus-strong" class="section-name selfRef">Weak versus Strong</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-validator-weak-200" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-validator-strong-201" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-8.8.1-1">
   Validators come in two flavors: strong or weak.  Weak validators are easy
   to generate but are far less useful for comparisons.  Strong validators
   are ideal for comparisons but can be very difficult (and occasionally
   impossible) to generate efficiently.  Rather than impose that all forms
   of resource adhere to the same strength of validator, HTTP exposes the
   type of validator in use and imposes restrictions on when weak validators
   can be used as preconditions.<a href="#section-8.8.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-2">
   A "strong validator" is representation metadata that changes value whenever
   a change occurs to the representation data that would be observable in the
   content of a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response to GET.<a href="#section-8.8.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-3">
   A strong validator might change for reasons other than a change to the
   representation data, such as when a
   semantically significant part of the representation metadata is changed
   (e.g., <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a>), but it is in the best interests of the
   origin server to only change the value when it is necessary to invalidate
   the stored responses held by remote caches and authoring tools.<a href="#section-8.8.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-4">
   Cache entries might persist for arbitrarily long periods, regardless
   of expiration times.  Thus, a cache might attempt to validate an
   entry using a validator that it obtained in the distant past.
   A strong validator is unique across all versions of all
   representations associated with a particular resource over time.
   However, there is no implication of uniqueness across representations
   of different resources (i.e., the same strong validator might be
   in use for representations of multiple resources at the same time
   and does not imply that those representations are equivalent).<a href="#section-8.8.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-5">
   There are a variety of strong validators used in practice.  The best are
   based on strict revision control, wherein each change to a representation
   always results in a unique node name and revision identifier being assigned
   before the representation is made accessible to GET.
   A collision-resistant hash
   function applied to the representation data is also sufficient if the data
   is available prior to the response header fields being sent and the digest
   does not need to be recalculated every time a validation request is
   received.  However, if a resource has distinct representations that differ
   only in their metadata, such as might occur with content negotiation over
   media types that happen to share the same data format, then the origin
   server needs to incorporate additional information in the validator to
   distinguish those representations.<a href="#section-8.8.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-6">
   In contrast, a "weak validator" is representation metadata
   that might not change for every change to the representation data. This
   weakness might be due to limitations in how the value is calculated
   (e.g., clock resolution), an inability to ensure uniqueness for all
   possible representations of the resource, or a desire of the resource
   owner to group representations by some self-determined set of
   equivalency rather than unique sequences of data.<a href="#section-8.8.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-7">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> change a weak entity tag whenever it
   considers prior representations to be unacceptable as a substitute for
   the current representation. In other words, a weak entity tag ought to
   change whenever the origin server wants caches to invalidate old
   responses.<a href="#section-8.8.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-8">
   For example, the representation of a weather report that changes in
   content every second, based on dynamic measurements, might be grouped
   into sets of equivalent representations (from the origin server's
   perspective) with the same weak validator in order to allow cached
   representations to be valid for a reasonable period of time (perhaps
   adjusted dynamically based on server load or weather quality).
   Likewise, a representation's modification time, if defined with only
   one-second resolution, might be a weak validator if it is possible
   for the representation to be modified twice during a single second and
   retrieved between those modifications.<a href="#section-8.8.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-9">
   Likewise, a validator is weak if it is shared by two or more
   representations of a given resource at the same time, unless those
   representations have identical representation data. For example, if the
   origin server sends the same validator for a representation with a gzip
   content coding applied as it does for a representation with no content
   coding, then that validator is weak. However, two simultaneous
   representations might share the same strong validator if they differ only
   in the representation metadata, such as when two different media types are
   available for the same representation data.<a href="#section-8.8.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.1-10">
   Strong validators are usable for all conditional requests, including cache
   validation, partial content ranges, and "lost update" avoidance.
   Weak validators are only usable when the client does not require exact
   equality with previously obtained representation data, such as when
   validating a cache entry or limiting a web traversal to recent changes.<a href="#section-8.8.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.last-modified">
<section id="section-8.8.2">
          <h4 id="name-last-modified">
<a href="#section-8.8.2" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.2. </a><a href="#name-last-modified" class="section-name selfRef">Last-Modified</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-last-modified-202" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-last-modified" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-last-modified-header-field-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-8.8.2-1">
   The "Last-Modified" header field in a response provides a timestamp
   indicating the date and time at which the origin server believes the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> was last modified, as determined at the conclusion
   of handling the request.<a href="#section-8.8.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-last-modified-205" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-8.8.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Last-Modified = HTTP-date
</pre><a href="#section-8.8.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.8.2-3">
   An example of its use is<a href="#section-8.8.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.8.2-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Last-Modified: Tue, 15 Nov 1994 12:45:26 GMT
</pre><a href="#section-8.8.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<div id="lastmod.generation">
<section id="section-8.8.2.1">
            <h5 id="name-generation">
<a href="#section-8.8.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.2.1. </a><a href="#name-generation" class="section-name selfRef">Generation</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-8.8.2.1-1">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send Last-Modified for any selected
   representation for which a last modification date can be reasonably
   and consistently determined, since its use in conditional requests
   and evaluating cache freshness (<span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>) can
   substantially reduce unnecessary transfers and significantly
   improve service availability and scalability.<a href="#section-8.8.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.2.1-2">
   A representation is typically the sum of many parts behind the
   resource interface.  The last-modified time would usually be
   the most recent time that any of those parts were changed.
   How that value is determined for any given resource is an
   implementation detail beyond the scope of this specification.<a href="#section-8.8.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.2.1-3">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> obtain the Last-Modified value of the
   representation as close as possible to the time that it generates the
   <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a> field value for its response. This allows a recipient to
   make an accurate assessment of the representation's modification time,
   especially if the representation changes near the time that the
   response is generated.<a href="#section-8.8.2.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.2.1-4">
   An origin server with a clock (as defined in <a href="#http.date" class="xref">Section 5.6.7</a>)
   <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a Last-Modified date that is later than the
   server's time of message origination
   (<a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a>, <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Section 6.6.1</a>).
   If the last modification time is derived from implementation-specific
   metadata that evaluates to some time in the future, according to the
   origin server's clock, then the origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> replace that
   value with the message origination date. This prevents a future
   modification date from having an adverse impact on cache validation.<a href="#section-8.8.2.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.2.1-5">
   An origin server without a clock <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a Last-Modified
   date for a response unless that date value was assigned to the resource
   by some other system (presumably one with a clock).<a href="#section-8.8.2.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="lastmod.comparison">
<section id="section-8.8.2.2">
            <h5 id="name-comparison">
<a href="#section-8.8.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.2.2. </a><a href="#name-comparison" class="section-name selfRef">Comparison</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-8.8.2.2-1">
   A Last-Modified time, when used as a validator in a request, is
   implicitly weak unless it is possible to deduce that it is strong,
   using the following rules:<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-8.8.2.2-2.1">The validator is being compared by an origin server to the
       actual current validator for the representation and,<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li class="normal" id="section-8.8.2.2-2.2">That origin server reliably knows that the associated representation did
       not change twice during the second covered by the presented
       validator;<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-2.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            </ul>
<p id="section-8.8.2.2-3">
   or<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-8.8.2.2-4.1">The validator is about to be used by a client in an
       <a href="#field.if-modified-since" class="xref">If-Modified-Since</a>,
       <a href="#field.if-unmodified-since" class="xref">If-Unmodified-Since</a>, or <a href="#field.if-range" class="xref">If-Range</a> header
       field, because the client has a cache entry for the associated
       representation, and<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li class="normal" id="section-8.8.2.2-4.2">That cache entry includes a <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a> value which is
       at least one second after the Last-Modified value and
       the client has reason to believe that they were generated by the
       same clock or that there is enough difference between the Last-Modified
       and Date values to make clock synchronization issues unlikely;<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-4.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            </ul>
<p id="section-8.8.2.2-5">
   or<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-8.8.2.2-6.1">The validator is being compared by an intermediate cache to the
       validator stored in its cache entry for the representation, and<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-6.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li class="normal" id="section-8.8.2.2-6.2">That cache entry includes a <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a> value which is
       at least one second after the Last-Modified value and
       the cache has reason to believe that they were generated by the
       same clock or that there is enough difference between the Last-Modified
       and Date values to make clock synchronization issues unlikely.<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-6.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            </ul>
<p id="section-8.8.2.2-7">
   This method relies on the fact that if two different responses were
   sent by the origin server during the same second, but both had the
   same Last-Modified time, then at least one of those responses would
   have a <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a> value equal to its Last-Modified time.<a href="#section-8.8.2.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.etag">
<section id="section-8.8.3">
          <h4 id="name-etag">
<a href="#section-8.8.3" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.3. </a><a href="#name-etag" class="section-name selfRef">ETag</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-etag-206" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-etag-207" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-trailer-fields-etag-208" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-etag-field-209" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-8.8.3-1">
   The "ETag" field in a response provides the current entity tag for
   the <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>, as determined at the conclusion of handling
   the request.
   An entity tag is an opaque validator for differentiating between
   multiple representations of the same resource, regardless of whether
   those multiple representations are due to resource state changes over
   time, content negotiation resulting in multiple representations being
   valid at the same time, or both. An entity tag consists of an opaque
   quoted string, possibly prefixed by a weakness indicator.<a href="#section-8.8.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-etag-210" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-entity-tag-211" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-weak-212" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-opaque-tag-213" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-etagc-214" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-8.8.3-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  ETag       = entity-tag

  entity-tag = [ weak ] opaque-tag
  weak       = %s"W/"
  opaque-tag = DQUOTE *etagc DQUOTE
  etagc      = %x21 / %x23-7E / obs-text
             ; VCHAR except double quotes, plus obs-text
</pre><a href="#section-8.8.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<aside id="section-8.8.3-3">
            <p id="section-8.8.3-3.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Previously, opaque-tag was defined to be a quoted-string
    (<span>[<a href="#RFC2616" class="xref">RFC2616</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2616#section-3.11" class="relref">Section 3.11</a></span>); thus, some recipients
    might perform backslash unescaping. Servers therefore ought to avoid
    backslash characters in entity tags.<a href="#section-8.8.3-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<p id="section-8.8.3-4">
   An entity tag can be more reliable for validation than a modification
   date in situations where it is inconvenient to store modification
   dates, where the one-second resolution of HTTP-date values is not
   sufficient, or where modification dates are not consistently maintained.<a href="#section-8.8.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3-5">
  Examples:<a href="#section-8.8.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.8.3-6">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">ETag: "xyzzy"
ETag: W/"xyzzy"
ETag: ""
</pre><a href="#section-8.8.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.8.3-7">
   An entity tag can be either a weak or strong validator, with
   strong being the default.  If an origin server provides an entity tag
   for a representation and the generation of that entity tag does not satisfy
   all of the characteristics of a strong validator
   (<a href="#weak.and.strong.validators" class="xref">Section 8.8.1</a>), then the origin server
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> mark the entity tag as weak by prefixing its opaque value
   with "W/" (case-sensitive).<a href="#section-8.8.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3-8">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send the ETag field in a trailer section (see
   <a href="#trailer.fields" class="xref">Section 6.5</a>). However, since trailers are often
   ignored, it is preferable to send ETag as a header field unless the
   entity tag is generated while sending the content.<a href="#section-8.8.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="entity.tag.generation">
<section id="section-8.8.3.1">
            <h5 id="name-generation-2">
<a href="#section-8.8.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.3.1. </a><a href="#name-generation-2" class="section-name selfRef">Generation</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-8.8.3.1-1">
   The principle behind entity tags is that only the service author
   knows the implementation of a resource well enough to select the
   most accurate and efficient validation mechanism for that resource,
   and that any such mechanism can be mapped to a simple sequence of
   octets for easy comparison.  Since the value is opaque, there is no
   need for the client to be aware of how each entity tag is constructed.<a href="#section-8.8.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3.1-2">
   For example, a resource that has implementation-specific versioning
   applied to all changes might use an internal revision number, perhaps
   combined with a variance identifier for content negotiation, to
   accurately differentiate between representations.
   Other implementations might use a collision-resistant hash of
   representation content, a combination of various file attributes, or
   a modification timestamp that has sub-second resolution.<a href="#section-8.8.3.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3.1-3">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send an ETag for any selected representation
   for which detection of changes can be reasonably and consistently
   determined, since the entity tag's use in conditional requests and
   evaluating cache freshness (<span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>) can
   substantially reduce unnecessary transfers and significantly
   improve service availability, scalability, and reliability.<a href="#section-8.8.3.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="entity.tag.comparison">
<section id="section-8.8.3.2">
            <h5 id="name-comparison-2">
<a href="#section-8.8.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.3.2. </a><a href="#name-comparison-2" class="section-name selfRef">Comparison</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-8.8.3.2-1">
   There are two entity tag comparison functions, depending on whether or not
   the comparison context allows the use of weak validators:<a href="#section-8.8.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span class="break"></span><dl class="dlParallel" id="section-8.8.3.2-2">
              <dt id="section-8.8.3.2-2.1">
                        "Strong comparison":
              </dt>
              <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-8.8.3.2-2.2">
    two entity tags are equivalent if both are not weak and their opaque-tags
    match character-by-character.<a href="#section-8.8.3.2-2.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
              <dd class="break"></dd>
<dt id="section-8.8.3.2-2.3">
                        "Weak comparison":
              </dt>
              <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-8.8.3.2-2.4">
    two entity tags are equivalent if their opaque-tags match
    character-by-character, regardless of either or both being tagged as "weak".<a href="#section-8.8.3.2-2.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
            <dd class="break"></dd>
</dl>
<p id="section-8.8.3.2-3">
   The example below shows the results for a set of entity tag pairs and both
   the weak and strong comparison function results:<a href="#section-8.8.3.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<table class="left" id="table-3">
              <caption><a href="#table-3" class="selfRef">Table 3</a></caption>
<thead>
                <tr>
                  <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">ETag 1</th>
                  <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">ETag 2</th>
                  <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Strong Comparison</th>
                  <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Weak Comparison</th>
                </tr>
              </thead>
              <tbody>
                <tr>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">W/"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">W/"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">no match</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">match</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">W/"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">W/"2"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">no match</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">no match</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">W/"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">no match</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">match</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">"1"</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">match</td>
                  <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">match</td>
                </tr>
              </tbody>
            </table>
</section>
</div>
<div id="example.entity.tag.vs.conneg">
<section id="section-8.8.3.3">
            <h5 id="name-example-entity-tags-varying">
<a href="#section-8.8.3.3" class="section-number selfRef">8.8.3.3. </a><a href="#name-example-entity-tags-varying" class="section-name selfRef">Example: Entity Tags Varying on Content-Negotiated Resources</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-8.8.3.3-1">
   Consider a resource that is subject to content negotiation
   (<a href="#content.negotiation" class="xref">Section 12</a>), and where the representations sent in response to
   a GET request vary based on the <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a> request
   header field (<a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Section 12.5.3</a>):<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3.3-2">
   >> Request:<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.8.3.3-3">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">GET /index HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
Accept-Encoding: gzip

</pre><a href="#section-8.8.3.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.8.3.3-4">
   In this case, the response might or might not use the gzip content coding.
   If it does not, the response might look like:<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3.3-5">
   >> Response:<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.8.3.3-6">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Fri, 26 Mar 2010 00:05:00 GMT
ETag: "123-a"
Content-Length: 70
Vary: Accept-Encoding
Content-Type: text/plain

Hello World!
Hello World!
Hello World!
Hello World!
Hello World!
</pre><a href="#section-8.8.3.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-8.8.3.3-7">
   An alternative representation that does use gzip content coding would be:<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-8.8.3.3-8">
   >> Response:<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-8.8.3.3-9">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Fri, 26 Mar 2010 00:05:00 GMT
ETag: "123-b"
Content-Length: 43
Vary: Accept-Encoding
Content-Type: text/plain
Content-Encoding: gzip

...binary data...</pre><a href="#section-8.8.3.3-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<aside id="section-8.8.3.3-10">
              <p id="section-8.8.3.3-10.1">
                        <strong>Note:</strong> Content codings are a property of the representation data,
    so a strong entity tag for a content-encoded representation has to be
    distinct from the entity tag of an unencoded representation to prevent
    potential conflicts during cache updates and range requests. In contrast,
    transfer codings (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-7" class="relref">Section 7</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) apply only during message transfer
    and do not result in distinct entity tags.<a href="#section-8.8.3.3-10.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="methods">
<section id="section-9">
      <h2 id="name-methods">
<a href="#section-9" class="section-number selfRef">9. </a><a href="#name-methods" class="section-name selfRef">Methods</a>
      </h2>
<div id="method.overview">
<section id="section-9.1">
        <h3 id="name-overview">
<a href="#section-9.1" class="section-number selfRef">9.1. </a><a href="#name-overview" class="section-name selfRef">Overview</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-9.1-1">
   The request method token is the primary source of request semantics;
   it indicates the purpose for which the client has made this request
   and what is expected by the client as a successful result.<a href="#section-9.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.1-2">
   The request method's semantics might be further specialized by the
   semantics of some header fields when present in a request
   if those additional semantics do not conflict with the method.
   For example, a client can send conditional request header fields
   (<a href="#preconditions" class="xref">Section 13.1</a>) to make the requested
   action conditional on the current state of the target resource.<a href="#section-9.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.1-3">
   HTTP is designed to be usable as an interface to distributed
   object systems. The request method invokes an action to be applied to
   a <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> in much the same way that a remote
   method invocation can be sent to an identified object.<a href="#section-9.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-method-215" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-9.1-4">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  method = token
</pre><a href="#section-9.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-9.1-5">
   The method token is case-sensitive because it might be used as a gateway
   to object-based systems with case-sensitive method names. By convention,
   standardized methods are defined in all-uppercase US-ASCII letters.<a href="#section-9.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.1-6">
   Unlike distributed objects, the standardized request methods in HTTP are
   not resource-specific, since uniform interfaces provide for better
   visibility and reuse in network-based systems <span>[<a href="#REST" class="xref">REST</a>]</span>.
   Once defined, a standardized method ought to have the same semantics when
   applied to any resource, though each resource determines for itself
   whether those semantics are implemented or allowed.<a href="#section-9.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.1-7">
   This specification defines a number of standardized methods that are
   commonly used in HTTP, as outlined by the following table.<a href="#section-9.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="table.of.methods">
<table class="left" id="table-4">
          <caption><a href="#table-4" class="selfRef">Table 4</a></caption>
<thead>
            <tr>
              <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Method Name</th>
              <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Description</th>
              <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Section</th>
            </tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">GET</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Transfer a current representation of the target resource.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#GET" class="xref">9.3.1</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">HEAD</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Same as GET, but do not transfer the response content.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#HEAD" class="xref">9.3.2</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">POST</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Perform resource-specific processing on the request content.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#POST" class="xref">9.3.3</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">PUT</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Replace all current representations of the target resource with
        the request content.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#PUT" class="xref">9.3.4</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">DELETE</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Remove all current representations of the target resource.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#DELETE" class="xref">9.3.5</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">CONNECT</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Establish a tunnel to the server identified by the target resource.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#CONNECT" class="xref">9.3.6</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">OPTIONS</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Describe the communication options for the target resource.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#OPTIONS" class="xref">9.3.7</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">TRACE</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Perform a message loop-back test along the path to the target resource.</td>
              <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">
                <a href="#TRACE" class="xref">9.3.8</a>
              </td>
            </tr>
          </tbody>
        </table>
</div>
<p id="section-9.1-9">
   All general-purpose servers <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> support the methods GET and HEAD.
   All other methods are <span class="bcp14">OPTIONAL</span>.<a href="#section-9.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.1-10">
   The set of methods allowed by a target resource can be listed in an
   <a href="#field.allow" class="xref">Allow</a> header field (<a href="#field.allow" class="xref">Section 10.2.1</a>).
   However, the set of allowed methods can change dynamically.
   An origin server that receives a request method that is unrecognized or
   not implemented <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> respond with the
   <a href="#status.501" class="xref">501 (Not Implemented)</a> status code.
   An origin server that receives a request method that is recognized and
   implemented, but not allowed for the target resource, <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> respond
   with the <a href="#status.405" class="xref">405 (Method Not Allowed)</a> status code.<a href="#section-9.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.1-11">
   Additional methods, outside the scope of this specification, have been
   specified for use in HTTP. All such methods ought to be registered
   within the "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Method Registry",
   as described in <a href="#method.extensibility" class="xref">Section 16.1</a>.<a href="#section-9.1-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="method.properties">
<section id="section-9.2">
        <h3 id="name-common-method-properties">
<a href="#section-9.2" class="section-number selfRef">9.2. </a><a href="#name-common-method-properties" class="section-name selfRef">Common Method Properties</a>
        </h3>
<div id="safe.methods">
<section id="section-9.2.1">
          <h4 id="name-safe-methods">
<a href="#section-9.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">9.2.1. </a><a href="#name-safe-methods" class="section-name selfRef">Safe Methods</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-safe-216" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.2.1-1">
   Request methods are considered "safe" if
   their defined semantics are essentially read-only; i.e., the client does
   not request, and does not expect, any state change on the origin server
   as a result of applying a safe method to a target resource.  Likewise,
   reasonable use of a safe method is not expected to cause any harm,
   loss of property, or unusual burden on the origin server.<a href="#section-9.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.1-2">
   This definition of safe methods does not prevent an implementation from
   including behavior that is potentially harmful, that is not entirely read-only,
   or that causes side effects while invoking a safe method.  What is
   important, however, is that the client did not request that additional
   behavior and cannot be held accountable for it.  For example,
   most servers append request information to access log files at the
   completion of every response, regardless of the method, and that is
   considered safe even though the log storage might become full and cause
   the server to fail.  Likewise, a safe request initiated by selecting an
   advertisement on the Web will often have the side effect of charging an
   advertising account.<a href="#section-9.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.1-3">
   Of the request methods defined by this specification, the
   <a href="#GET" class="xref">GET</a>, <a href="#HEAD" class="xref">HEAD</a>, <a href="#OPTIONS" class="xref">OPTIONS</a>, and
   <a href="#TRACE" class="xref">TRACE</a> methods are defined to be safe.<a href="#section-9.2.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.1-4">
   The purpose of distinguishing between safe and unsafe methods is to
   allow automated retrieval processes (spiders) and cache performance
   optimization (pre-fetching) to work without fear of causing harm.
   In addition, it allows a user agent to apply appropriate constraints
   on the automated use of unsafe methods when processing potentially
   untrusted content.<a href="#section-9.2.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.1-5">
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> distinguish between safe and unsafe methods when
   presenting potential actions to a user, such that the user can be made
   aware of an unsafe action before it is requested.<a href="#section-9.2.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.1-6">
   When a resource is constructed such that parameters within the target URI
   have the effect of selecting an action, it is the resource
   owner's responsibility to ensure that the action is consistent with the
   request method semantics.
   For example, it is common for Web-based content editing software
   to use actions within query parameters, such as "page?do=delete".
   If the purpose of such a resource is to perform an unsafe action, then
   the resource owner <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> disable or disallow that action when it is
   accessed using a safe request method. Failure to do so will result in
   unfortunate side effects when automated processes perform a GET on
   every URI reference for the sake of link maintenance, pre-fetching,
   building a search index, etc.<a href="#section-9.2.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="idempotent.methods">
<section id="section-9.2.2">
          <h4 id="name-idempotent-methods">
<a href="#section-9.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">9.2.2. </a><a href="#name-idempotent-methods" class="section-name selfRef">Idempotent Methods</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-idempotent-217" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.2.2-1">
   A request method is considered "idempotent"
   if the intended effect on the server of multiple identical requests with
   that method is the same as the effect for a single such request.
   Of the request methods defined by this
   specification, <a href="#PUT" class="xref">PUT</a>, <a href="#DELETE" class="xref">DELETE</a>, and safe request
   methods are idempotent.<a href="#section-9.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.2-2">
   Like the definition of safe, the idempotent property only applies to
   what has been requested by the user; a server is free to log each request
   separately, retain a revision control history, or implement other
   non-idempotent side effects for each idempotent request.<a href="#section-9.2.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.2-3">
   Idempotent methods are distinguished because the request can be repeated
   automatically if a communication failure occurs before the client is
   able to read the server's response.  For example, if a client sends a PUT
   request and the underlying connection is closed before any response is
   received, then the client can establish a new connection and retry the
   idempotent request. It knows that repeating the request will have
   the same intended effect, even if the original request succeeded, though
   the response might differ.<a href="#section-9.2.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.2-4">
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> automatically retry a request with a non-idempotent
   method unless it has some means to know that the request semantics are
   actually idempotent, regardless of the method, or some means to detect that
   the original request was never applied.<a href="#section-9.2.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.2-5">
   For example, a user agent can repeat a POST request automatically if it
   knows (through design or configuration) that the request is safe for that
   resource. Likewise, a user agent designed specifically to operate on
   a version control repository might be able to recover from partial failure
   conditions by checking the target resource revision(s) after a failed
   connection, reverting or fixing any changes that were partially applied,
   and then automatically retrying the requests that failed.<a href="#section-9.2.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.2-6">
   Some clients take a riskier approach and attempt to guess when an
   automatic retry is possible. For example, a client might automatically
   retry a POST request if the underlying transport connection closed before
   any part of a response is received, particularly if an idle persistent
   connection was used.<a href="#section-9.2.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.2-7">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> automatically retry non-idempotent requests.
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> automatically retry a failed automatic retry.<a href="#section-9.2.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="cacheable.methods">
<section id="section-9.2.3">
          <h4 id="name-methods-and-caching">
<a href="#section-9.2.3" class="section-number selfRef">9.2.3. </a><a href="#name-methods-and-caching" class="section-name selfRef">Methods and Caching</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-9.2.3-1">
   For a cache to store and use a response, the associated method needs to
   explicitly allow caching and to detail under what conditions a response can
   be used to satisfy subsequent requests; a method definition that does not
   do so cannot be cached. For additional requirements see <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-9.2.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.2.3-2">
   This specification defines caching semantics for GET, HEAD, and POST,
   although the overwhelming majority of cache implementations only support
   GET and HEAD.<a href="#section-9.2.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="method.definitions">
<section id="section-9.3">
        <h3 id="name-method-definitions">
<a href="#section-9.3" class="section-number selfRef">9.3. </a><a href="#name-method-definitions" class="section-name selfRef">Method Definitions</a>
        </h3>
<div id="GET">
<section id="section-9.3.1">
          <h4 id="name-get">
<a href="#section-9.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.1. </a><a href="#name-get" class="section-name selfRef">GET</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-get-method-218" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-get-219" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.1-1">
   The GET method requests transfer of a current
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> for the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>.
   A successful response reflects the quality of "sameness" identified by
   the target URI (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-1.2.2" class="relref">Section 1.2.2</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>). Hence,
   retrieving identifiable information via HTTP is usually performed by
   making a GET request on an identifier associated with the potential for
   providing that information in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response.<a href="#section-9.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-2">
   GET is the primary mechanism of information retrieval and the focus of
   almost all performance optimizations. Applications that produce a URI for
   each important resource can benefit from those optimizations while enabling
   their reuse by other applications, creating a network effect that promotes
   further expansion of the Web.<a href="#section-9.3.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-3">
   It is tempting to think of resource identifiers as remote file system
   pathnames and of representations as being a copy of the contents of such
   files. In fact, that is how many resources are implemented (see
   <a href="#attack.pathname" class="xref">Section 17.3</a> for related security considerations).
   However, there are no such limitations in practice.<a href="#section-9.3.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-4">
   The HTTP interface for
   a resource is just as likely to be implemented as a tree of content
   objects, a programmatic view on various database records, or a gateway to
   other information systems. Even when the URI mapping mechanism is tied to a
   file system, an origin server might be configured to execute the files with
   the request as input and send the output as the representation rather than
   transfer the files directly. Regardless, only the origin server needs to
   know how each resource identifier corresponds to an implementation
   and how that implementation manages to select and send a current
   representation of the target resource.<a href="#section-9.3.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-5">
   A client can alter the semantics of GET to be a "range request", requesting
   transfer of only some part(s) of the selected representation, by sending a
   <a href="#field.range" class="xref">Range</a> header field in the request (<a href="#field.range" class="xref">Section 14.2</a>).<a href="#section-9.3.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-6">
   Although request message framing is independent of the method used,
   content received in a GET request has no generally defined semantics,
   cannot alter the meaning or target of the request, and might lead some
   implementations to reject the request and close the connection because of
   its potential as a request smuggling attack
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-11.2" class="relref">Section 11.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate content in a GET request unless it is
   made directly to an origin server that has previously indicated,
   in or out of band, that such a request has a purpose and will be adequately
   supported. An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> rely on private agreements to
   receive content, since participants in HTTP communication are often
   unaware of intermediaries along the request chain.<a href="#section-9.3.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-7">
   The response to a GET request is cacheable; a cache <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use it to satisfy
   subsequent GET and HEAD requests unless otherwise indicated by the
