   Cache-Control header field (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2" class="relref">Section 5.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-9.3.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.1-8">
   When information retrieval is performed with a mechanism that constructs a
   target URI from user-provided information, such as the query fields of a
   form using GET, potentially sensitive data might be provided that would not
   be appropriate for disclosure within a URI
   (see <a href="#sensitive.information.in.uris" class="xref">Section 17.9</a>). In some cases, the
   data can be filtered or transformed such that it would not reveal such
   information. In others, particularly when there is no benefit from caching
   a response, using the POST method (<a href="#POST" class="xref">Section 9.3.3</a>) instead of GET
   can transmit such information in the request content rather than within
   the target URI.<a href="#section-9.3.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="HEAD">
<section id="section-9.3.2">
          <h4 id="name-head">
<a href="#section-9.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.2. </a><a href="#name-head" class="section-name selfRef">HEAD</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-head-method-220" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-head-221" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.2-1">
   The HEAD method is identical to GET except that the server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span>
   send content in the response. HEAD is used to obtain metadata about the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> without transferring its
   representation data, often for the sake of testing hypertext links or
   finding recent modifications.<a href="#section-9.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.2-2">
   The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send the same header fields in response to a HEAD
   request as it would have sent if the request method had been GET.
   However, a server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> omit header fields for which a value is determined
   only while generating the content. For example, some servers buffer a
   dynamic response to GET until a minimum amount of data is generated so
   that they can more efficiently delimit small responses or make late
   decisions with regard to content selection. Such a response to GET might
   contain <a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Content-Length</a> and <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Vary</a> fields, for
   example, that are not generated within a HEAD response. These minor
   inconsistencies are considered preferable to generating and discarding the
   content for a HEAD request, since HEAD is usually requested for the
   sake of efficiency.<a href="#section-9.3.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.2-3">
   Although request message framing is independent of the method used,
   content received in a HEAD request has no generally defined semantics,
   cannot alter the meaning or target of the request, and might lead some
   implementations to reject the request and close the connection because of
   its potential as a request smuggling attack
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-11.2" class="relref">Section 11.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate content in a HEAD request unless it is
   made directly to an origin server that has previously indicated,
   in or out of band, that such a request has a purpose and will be adequately
   supported. An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> rely on private agreements to
   receive content, since participants in HTTP communication are often
   unaware of intermediaries along the request chain.<a href="#section-9.3.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.2-4">
   The response to a HEAD request is cacheable; a cache <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use it to
   satisfy subsequent HEAD requests unless otherwise indicated by the
   Cache-Control header field (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2" class="relref">Section 5.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).
   A HEAD response might also affect previously cached responses to GET;
   see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.3.5" class="relref">Section 4.3.5</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-9.3.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="POST">
<section id="section-9.3.3">
          <h4 id="name-post">
<a href="#section-9.3.3" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.3. </a><a href="#name-post" class="section-name selfRef">POST</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-post-method-222" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-post-223" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.3-1">
   The POST method requests that the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> process
   the representation enclosed in the request according to the resource's own
   specific semantics. For example, POST is used for the following functions
   (among others):<a href="#section-9.3.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-9.3.3-2.1">Providing a block of data, such as the fields entered into an HTML
      form, to a data-handling process;<a href="#section-9.3.3-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-9.3.3-2.2">Posting a message to a bulletin board, newsgroup, mailing list, blog,
      or similar group of articles;<a href="#section-9.3.3-2.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-9.3.3-2.3">Creating a new resource that has yet to be identified by the origin
      server; and<a href="#section-9.3.3-2.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-9.3.3-2.4">Appending data to a resource's existing representation(s).<a href="#section-9.3.3-2.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-9.3.3-3">
   An origin server indicates response semantics by choosing an appropriate
   status code depending on the result of processing the POST request;
   almost all of the status codes defined by this specification could be
   received in a response to POST (the exceptions being <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a>,
   <a href="#status.304" class="xref">304 (Not Modified)</a>, and <a href="#status.416" class="xref">416 (Range Not Satisfiable)</a>).<a href="#section-9.3.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.3-4">
   If one or more resources has been created on the origin server as a result
   of successfully processing a POST request, the origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send
   a <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> response containing a <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a>
   header field that provides an identifier for the primary resource created
   (<a href="#field.location" class="xref">Section 10.2.2</a>) and a representation that describes the
   status of the request while referring to the new resource(s).<a href="#section-9.3.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.3-5">
   Responses to POST requests are only cacheable when they include explicit
   freshness information (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.1" class="relref">Section 4.2.1</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>) and a
   <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a> header field that has the same value as
   the POST's target URI (<a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Section 8.7</a>). A cached POST response can be reused
   to satisfy a later GET or HEAD request. In contrast, a POST request cannot
   be satisfied by a cached POST response because POST is potentially unsafe;
   see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4" class="relref">Section 4</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-9.3.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.3-6">
   If the result of processing a POST would be equivalent to a representation
   of an existing resource, an origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> redirect the user agent to
   that resource by sending a <a href="#status.303" class="xref">303 (See Other)</a> response with the
   existing resource's identifier in the <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> field.
   This has the benefits of providing the user agent a resource identifier
   and transferring the representation via a method more amenable to shared
   caching, though at the cost of an extra request if the user agent does not
   already have the representation cached.<a href="#section-9.3.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="PUT">
<section id="section-9.3.4">
          <h4 id="name-put">
<a href="#section-9.3.4" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.4. </a><a href="#name-put" class="section-name selfRef">PUT</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-put-method-224" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-put-225" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.4-1">
   The PUT method requests that the state of the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>
   be created or replaced with the state defined by the representation
   enclosed in the request message content.  A successful PUT of a given
   representation would suggest that a subsequent GET on that same target
   resource will result in an equivalent representation being sent in
   a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response.  However, there is no guarantee that
   such a state change will be observable, since the target resource might be
   acted upon by other user agents in parallel, or might be subject to dynamic
   processing by the origin server, before any subsequent GET is received.
   A successful response only implies that the user agent's intent was
   achieved at the time of its processing by the origin server.<a href="#section-9.3.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-2">
   If the target resource does not have a current representation and
   the PUT successfully creates one, then the origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> inform
   the user agent by sending a <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> response.  If the
   target resource does have a current representation and that representation is
   successfully modified in accordance with the state of the enclosed
   representation, then the origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send either a
   <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> or a <a href="#status.204" class="xref">204 (No Content)</a> response to
   indicate successful completion of the request.<a href="#section-9.3.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-3">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> verify that the PUT representation is consistent
   with its configured constraints for the target resource. For example, if
   an origin server determines a resource's representation metadata based on
   the URI, then the origin server needs to ensure that the content received
   in a successful PUT request is consistent with that metadata. When a PUT
   representation is inconsistent with the target resource, the origin
   server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> either make them consistent, by transforming the
   representation or changing the resource configuration, or respond
   with an appropriate error message containing sufficient information
   to explain why the representation is unsuitable.  The
   <a href="#status.409" class="xref">409 (Conflict)</a> or <a href="#status.415" class="xref">415 (Unsupported Media Type)</a>
   status codes are suggested, with the latter being specific to constraints on
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> values.<a href="#section-9.3.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-4">
   For example, if the target resource is configured to always have a
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> of "text/html" and the representation being PUT
   has a Content-Type of "image/jpeg", the origin server ought to do one of:<a href="#section-9.3.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="a" class="normal type-a" id="section-9.3.4-5">
                  <li id="section-9.3.4-5.1">reconfigure the target resource to reflect the new media type;<a href="#section-9.3.4-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-9.3.4-5.2">transform the PUT representation to a format consistent with that
       of the resource before saving it as the new resource state; or,<a href="#section-9.3.4-5.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-9.3.4-5.3">reject the request with a <a href="#status.415" class="xref">415 (Unsupported Media Type)</a>
       response indicating that the target resource is limited to "text/html",
       perhaps including a link to a different resource that would be a
       suitable target for the new representation.<a href="#section-9.3.4-5.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ol>
<p id="section-9.3.4-6">
   HTTP does not define exactly how a PUT method affects the state
   of an origin server beyond what can be expressed by the intent of
   the user agent request and the semantics of the origin server response.
   It does not define what a resource might be, in any sense of that
   word, beyond the interface provided via HTTP.  It does not define
   how resource state is "stored", nor how such storage might change
   as a result of a change in resource state, nor how the origin server
   translates resource state into representations.  Generally speaking,
   all implementation details behind the resource interface are
   intentionally hidden by the server.<a href="#section-9.3.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-7">
   This extends to how header and trailer fields are stored; while common
   header fields like <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> will typically be stored
   and returned upon subsequent GET requests, header and trailer field
   handling is specific to the resource that received the request. As a result,
   an origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> ignore unrecognized header and trailer fields
   received in a PUT request (i.e., not save them as part of the resource
   state).<a href="#section-9.3.4-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-8">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a validator field
   (<a href="#response.validator" class="xref">Section 8.8</a>), such as an <a href="#field.etag" class="xref">ETag</a> or
   <a href="#field.last-modified" class="xref">Last-Modified</a> field, in a successful response to PUT unless
   the request's representation data was saved without any transformation
   applied to the content (i.e., the resource's new representation data is
   identical to the content received in the PUT request) and the
   validator field value reflects the new representation.
   This requirement allows a user agent to know when the representation it
   sent (and retains in memory) is the result of the PUT, and thus it doesn't
   need to be retrieved again from the origin server. The new validator(s)
   received in the response can be used for future conditional requests in
   order to prevent accidental overwrites (<a href="#preconditions" class="xref">Section 13.1</a>).<a href="#section-9.3.4-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-9">
   The fundamental difference between the POST and PUT methods is
   highlighted by the different intent for the enclosed representation.
   The target resource in a POST request is intended to handle the
   enclosed representation according to the resource's own semantics,
   whereas the enclosed representation in a PUT request is defined as
   replacing the state of the target resource. Hence, the intent of PUT is
   idempotent and visible to intermediaries, even though the exact effect is
   only known by the origin server.<a href="#section-9.3.4-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-10">
   Proper interpretation of a PUT request presumes that the user agent knows
   which target resource is desired. A service that selects a proper URI on
   behalf of the client, after receiving a state-changing request, <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> be
   implemented using the POST method rather than PUT. If the origin server
   will not make the requested PUT state change to the target resource and
   instead wishes to have it applied to a different resource, such as when the
   resource has been moved to a different URI, then the origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
   send an appropriate <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a> response; the
   user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> then make its own decision regarding whether or not to
   redirect the request.<a href="#section-9.3.4-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-11">
   A PUT request applied to the target resource can have side effects
   on other resources.  For example, an article might have a URI for
   identifying "the current version" (a resource) that is separate
   from the URIs identifying each particular version (different
   resources that at one point shared the same state as the current version
   resource).  A successful PUT request on "the current version" URI might
   therefore create a new version resource in addition to changing the
   state of the target resource, and might also cause links to be added
   between the related resources.<a href="#section-9.3.4-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-12">
   Some origin servers support use of the <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header
   field (<a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Section 14.4</a>) as a request modifier to
   perform a partial PUT, as described in <a href="#partial.PUT" class="xref">Section 14.5</a>.<a href="#section-9.3.4-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.4-13">
   Responses to the PUT method are not cacheable. If a successful PUT request
   passes through a cache that has one or more stored responses for the
   target URI, those stored responses will be invalidated
   (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.4" class="relref">Section 4.4</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-9.3.4-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="DELETE">
<section id="section-9.3.5">
          <h4 id="name-delete">
<a href="#section-9.3.5" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.5. </a><a href="#name-delete" class="section-name selfRef">DELETE</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-delete-method-226" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-delete-227" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.5-1">
   The DELETE method requests that the origin server remove the association
   between the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> and its current functionality.
   In effect, this method is similar to the "rm" command in UNIX: it expresses a
   deletion operation on the URI mapping of the origin server rather than an
   expectation that the previously associated information be deleted.<a href="#section-9.3.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.5-2">
   If the target resource has one or more current representations, they might
   or might not be destroyed by the origin server, and the associated storage
   might or might not be reclaimed, depending entirely on the nature of the
   resource and its implementation by the origin server (which are beyond the
   scope of this specification). Likewise, other implementation aspects of a
   resource might need to be deactivated or archived as a result of a DELETE,
   such as database or gateway connections. In general, it is assumed that the
   origin server will only allow DELETE on resources for which it has a
   prescribed mechanism for accomplishing the deletion.<a href="#section-9.3.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.5-3">
   Relatively few resources allow the DELETE method -- its primary use
   is for remote authoring environments, where the user has some direction
   regarding its effect. For example, a resource that was previously created
   using a PUT request, or identified via the Location header field after a
   <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> response to a POST request, might allow a
   corresponding DELETE request to undo those actions.  Similarly, custom
   user agent implementations that implement an authoring function, such as
   revision control clients using HTTP for remote operations, might use
   DELETE based on an assumption that the server's URI space has been crafted
   to correspond to a version repository.<a href="#section-9.3.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.5-4">
   If a DELETE method is successfully applied, the origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send<a href="#section-9.3.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-9.3.5-5.1">a <a href="#status.202" class="xref">202 (Accepted)</a> status code if the action will likely succeed but
   has not yet been enacted,<a href="#section-9.3.5-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-9.3.5-5.2">a <a href="#status.204" class="xref">204 (No Content)</a> status code if the action has been
   enacted and no further information is to be supplied, or<a href="#section-9.3.5-5.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-9.3.5-5.3">a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> status code if the action has been enacted and
   the response message includes a representation describing the status.<a href="#section-9.3.5-5.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-9.3.5-6">
   Although request message framing is independent of the method used,
   content received in a DELETE request has no generally defined semantics,
   cannot alter the meaning or target of the request, and might lead some
   implementations to reject the request and close the connection because of
   its potential as a request smuggling attack
   (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-11.2" class="relref">Section 11.2</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate content in a DELETE request unless it is
   made directly to an origin server that has previously indicated,
   in or out of band, that such a request has a purpose and will be adequately
   supported. An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> rely on private agreements to
   receive content, since participants in HTTP communication are often
   unaware of intermediaries along the request chain.<a href="#section-9.3.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.5-7">
   Responses to the DELETE method are not cacheable. If a successful DELETE
   request passes through a cache that has one or more stored responses for
   the target URI, those stored responses will be invalidated (see
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.4" class="relref">Section 4.4</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-9.3.5-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="CONNECT">
<section id="section-9.3.6">
          <h4 id="name-connect">
<a href="#section-9.3.6" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.6. </a><a href="#name-connect" class="section-name selfRef">CONNECT</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-connect-method-228" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-connect-229" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.6-1">
   The CONNECT method requests that the recipient establish a tunnel to the
   destination origin server identified by the request target and, if
   successful, thereafter restrict its behavior to blind forwarding of
   data, in both directions, until the tunnel is closed.
   Tunnels are commonly used to create an end-to-end virtual connection,
   through one or more proxies, which can then be secured using TLS
   (Transport Layer Security, <span>[<a href="#TLS13" class="xref">TLS13</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-9.3.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-2">
   CONNECT uses a special form of request target, unique to this method,
   consisting of only the host and port number of the tunnel destination,
   separated by a colon. There is no default port; a client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send the
   port number even if the CONNECT request is based on a URI reference that
   contains an authority component with an elided port
   (<a href="#uri.references" class="xref">Section 4.1</a>). For example,<a href="#section-9.3.6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-9.3.6-3">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">CONNECT server.example.com:80 HTTP/1.1
Host: server.example.com

</pre><a href="#section-9.3.6-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-9.3.6-4">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> reject a CONNECT request that targets an empty or invalid
   port number, typically by responding with a 400 (Bad Request) status code.<a href="#section-9.3.6-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-5">
   Because CONNECT changes the request/response nature of an HTTP connection,
   specific HTTP versions might have different ways of mapping its semantics
   into the protocol's wire format.<a href="#section-9.3.6-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-6">
   CONNECT is intended for use in requests to a proxy.
   The recipient can establish a tunnel either by directly connecting to
   the server identified by the request target or, if configured to use
   another proxy, by forwarding the CONNECT request to the next inbound proxy.
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> accept a CONNECT request, but most origin servers
   do not implement CONNECT.<a href="#section-9.3.6-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-7">
   Any <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a> response indicates
   that the sender (and all inbound proxies) will switch to tunnel mode
   immediately after the response header section; data received after that
   header section is from the server identified by the request target.
   Any response other than a successful response indicates that the tunnel
   has not yet been formed.<a href="#section-9.3.6-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-8">
   A tunnel is closed when a tunnel intermediary detects that either side
   has closed its connection: the intermediary <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> attempt to send any
   outstanding data that came from the closed side to the other side, close
   both connections, and then discard any remaining data left undelivered.<a href="#section-9.3.6-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-9">
   Proxy authentication might be used to establish the
   authority to create a tunnel.  For example,<a href="#section-9.3.6-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-9.3.6-10">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">CONNECT server.example.com:443 HTTP/1.1
Host: server.example.com:443
Proxy-Authorization: basic aGVsbG86d29ybGQ=

</pre><a href="#section-9.3.6-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-9.3.6-11">
   There are significant risks in establishing a tunnel to arbitrary servers,
   particularly when the destination is a well-known or reserved TCP port that
   is not intended for Web traffic. For example, a CONNECT to
   "example.com:25" would suggest that the proxy connect to the reserved
   port for SMTP traffic; if allowed, that could trick the proxy into
   relaying spam email. Proxies that support CONNECT <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> restrict its
   use to a limited set of known ports or a configurable list of safe
   request targets.<a href="#section-9.3.6-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-12">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send any Transfer-Encoding or
   <a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Content-Length</a> header fields in a
   <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a> response to CONNECT.
   A client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> ignore any Content-Length or Transfer-Encoding header
   fields received in a successful response to CONNECT.<a href="#section-9.3.6-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-13">
   A CONNECT request message does not have content. The interpretation of
   data sent after the header section of the CONNECT request message is
   specific to the version of HTTP in use.<a href="#section-9.3.6-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.6-14">
   Responses to the CONNECT method are not cacheable.<a href="#section-9.3.6-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="OPTIONS">
<section id="section-9.3.7">
          <h4 id="name-options">
<a href="#section-9.3.7" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.7. </a><a href="#name-options" class="section-name selfRef">OPTIONS</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-options-method-230" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-options-231" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.7-1">
   The OPTIONS method requests information about the communication options
   available for the target resource, at either the origin server or an
   intervening intermediary. This method allows a client to determine the
   options and/or requirements associated with a resource, or the capabilities
   of a server, without implying a resource action.<a href="#section-9.3.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.7-2">
   An OPTIONS request with an asterisk ("*") as the request target
   (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>) applies to the server in general rather than to a
   specific resource. Since a server's communication options typically depend
   on the resource, the "*" request is only useful as a "ping" or "no-op"
   type of method; it does nothing beyond allowing the client to test
   the capabilities of the server. For example, this can be used to test
   a proxy for HTTP/1.1 conformance (or lack thereof).<a href="#section-9.3.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.7-3">
   If the request target is not an asterisk, the OPTIONS request applies
   to the options that are available when communicating with the target
   resource.<a href="#section-9.3.7-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.7-4">
   A server generating a successful response to OPTIONS <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send any
   header that might indicate optional features implemented by the
   server and applicable to the target resource (e.g., <a href="#field.allow" class="xref">Allow</a>),
   including potential extensions not defined by this specification.
   The response content, if any, might also describe the communication options
   in a machine or human-readable representation. A standard format for such a
   representation is not defined by this specification, but might be defined by
   future extensions to HTTP.<a href="#section-9.3.7-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.7-5">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a <a href="#field.max-forwards" class="xref">Max-Forwards</a> header field in an
   OPTIONS request to target a specific recipient in the request chain (see
   <a href="#field.max-forwards" class="xref">Section 7.6.2</a>). A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a
   Max-Forwards header field while forwarding a request unless that request
   was received with a Max-Forwards field.<a href="#section-9.3.7-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.7-6">
   A client that generates an OPTIONS request containing content
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send a valid <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> header field describing
   the representation media type. Note that this specification does not define
   any use for such content.<a href="#section-9.3.7-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.7-7">
   Responses to the OPTIONS method are not cacheable.<a href="#section-9.3.7-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="TRACE">
<section id="section-9.3.8">
          <h4 id="name-trace">
<a href="#section-9.3.8" class="section-number selfRef">9.3.8. </a><a href="#name-trace" class="section-name selfRef">TRACE</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-trace-method-232" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-method-trace-233" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-9.3.8-1">
   The TRACE method requests a remote, application-level loop-back of the
   request message. The final recipient of the request <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> reflect the
   message received, excluding some fields described below, back to the client
   as the content of a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response. The "message/http"
   format (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-10.1" class="relref">Section 10.1</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) is one way to do so.
   The final recipient is either the origin server or the first server to
   receive a <a href="#field.max-forwards" class="xref">Max-Forwards</a> value of zero (0) in the request
   (<a href="#field.max-forwards" class="xref">Section 7.6.2</a>).<a href="#section-9.3.8-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.8-2">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate fields in a TRACE request containing
   sensitive data that might be disclosed by the response. For example, it
   would be foolish for a user agent to send stored user credentials
   (<a href="#authentication" class="xref">Section 11</a>) or cookies <span>[<a href="#COOKIE" class="xref">COOKIE</a>]</span> in a TRACE
   request. The final recipient of the request <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> exclude any request
   fields that are likely to contain sensitive data when that recipient
   generates the response content.<a href="#section-9.3.8-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.8-3">
   TRACE allows the client to see what is being received at the other
   end of the request chain and use that data for testing or diagnostic
   information. The value of the <a href="#field.via" class="xref">Via</a> header field (<a href="#field.via" class="xref">Section 7.6.3</a>)
   is of particular interest, since it acts as a trace of the request chain.
   Use of the <a href="#field.max-forwards" class="xref">Max-Forwards</a> header field allows the client to
   limit the length of the request chain, which is useful for testing a chain
   of proxies forwarding messages in an infinite loop.<a href="#section-9.3.8-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.8-4">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send content in a TRACE request.<a href="#section-9.3.8-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-9.3.8-5">
   Responses to the TRACE method are not cacheable.<a href="#section-9.3.8-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="context">
<section id="section-10">
      <h2 id="name-message-context">
<a href="#section-10" class="section-number selfRef">10. </a><a href="#name-message-context" class="section-name selfRef">Message Context</a>
      </h2>
<div id="request.context">
<section id="section-10.1">
        <h3 id="name-request-context-fields">
<a href="#section-10.1" class="section-number selfRef">10.1. </a><a href="#name-request-context-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Request Context Fields</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-10.1-1">
   The request header fields below provide additional information about the
   request context, including information about the user, user agent, and
   resource behind the request.<a href="#section-10.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="field.expect">
<section id="section-10.1.1">
          <h4 id="name-expect">
<a href="#section-10.1.1" class="section-number selfRef">10.1.1. </a><a href="#name-expect" class="section-name selfRef">Expect</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-expect-234" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-expect-235" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-expect-header-field-236" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-100-continue-expect-value-2" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.1.1-1">
   The "Expect" header field in a request indicates a certain set of
   behaviors (expectations) that need to be supported by the server in
   order to properly handle this request.<a href="#section-10.1.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-expect-238" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.1.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Expect =      #expectation
  expectation = token [ "=" ( token / quoted-string ) parameters ]
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.1-3">
   The Expect field value is case-insensitive.<a href="#section-10.1.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.1-4">
   The only expectation defined by this specification is "100-continue"
   (with no defined parameters).<a href="#section-10.1.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.1-5">
   A server that receives an Expect field value containing a member other than
   <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">100-continue</a>
            <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> respond with a
   <a href="#status.417" class="xref">417 (Expectation Failed)</a> status code to indicate that the
   unexpected expectation cannot be met.<a href="#section-10.1.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.1-6">
   A "100-continue" expectation informs recipients that the
   client is about to send (presumably large) content in this request
   and wishes to receive a <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> interim response if
   the method, target URI, and header fields are not sufficient to cause an immediate
   success, redirect, or error response. This allows the client to wait for an
   indication that it is worthwhile to send the content before actually
   doing so, which can improve efficiency when the data is huge or
   when the client anticipates that an error is likely (e.g., when sending a
   state-changing method, for the first time, without previously verified
   authentication credentials).<a href="#section-10.1.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.1-7">
   For example, a request that begins with<a href="#section-10.1.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.1.1-8">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">PUT /somewhere/fun HTTP/1.1
Host: origin.example.com
Content-Type: video/h264
Content-Length: 1234567890987
Expect: 100-continue

</pre><a href="#section-10.1.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.1-9">
   allows the origin server to immediately respond with an error message, such
   as <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a> or <a href="#status.405" class="xref">405 (Method Not Allowed)</a>,
   before the client starts filling the pipes with an unnecessary data
   transfer.<a href="#section-10.1.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.1-10">
   Requirements for clients:<a href="#section-10.1.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-11.1">
    A client <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a 100-continue expectation in a request that
    does not include content.<a href="#section-10.1.1-11.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-11.2">
    A client that will wait for a <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response
    before sending the request content <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send an
    <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Expect</a> header field containing a 100-continue expectation.<a href="#section-10.1.1-11.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-11.3">
    A client that sends a 100-continue expectation is not required to wait
    for any specific length of time; such a client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> proceed to send the
    content even if it has not yet received a response. Furthermore,
    since <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> responses cannot be sent through an
    HTTP/1.0 intermediary, such a client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> wait for an indefinite
    period before sending the content.<a href="#section-10.1.1-11.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-11.4">
    A client that receives a <a href="#status.417" class="xref">417 (Expectation Failed)</a> status
    code in response to a request containing a 100-continue expectation
    <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> repeat that request without a 100-continue expectation, since
    the 417 response merely indicates that the response chain does not
    support expectations (e.g., it passes through an HTTP/1.0 server).<a href="#section-10.1.1-11.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-10.1.1-12">
   Requirements for servers:<a href="#section-10.1.1-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-13.1">
    A server that receives a 100-continue expectation in an HTTP/1.0 request
    <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> ignore that expectation.<a href="#section-10.1.1-13.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-13.2">
    A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> omit sending a <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response if
    it has already received some or all of the content for the
    corresponding request, or if the framing indicates that there is no
    content.<a href="#section-10.1.1-13.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-13.3">
    A server that sends a <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
    ultimately send a final status code, once it receives and processes the
    request content, unless the connection is closed prematurely.<a href="#section-10.1.1-13.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-13.4">
    A server that responds with a final status code before reading the
    entire request content <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> indicate whether it intends to
    close the connection (e.g., see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-9.6" class="relref">Section 9.6</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) or
    continue reading the request content.<a href="#section-10.1.1-13.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-10.1.1-14">
   Upon receiving an HTTP/1.1 (or later) request that has a method, target URI,
   and complete header section that contains a 100-continue expectation and
   an indication that request content will follow, an origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
   send either:<a href="#section-10.1.1-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-15.1">an immediate response with a final status code, if that status can be
      determined by examining just the method, target URI, and header fields, or<a href="#section-10.1.1-15.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-15.2">an immediate <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response to encourage the client
      to send the request content.<a href="#section-10.1.1-15.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-10.1.1-16">
   The origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> wait for the content
   before sending the <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response.<a href="#section-10.1.1-16" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.1-17">
   Upon receiving an HTTP/1.1 (or later) request that has a method, target URI,
   and complete header section that contains a 100-continue expectation and
   indicates a request content will follow, a proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> either:<a href="#section-10.1.1-17" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-18.1">send an immediate
   response with a final status code, if that status can be determined by
   examining just the method, target URI, and header fields, or<a href="#section-10.1.1-18.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-10.1.1-18.2">forward the request toward the origin server by sending a corresponding
   request-line and header section to the next inbound server.<a href="#section-10.1.1-18.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-10.1.1-19">
   If the proxy believes (from configuration or past interaction) that the
   next inbound server only supports HTTP/1.0, the proxy <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> generate an
   immediate <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a> response to encourage the client to
   begin sending the content.<a href="#section-10.1.1-19" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.from">
<section id="section-10.1.2">
          <h4 id="name-from">
<a href="#section-10.1.2" class="section-number selfRef">10.1.2. </a><a href="#name-from" class="section-name selfRef">From</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-from-239" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-from-240" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-from-header-field-241" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.1.2-1">
   The "From" header field contains an Internet email address for a human
   user who controls the requesting user agent. The address ought to be
   machine-usable, as defined by "mailbox"
   in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322#section-3.4" class="relref">Section 3.4</a> of [<a href="#RFC5322" class="xref">RFC5322</a>]</span>:<a href="#section-10.1.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-from-242" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.1.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  From    = mailbox

  mailbox = <mailbox, see [RFC5322], Section 3.4>
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.2-3">
   An example is:<a href="#section-10.1.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.1.2-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">From: spider-admin@example.org
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.2-5">
   The From header field is rarely sent by non-robotic user agents.
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> send a From header field without explicit
   configuration by the user, since that might conflict with the user's
   privacy interests or their site's security policy.<a href="#section-10.1.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.2-6">
   A robotic user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a valid From header field so that the
   person responsible for running the robot can be contacted if problems
   occur on servers, such as if the robot is sending excessive, unwanted,
   or invalid requests.<a href="#section-10.1.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.2-7">
   A server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> use the From header field for access control or
   authentication, since its value is expected to be visible to anyone
   receiving or observing the request and is often recorded within logfiles
   and error reports without any expectation of privacy.<a href="#section-10.1.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.referer">
<section id="section-10.1.3">
          <h4 id="name-referer">
<a href="#section-10.1.3" class="section-number selfRef">10.1.3. </a><a href="#name-referer" class="section-name selfRef">Referer</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-referer-243" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-referer-244" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-referer-header-field-245" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.1.3-1">
   The "Referer" [sic] header field allows the user agent to specify a URI
   reference for the resource from which the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target URI</a> was
   obtained (i.e., the "referrer", though the field name is misspelled).
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> include the fragment and userinfo components
   of the URI reference <span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>, if any, when generating the
   Referer field value.<a href="#section-10.1.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-referer-246" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.1.3-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Referer = absolute-URI / partial-URI
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.3-3">
   The field value is either an <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">absolute-URI</a> or a
   <a href="#uri.references" class="xref">partial-URI</a>. In the latter case (<a href="#uri" class="xref">Section 4</a>),
   the referenced URI is relative to the target URI
   (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-5" class="relref">Section 5</a></span>).<a href="#section-10.1.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.3-4">
   The Referer header field allows servers to generate back-links to other
   resources for simple analytics, logging, optimized caching, etc. It also
   allows obsolete or mistyped links to be found for maintenance. Some servers
   use the Referer header field as a means of denying links from other sites
   (so-called "deep linking") or restricting cross-site request forgery (CSRF),
   but not all requests contain it.<a href="#section-10.1.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.3-5">
   Example:<a href="#section-10.1.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.1.3-6">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Referer: http://www.example.org/hypertext/Overview.html
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.3-7">
   If the target URI was obtained from a source that does not have its own
   URI (e.g., input from the user keyboard, or an entry within the user's
   bookmarks/favorites), the user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> either exclude the Referer header field
   or send it with a value of "about:blank".<a href="#section-10.1.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.3-8">
   The Referer header field value need not convey the full URI of the referring
   resource; a user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> truncate parts other than the referring origin.<a href="#section-10.1.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.3-9">
   The Referer header field has the potential to reveal information about the request
   context or browsing history of the user, which is a privacy concern if the
   referring resource's identifier reveals personal information (such as an
   account name) or a resource that is supposed to be confidential (such as
   behind a firewall or internal to a secured service). Most general-purpose
   user agents do not send the Referer header field when the referring
   resource is a local "file" or "data" URI. A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> send a
   <a href="#field.referer" class="xref">Referer</a> header field if the referring resource was accessed with
   a secure protocol and the request target has an origin differing from that
   of the referring resource, unless the referring resource explicitly allows
   Referer to be sent. A user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send a
   <a href="#field.referer" class="xref">Referer</a> header field in an unsecured HTTP request if the
   referring resource was accessed with a secure protocol.
   See <a href="#sensitive.information.in.uris" class="xref">Section 17.9</a> for additional
   security considerations.<a href="#section-10.1.3-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.3-10">
   Some intermediaries have been known to indiscriminately remove Referer
   header fields from outgoing requests. This has the unfortunate side effect
   of interfering with protection against CSRF attacks, which can be far
   more harmful to their users. Intermediaries and user agent extensions that
   wish to limit information disclosure in Referer ought to restrict their
   changes to specific edits, such as replacing internal domain names with
   pseudonyms or truncating the query and/or path components.
   An intermediary <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> modify or delete the Referer header field when
   the field value shares the same scheme and host as the target URI.<a href="#section-10.1.3-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.te">
<section id="section-10.1.4">
          <h4 id="name-te">
<a href="#section-10.1.4" class="section-number selfRef">10.1.4. </a><a href="#name-te" class="section-name selfRef">TE</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-te-247" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-te-248" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-te-header-field-249" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.1.4-1">
   The "TE" header field describes capabilities of the client with regard to
   transfer codings and trailer sections.<a href="#section-10.1.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.4-2">
   As described in <a href="#trailer.fields" class="xref">Section 6.5</a>,
   a TE field with a "trailers" member sent in a request indicates that the
   client will not discard trailer fields.<a href="#section-10.1.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.4-3">
   TE is also used within HTTP/1.1 to advise servers about which transfer
   codings the client is able to accept in a response.
   As of publication, only HTTP/1.1 uses transfer codings
   (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-7" class="relref">Section 7</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-10.1.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.4-4">
   The TE field value is a list of members, with each member (aside from
   "trailers") consisting of a transfer coding name token with an optional
   weight indicating the client's relative preference for that
   transfer coding (<a href="#quality.values" class="xref">Section 12.4.2</a>) and
   optional parameters for that transfer coding.<a href="#section-10.1.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-te-250" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-t-codings-251" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-transfer-coding-252" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-transfer-parameter-" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.1.4-5">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  TE                 = #t-codings
  t-codings          = "trailers" / ( transfer-coding [ weight ] )
  transfer-coding    = token *( OWS ";" OWS transfer-parameter )
  transfer-parameter = token BWS "=" BWS ( token / quoted-string )
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.4-6">
   A sender of TE <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> also send a "TE" connection option within the
   <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Connection</a> header field (<a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Section 7.6.1</a>)
   to inform intermediaries not to forward this field.<a href="#section-10.1.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.user-agent">
<section id="section-10.1.5">
          <h4 id="name-user-agent">
<a href="#section-10.1.5" class="section-number selfRef">10.1.5. </a><a href="#name-user-agent" class="section-name selfRef">User-Agent</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-user-agent-254" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-user-agent-25" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-user-agent-header-field-256" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.1.5-1">
   The "User-Agent" header field contains information about the user agent
   originating the request, which is often used by servers to help identify
   the scope of reported interoperability problems, to work around or tailor
   responses to avoid particular user agent limitations, and for analytics
   regarding browser or operating system use. A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send
   a User-Agent header field in each request unless specifically configured not
   to do so.<a href="#section-10.1.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-user-agent-257" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.1.5-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  User-Agent = product *( RWS ( product / comment ) )
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.5-3">
   The User-Agent field value consists of one or more product identifiers,
   each followed by zero or more comments (<a href="#comments" class="xref">Section 5.6.5</a>), which together
   identify the user agent software and its significant subproducts.
   By convention, the product identifiers are listed in decreasing order of
   their significance for identifying the user agent software. Each product
   identifier consists of a name and optional version.<a href="#section-10.1.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-product-258" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-product-version-259" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.1.5-4">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  product         = token ["/" product-version]
  product-version = token
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.5-5">
   A sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> limit generated product identifiers to what is necessary
   to identify the product; a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate advertising or other
   nonessential information within the product identifier.
   A sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate information in <a href="#field.user-agent" class="xref">product-version</a>
   that is not a version identifier (i.e., successive versions of the same
   product name ought to differ only in the product-version portion of the
   product identifier).<a href="#section-10.1.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.5-6">
   Example:<a href="#section-10.1.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.1.5-7">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">User-Agent: CERN-LineMode/2.15 libwww/2.17b3
</pre><a href="#section-10.1.5-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.1.5-8">
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate a User-Agent header field containing needlessly
   fine-grained detail and <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> limit the addition of subproducts by third
   parties. Overly long and detailed User-Agent field values increase request
   latency and the risk of a user being identified against their wishes
   ("fingerprinting").<a href="#section-10.1.5-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.1.5-9">
   Likewise, implementations are encouraged not to use the product tokens of
   other implementations in order to declare compatibility with them, as this
   circumvents the purpose of the field. If a user agent masquerades as a
   different user agent, recipients can assume that the user intentionally
   desires to see responses tailored for that identified user agent, even
   if they might not work as well for the actual user agent being used.<a href="#section-10.1.5-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="response.context">
<section id="section-10.2">
        <h3 id="name-response-context-fields">
<a href="#section-10.2" class="section-number selfRef">10.2. </a><a href="#name-response-context-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Response Context Fields</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-10.2-1">
   The response header fields below provide additional information about the
   response, beyond what is implied by the status code, including information
   about the server, about the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>, or about related
   resources.<a href="#section-10.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="field.allow">
<section id="section-10.2.1">
          <h4 id="name-allow">
<a href="#section-10.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">10.2.1. </a><a href="#name-allow" class="section-name selfRef">Allow</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-allow-260" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-allow-261" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-allow-header-field-262" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.2.1-1">
   The "Allow" header field lists the set of methods advertised as
   supported by the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>. The purpose of this field
   is strictly to inform the recipient of valid request methods associated
   with the resource.<a href="#section-10.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-allow-263" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.2.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Allow = #method
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.1-3">
   Example of use:<a href="#section-10.2.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.2.1-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Allow: GET, HEAD, PUT
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.1-5">
   The actual set of allowed methods is defined by the origin server at the
   time of each request. An origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate an Allow header field in a
   <a href="#status.405" class="xref">405 (Method Not Allowed)</a> response and <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> do so in any
   other response. An empty Allow field value indicates that the resource
   allows no methods, which might occur in a 405 response if the resource has
   been temporarily disabled by configuration.<a href="#section-10.2.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.1-6">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> modify the Allow header field -- it does not need
   to understand all of the indicated methods in order to handle them
   according to the generic message handling rules.<a href="#section-10.2.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.location">
<section id="section-10.2.2">
          <h4 id="name-location">
<a href="#section-10.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">10.2.2. </a><a href="#name-location" class="section-name selfRef">Location</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-location-264" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-location-265" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-location-header-field-266" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.2.2-1">
   The "Location" header field is used in some responses to refer to a
   specific resource in relation to the response. The type of relationship is
   defined by the combination of request method and status code semantics.<a href="#section-10.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-location-267" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.2.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Location = URI-reference
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.2-3">
   The field value consists of a single URI-reference. When it has the form
   of a relative reference (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-4.2" class="relref">Section 4.2</a></span>),
   the final value is computed by resolving it against the target
   URI (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-5" class="relref">Section 5</a></span>).<a href="#section-10.2.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.2-4">
   For <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> responses, the Location value refers to
   the primary resource created by the request.
   For <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a> responses, the Location value refers
   to the preferred target resource for automatically redirecting the request.<a href="#section-10.2.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.2-5">
   If the Location value provided in a <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a>
   response does not have a fragment component, a user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> process the
   redirection as if the value inherits the fragment component of the URI
   reference used to generate the target URI (i.e., the redirection
   inherits the original reference's fragment, if any).<a href="#section-10.2.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.2-6">
   For example, a GET request generated for the URI reference
   "http://www.example.org/~tim" might result in a
   <a href="#status.303" class="xref">303 (See Other)</a> response containing the header field:<a href="#section-10.2.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.2.2-7">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Location: /People.html#tim
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.2-8">
   which suggests that the user agent redirect to
   "http://www.example.org/People.html#tim"<a href="#section-10.2.2-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.2-9">
   Likewise, a GET request generated for the URI reference
   "http://www.example.org/index.html#larry" might result in a
   <a href="#status.301" class="xref">301 (Moved Permanently)</a> response containing the header
   field:<a href="#section-10.2.2-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.2.2-10">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Location: http://www.example.net/index.html
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.2-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.2-11">
   which suggests that the user agent redirect to
   "http://www.example.net/index.html#larry", preserving the original fragment
   identifier.<a href="#section-10.2.2-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.2-12">
   There are circumstances in which a fragment identifier in a Location
   value would not be appropriate. For example, the Location header field in a
   <a href="#status.201" class="xref">201 (Created)</a> response is supposed to provide a URI that is
   specific to the created resource.<a href="#section-10.2.2-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-10.2.2-13">
            <p id="section-10.2.2-13.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Some recipients attempt to recover from Location header fields
    that are not valid URI references. This specification does not mandate or
    define such processing, but does allow it for the sake of robustness.
    A Location field value cannot allow a list of members because the comma list separator
    is a valid data character within a URI-reference. If an invalid message is sent with multiple
    Location field lines, a recipient along the path might combine those field lines into
    one value. Recovery of a valid Location field value from that situation is difficult and not
    interoperable across implementations.<a href="#section-10.2.2-13.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<aside id="section-10.2.2-14">
            <p id="section-10.2.2-14.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> The <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a> header field
    (<a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Section 8.7</a>) differs from Location in that the
    Content-Location refers to the most specific resource corresponding to the
    enclosed representation. It is therefore possible for a response to contain
    both the Location and Content-Location header fields.<a href="#section-10.2.2-14.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.retry-after">
<section id="section-10.2.3">
          <h4 id="name-retry-after">
<a href="#section-10.2.3" class="section-number selfRef">10.2.3. </a><a href="#name-retry-after" class="section-name selfRef">Retry-After</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-retry-after-268" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-retry-after-2" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-retry-after-header-field-27" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.2.3-1">
   Servers send the "Retry-After" header field to indicate how long the user
   agent ought to wait before making a follow-up request. When sent with a
   <a href="#status.503" class="xref">503 (Service Unavailable)</a> response, Retry-After indicates
   how long the service is expected to be unavailable to the client.
   When sent with any <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a> response, Retry-After
   indicates the minimum time that the user agent is asked to wait before
   issuing the redirected request.<a href="#section-10.2.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.3-2">
   The Retry-After field value can be either an HTTP-date or a number
   of seconds to delay after receiving the response.<a href="#section-10.2.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-retry-after-271" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.2.3-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Retry-After = HTTP-date / delay-seconds
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<div id="rule.delay-seconds">
<p id="section-10.2.3-4">
  
   A delay-seconds value is a non-negative decimal integer, representing time
   in seconds.<a href="#section-10.2.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<span id="iref-grammar-delay-seconds-272" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.2.3-5">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  delay-seconds  = 1*DIGIT
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.3-6">
   Two examples of its use are<a href="#section-10.2.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.2.3-7">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Retry-After: Fri, 31 Dec 1999 23:59:59 GMT
Retry-After: 120
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.3-8">
   In the latter example, the delay is 2 minutes.<a href="#section-10.2.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.server">
<section id="section-10.2.4">
          <h4 id="name-server">
<a href="#section-10.2.4" class="section-number selfRef">10.2.4. </a><a href="#name-server" class="section-name selfRef">Server</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-server-273" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-server-274" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-server-header-field-275" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-10.2.4-1">
   The "Server" header field contains information about the
   software used by the origin server to handle the request, which is often
   used by clients to help identify the scope of reported interoperability
   problems, to work around or tailor requests to avoid particular server
   limitations, and for analytics regarding server or operating system use.
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> generate a Server header field in its responses.<a href="#section-10.2.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-server-276" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-10.2.4-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Server = product *( RWS ( product / comment ) )
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.4-3">
   The Server header field value consists of one or more product identifiers, each
   followed by zero or more comments (<a href="#comments" class="xref">Section 5.6.5</a>), which together
   identify the origin server software and its significant subproducts.
   By convention, the product identifiers are listed in decreasing order of
   their significance for identifying the origin server software. Each product
   identifier consists of a name and optional version, as defined in
   <a href="#field.user-agent" class="xref">Section 10.1.5</a>.<a href="#section-10.2.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-10.2.4-4">
   Example:<a href="#section-10.2.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-10.2.4-5">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Server: CERN/3.0 libwww/2.17
</pre><a href="#section-10.2.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-10.2.4-6">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate a Server header field containing needlessly
   fine-grained detail and <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> limit the addition of subproducts by third
   parties. Overly long and detailed Server field values increase response
   latency and potentially reveal internal implementation details that might
   make it (slightly) easier for attackers to find and exploit known security
   holes.<a href="#section-10.2.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="authentication">
<section id="section-11">
      <h2 id="name-http-authentication">
<a href="#section-11" class="section-number selfRef">11. </a><a href="#name-http-authentication" class="section-name selfRef">HTTP Authentication</a>
      </h2>
<div id="auth.scheme">
<section id="section-11.1">
        <h3 id="name-authentication-scheme">
<a href="#section-11.1" class="section-number selfRef">11.1. </a><a href="#name-authentication-scheme" class="section-name selfRef">Authentication Scheme</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-11.1-1">
   HTTP provides a general framework for access control and authentication,
   via an extensible set of challenge-response authentication schemes, which
   can be used by a server to challenge a client request and by a client to
   provide authentication information. It uses a case-insensitive
   token to identify the authentication scheme:<a href="#section-11.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-auth-scheme-277" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-11.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  auth-scheme    = token
</pre><a href="#section-11.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.1-3">
   Aside from the general framework, this document does not specify any
   authentication schemes. New and existing authentication schemes are
   specified independently and ought to be registered within the
   "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Authentication Scheme Registry".
   For example, the "basic" and "digest" authentication schemes are defined by
   <span>[<a href="#RFC7617" class="xref">RFC7617</a>]</span> and
   <span>[<a href="#RFC7616" class="xref">RFC7616</a>]</span>, respectively.<a href="#section-11.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="auth.params">
<section id="section-11.2">
        <h3 id="name-authentication-parameters">
<a href="#section-11.2" class="section-number selfRef">11.2. </a><a href="#name-authentication-parameters" class="section-name selfRef">Authentication Parameters</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-11.2-1">
   The authentication scheme is followed by additional information necessary
   for achieving authentication via that scheme as either a
   comma-separated list of parameters or a single sequence of characters
   capable of holding base64-encoded information.<a href="#section-11.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-token68-278" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-11.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  token68        = 1*( ALPHA / DIGIT /
                       "-" / "." / "_" / "~" / "+" / "/" ) *"="
</pre><a href="#section-11.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.2-3">
   The token68 syntax allows the 66 unreserved URI characters
   (<span>[<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>), plus a few others, so that it can hold a
   base64, base64url (URL and filename safe alphabet), base32, or base16 (hex)
   encoding, with or without padding, but excluding whitespace
   (<span>[<a href="#RFC4648" class="xref">RFC4648</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-11.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.2-4">
   Authentication parameters are name/value pairs, where the name token is
   matched case-insensitively
   and each parameter name <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> only occur once per challenge.<a href="#section-11.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-auth-param-279" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-11.2-5">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  auth-param     = token BWS "=" BWS ( token / quoted-string )
</pre><a href="#section-11.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.2-6">
   Parameter values can be expressed either as "token" or as "quoted-string"
   (<a href="#fields.components" class="xref">Section 5.6</a>).
   Authentication scheme definitions need to accept both notations, both for
   senders and recipients, to allow recipients to use generic parsing
   components regardless of the authentication scheme.<a href="#section-11.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.2-7">
   For backwards compatibility, authentication scheme definitions can restrict
   the format for senders to one of the two variants. This can be important
   when it is known that deployed implementations will fail when encountering
   one of the two formats.<a href="#section-11.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="challenge.and.response">
<section id="section-11.3">
        <h3 id="name-challenge-and-response">
<a href="#section-11.3" class="section-number selfRef">11.3. </a><a href="#name-challenge-and-response" class="section-name selfRef">Challenge and Response</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-11.3-1">
   A <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a> response message is used by an origin
   server to challenge the authorization of a user agent, including a
   <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a> header field containing at least one
   challenge applicable to the requested resource.<a href="#section-11.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.3-2">
   A <a href="#status.407" class="xref">407 (Proxy Authentication Required)</a> response message is
   used by a proxy to challenge the authorization of a client, including a
   <a href="#field.proxy-authenticate" class="xref">Proxy-Authenticate</a> header field containing at least one
   challenge applicable to the proxy for the requested resource.<a href="#section-11.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-challenge-280" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-11.3-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  challenge   = auth-scheme [ 1*SP ( token68 / #auth-param ) ]
</pre><a href="#section-11.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<aside id="section-11.3-4">
          <p id="section-11.3-4.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> Many clients fail to parse a challenge that contains an unknown
     scheme. A workaround for this problem is to list well-supported schemes
     (such as "basic") first.<a href="#section-11.3-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<p id="section-11.3-5">
   A user agent that wishes to authenticate itself with an origin server
   -- usually, but not necessarily, after receiving a
   <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a> -- can do so by including an
