   <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> header field with the request.<a href="#section-11.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.3-6">
   A client that wishes to authenticate itself with a proxy -- usually,
   but not necessarily, after receiving a
   <a href="#status.407" class="xref">407 (Proxy Authentication Required)</a> -- can do so by
   including a <a href="#field.proxy-authorization" class="xref">Proxy-Authorization</a> header field with the
   request.<a href="#section-11.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="credentials">
<section id="section-11.4">
        <h3 id="name-credentials">
<a href="#section-11.4" class="section-number selfRef">11.4. </a><a href="#name-credentials" class="section-name selfRef">Credentials</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-11.4-1">
   Both the <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> field value and the
   <a href="#field.proxy-authorization" class="xref">Proxy-Authorization</a> field value contain the client's
   credentials for the realm of the resource being requested, based upon a
   challenge received in a response (possibly at some point in the past).
   When creating their values, the user agent ought to do so by selecting the
   challenge with what it considers to be the most secure auth-scheme that it
   understands, obtaining credentials from the user as appropriate.
   Transmission of credentials within header field values implies significant
   security considerations regarding the confidentiality of the underlying
   connection, as described in
   <a href="#confidentiality.of.credentials" class="xref">Section 17.16.1</a>.<a href="#section-11.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-credentials-281" class="iref"></span>
        <div id="section-11.4-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  credentials = auth-scheme [ 1*SP ( token68 / #auth-param ) ]
</pre><a href="#section-11.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.4-3">
   Upon receipt of a request for a protected resource that omits credentials,
   contains invalid credentials (e.g., a bad password) or partial credentials
   (e.g., when the authentication scheme requires more than one round trip),
   an origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a> response
   that contains a <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a> header field with at least
   one (possibly new) challenge applicable to the requested resource.<a href="#section-11.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.4-4">
   Likewise, upon receipt of a request that omits proxy credentials or
   contains invalid or partial proxy credentials, a proxy that requires
   authentication <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a
   <a href="#status.407" class="xref">407 (Proxy Authentication Required)</a> response that contains
   a <a href="#field.proxy-authenticate" class="xref">Proxy-Authenticate</a> header field with at least one
   (possibly new) challenge applicable to the proxy.<a href="#section-11.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.4-5">
   A server that receives valid credentials that are not adequate to gain
   access ought to respond with the <a href="#status.403" class="xref">403 (Forbidden)</a> status
   code (<a href="#status.403" class="xref">Section 15.5.4</a>).<a href="#section-11.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.4-6">
   HTTP does not restrict applications to this simple challenge-response
   framework for access authentication. Additional mechanisms can be used,
   such as authentication at the transport level or via message encapsulation,
   and with additional header fields specifying authentication information.
   However, such additional mechanisms are not defined by this specification.<a href="#section-11.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.4-7">
   Note that various custom mechanisms for user authentication use the
   Set-Cookie and Cookie header fields, defined in <span>[<a href="#COOKIE" class="xref">COOKIE</a>]</span>,
   for passing tokens related to authentication.<a href="#section-11.4-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="protection.space">
<section id="section-11.5">
        <h3 id="name-establishing-a-protection-s">
<a href="#section-11.5" class="section-number selfRef">11.5. </a><a href="#name-establishing-a-protection-s" class="section-name selfRef">Establishing a Protection Space (Realm)</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-protection-space-282" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-realm-283" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-origin-284" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-11.5-1">
   The "realm" authentication parameter is reserved for use by
   authentication schemes that wish to indicate a scope of protection.<a href="#section-11.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.5-2">
   A "protection space" is defined by the origin (see
   <a href="#origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.1</a>) of the
   server being accessed, in combination with the realm value if present.
   These realms allow the protected resources on a server to be
   partitioned into a set of protection spaces, each with its own
   authentication scheme and/or authorization database. The realm value
   is a string, generally assigned by the origin server, that can have
   additional semantics specific to the authentication scheme. Note that a
   response can have multiple challenges with the same auth-scheme but
   with different realms.<a href="#section-11.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.5-3">
   The protection space determines the domain over which credentials can
   be automatically applied. If a prior request has been authorized, the
   user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> reuse the same credentials for all other requests within
   that protection space for a period of time determined by the authentication
   scheme, parameters, and/or user preferences (such as a configurable
   inactivity timeout).<a href="#section-11.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.5-4">
   The extent of a protection space, and therefore the requests to which
   credentials might be automatically applied, is not necessarily known to
   clients without additional information. An authentication scheme might
   define parameters that describe the extent of a protection space. Unless
   specifically allowed by the authentication scheme, a single protection
   space cannot extend outside the scope of its server.<a href="#section-11.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.5-5">
   For historical reasons, a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> only generate the quoted-string syntax.
   Recipients might have to support both token and quoted-string syntax for
   maximum interoperability with existing clients that have been accepting both
   notations for a long time.<a href="#section-11.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="auth.user.origin.server">
<section id="section-11.6">
        <h3 id="name-authenticating-users-to-ori">
<a href="#section-11.6" class="section-number selfRef">11.6. </a><a href="#name-authenticating-users-to-ori" class="section-name selfRef">Authenticating Users to Origin Servers</a>
        </h3>
<div id="field.www-authenticate">
<section id="section-11.6.1">
          <h4 id="name-www-authenticate">
<a href="#section-11.6.1" class="section-number selfRef">11.6.1. </a><a href="#name-www-authenticate" class="section-name selfRef">WWW-Authenticate</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-www-authenticate-285" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-www-authentic" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-www-authenticate-header-fie" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-11.6.1-1">
   The "WWW-Authenticate" response header field indicates the authentication
   scheme(s) and parameters applicable to the target resource.<a href="#section-11.6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-www-authenticate-28" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-11.6.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  WWW-Authenticate = #challenge
</pre><a href="#section-11.6.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.6.1-3">
   A server generating a <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a> response
   <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send a WWW-Authenticate header field containing at least one
   challenge.  A server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> generate a WWW-Authenticate header field
   in other response messages to indicate that supplying credentials
   (or different credentials) might affect the response.<a href="#section-11.6.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.1-4">
   A proxy forwarding a response <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> modify any
   <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a> header fields in that response.<a href="#section-11.6.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.1-5">
   User agents are advised to take special care in parsing the field value, as
   it might contain more than one challenge, and each challenge can contain a
   comma-separated list of authentication parameters. Furthermore, the header
   field itself can occur multiple times.<a href="#section-11.6.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.1-6">
  For instance:<a href="#section-11.6.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-11.6.1-7">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm="simple", Newauth realm="apps",
                 type=1, title="Login to \"apps\""
</pre><a href="#section-11.6.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.6.1-8">
  This header field contains two challenges, one for the "Basic" scheme with
  a realm value of "simple" and another for the "Newauth" scheme with a
  realm value of "apps". It also contains two additional parameters, "type" and "title".<a href="#section-11.6.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.1-9">
  Some user agents do not recognize this form, however. As a result, sending
  a WWW-Authenticate field value with more than one member on the same field
  line might not be interoperable.<a href="#section-11.6.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-11.6.1-10">
            <p id="section-11.6.1-10.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> The challenge grammar production uses the list syntax as
    well. Therefore, a sequence of comma, whitespace, and comma can be
    considered either as applying to the preceding challenge, or to be an
    empty entry in the list of challenges. In practice, this ambiguity
    does not affect the semantics of the header field value and thus is
    harmless.<a href="#section-11.6.1-10.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.authorization">
<section id="section-11.6.2">
          <h4 id="name-authorization">
<a href="#section-11.6.2" class="section-number selfRef">11.6.2. </a><a href="#name-authorization" class="section-name selfRef">Authorization</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-authorization-289" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-authorization" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-authorization-header-field-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-11.6.2-1">
   The "Authorization" header field allows a user agent to authenticate itself
   with an origin server -- usually, but not necessarily, after receiving
   a <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a> response. Its value consists of
   credentials containing the authentication information of the user agent for
   the realm of the resource being requested.<a href="#section-11.6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-authorization-292" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-11.6.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Authorization = credentials
</pre><a href="#section-11.6.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.6.2-3">
   If a request is authenticated and a realm specified, the same credentials
   are presumed to be valid for all other requests within this realm (assuming
   that the authentication scheme itself does not require otherwise, such as
   credentials that vary according to a challenge value or using synchronized
   clocks).<a href="#section-11.6.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.2-4">
   A proxy forwarding a request <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> modify any
   <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> header fields in that request.
   See <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-3.5" class="relref">Section 3.5</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span> for details of and requirements
   pertaining to handling of the Authorization header field by HTTP caches.<a href="#section-11.6.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.authentication-info">
<section id="section-11.6.3">
          <h4 id="name-authentication-info">
<a href="#section-11.6.3" class="section-number selfRef">11.6.3. </a><a href="#name-authentication-info" class="section-name selfRef">Authentication-Info</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-authentication-info-" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-authenticatio" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-authentication-info-header-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-11.6.3-1">
   HTTP authentication schemes can use the "Authentication-Info" response
   field to communicate information after the client's authentication credentials have been accepted.
   This information can include a finalization message from the server (e.g., it can contain the
   server authentication).<a href="#section-11.6.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.3-2">
   The field value is a list of parameters (name/value pairs), using the "auth-param"
   syntax defined in <a href="#challenge.and.response" class="xref">Section 11.3</a>.
   This specification only describes the generic format; authentication schemes
   using Authentication-Info will define the individual parameters. The "Digest"
   Authentication Scheme, for instance, defines multiple parameters in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7616#section-3.5" class="relref">Section 3.5</a> of [<a href="#RFC7616" class="xref">RFC7616</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-11.6.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-authentication-info" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-11.6.3-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Authentication-Info = #auth-param
</pre><a href="#section-11.6.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.6.3-4">
   The Authentication-Info field can be used in any HTTP response,
   independently of request method and status code. Its semantics are defined
   by the authentication scheme indicated by the <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Section 11.6.2</a>) of the corresponding request.<a href="#section-11.6.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.3-5">
   A proxy forwarding a response is not allowed to modify the field value in any
   way.<a href="#section-11.6.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.6.3-6">
   Authentication-Info can be sent as a trailer field
   (<a href="#trailer.fields" class="xref">Section 6.5</a>)
   when the authentication scheme explicitly allows this.<a href="#section-11.6.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="auth.client.proxy">
<section id="section-11.7">
        <h3 id="name-authenticating-clients-to-p">
<a href="#section-11.7" class="section-number selfRef">11.7. </a><a href="#name-authenticating-clients-to-p" class="section-name selfRef">Authenticating Clients to Proxies</a>
        </h3>
<div id="field.proxy-authenticate">
<section id="section-11.7.1">
          <h4 id="name-proxy-authenticate">
<a href="#section-11.7.1" class="section-number selfRef">11.7.1. </a><a href="#name-proxy-authenticate" class="section-name selfRef">Proxy-Authenticate</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-proxy-authenticate-2" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-proxy-authent" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-proxy-authenticate-header-f" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-11.7.1-1">
   The "Proxy-Authenticate" header field consists of at least one
   challenge that indicates the authentication scheme(s) and parameters
   applicable to the proxy for this request.
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send at least one Proxy-Authenticate header field in
   each <a href="#status.407" class="xref">407 (Proxy Authentication Required)</a> response that it
   generates.<a href="#section-11.7.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-proxy-authenticate-" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-11.7.1-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Proxy-Authenticate = #challenge
</pre><a href="#section-11.7.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.7.1-3">
   Unlike <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a>, the Proxy-Authenticate header field
   applies only to the next outbound client on the response chain.
   This is because only the client that chose a given proxy is likely to have
   the credentials necessary for authentication.  However, when multiple
   proxies are used within the same administrative domain, such as office and
   regional caching proxies within a large corporate network, it is common
   for credentials to be generated by the user agent and passed through the
   hierarchy until consumed.  Hence, in such a configuration, it will appear
   as if Proxy-Authenticate is being forwarded because each proxy will send
   the same challenge set.<a href="#section-11.7.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.7.1-4">
   Note that the parsing considerations for <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a>
   apply to this header field as well; see <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">Section 11.6.1</a>
   for details.<a href="#section-11.7.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.proxy-authorization">
<section id="section-11.7.2">
          <h4 id="name-proxy-authorization">
<a href="#section-11.7.2" class="section-number selfRef">11.7.2. </a><a href="#name-proxy-authorization" class="section-name selfRef">Proxy-Authorization</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-proxy-authorization-" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-proxy-authori" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-proxy-authorization-header-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-11.7.2-1">
   The "Proxy-Authorization" header field allows the client to
   identify itself (or its user) to a proxy that requires
   authentication. Its value consists of credentials containing the
   authentication information of the client for the proxy and/or realm of the
   resource being requested.<a href="#section-11.7.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-proxy-authorization" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-11.7.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Proxy-Authorization = credentials
</pre><a href="#section-11.7.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.7.2-3">
   Unlike <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a>, the Proxy-Authorization header field
   applies only to the next inbound proxy that demanded authentication using
   the <a href="#field.proxy-authenticate" class="xref">Proxy-Authenticate</a> header field. When multiple proxies are used
   in a chain, the Proxy-Authorization header field is consumed by the first
   inbound proxy that was expecting to receive credentials. A proxy <span class="bcp14">MAY</span>
   relay the credentials from the client request to the next proxy if that is
   the mechanism by which the proxies cooperatively authenticate a given
   request.<a href="#section-11.7.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.proxy-authentication-info">
<section id="section-11.7.3">
          <h4 id="name-proxy-authentication-info">
<a href="#section-11.7.3" class="section-number selfRef">11.7.3. </a><a href="#name-proxy-authentication-info" class="section-name selfRef">Proxy-Authentication-Info</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-proxy-authentication" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-proxy-authenti" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-proxy-authentication-info-h" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-11.7.3-1">
   The "Proxy-Authentication-Info" response header field is equivalent to
   <a href="#field.authentication-info" class="xref">Authentication-Info</a>, except that it applies to proxy authentication (<a href="#challenge.and.response" class="xref">Section 11.3</a>)
   and its semantics are defined by the
   authentication scheme indicated by the Proxy-Authorization header field
   (<a href="#field.proxy-authorization" class="xref">Section 11.7.2</a>)
   of the corresponding request:<a href="#section-11.7.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-proxy-authenticatio" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-11.7.3-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Proxy-Authentication-Info = #auth-param
</pre><a href="#section-11.7.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-11.7.3-3">
   However, unlike <a href="#field.authentication-info" class="xref">Authentication-Info</a>, the Proxy-Authentication-Info header
   field applies only to the next outbound client on the response chain. This is
   because only the client that chose a given proxy is likely to have the
   credentials necessary for authentication. However, when multiple proxies are
   used within the same administrative domain, such as office and regional
   caching proxies within a large corporate network, it is common for
   credentials to be generated by the user agent and passed through the
   hierarchy until consumed. Hence, in such a configuration, it will appear as
   if Proxy-Authentication-Info is being forwarded because each proxy will send
   the same field value.<a href="#section-11.7.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-11.7.3-4">
   Proxy-Authentication-Info can be sent as a trailer field
   (<a href="#trailer.fields" class="xref">Section 6.5</a>)
   when the authentication scheme explicitly allows this.<a href="#section-11.7.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="content.negotiation">
<section id="section-12">
      <h2 id="name-content-negotiation">
<a href="#section-12" class="section-number selfRef">12. </a><a href="#name-content-negotiation" class="section-name selfRef">Content Negotiation</a>
      </h2>
<p id="section-12-1">
   When responses convey content, whether indicating a success or
   an error, the origin server often has different ways of representing that
   information; for example, in different formats, languages, or encodings.
   Likewise, different users or user agents might have differing capabilities,
   characteristics, or preferences that could influence which representation,
   among those available, would be best to deliver. For this reason, HTTP
   provides mechanisms for <a href="#content.negotiation" class="xref">content negotiation</a>.<a href="#section-12-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12-2">
   This specification defines three patterns of content negotiation that can
   be made visible within the protocol:
   "proactive" negotiation, where the server selects the representation based
   upon the user agent's stated preferences; "reactive" negotiation,
   where the server provides a list of representations for the user agent to
   choose from; and "request content" negotiation, where the user agent
   selects the representation for a future request based upon the server's
   stated preferences in past responses.<a href="#section-12-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12-3">
   Other patterns of content negotiation include
   "conditional content", where the representation consists of multiple
   parts that are selectively rendered based on user agent parameters,
   "active content", where the representation contains a script that
   makes additional (more specific) requests based on the user agent
   characteristics, and "Transparent Content Negotiation"
   (<span>[<a href="#RFC2295" class="xref">RFC2295</a>]</span>), where content selection is performed by
   an intermediary. These patterns are not mutually exclusive, and each has
   trade-offs in applicability and practicality.<a href="#section-12-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12-4">
   Note that, in all cases, HTTP is not aware of the resource semantics.
   The consistency with which an origin server responds to requests, over time
   and over the varying dimensions of content negotiation, and thus the
   "sameness" of a resource's observed representations over time, is
   determined entirely by whatever entity or algorithm selects or generates
   those responses.<a href="#section-12-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="proactive.negotiation">
<section id="section-12.1">
        <h3 id="name-proactive-negotiation">
<a href="#section-12.1" class="section-number selfRef">12.1. </a><a href="#name-proactive-negotiation" class="section-name selfRef">Proactive Negotiation</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-12.1-1">
   When content negotiation preferences are sent by the user agent in a
   request to encourage an algorithm located at the server to
   select the preferred representation, it is called
   "proactive negotiation"
   (a.k.a., "server-driven negotiation"). Selection is based on
   the available representations for a response (the dimensions over which it
   might vary, such as language, content coding, etc.) compared to various
   information supplied in the request, including both the explicit
   negotiation header fields below and implicit
   characteristics, such as the client's network address or parts of the
   <a href="#field.user-agent" class="xref">User-Agent</a> field.<a href="#section-12.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.1-2">
   Proactive negotiation is advantageous when the algorithm for
   selecting from among the available representations is difficult to
   describe to a user agent, or when the server desires to send its
   "best guess" to the user agent along with the first response (when that
   "best guess" is good enough for the user, this avoids the round-trip
   delay of a subsequent request). In order to improve the server's
   guess, a user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send request header fields that describe
   its preferences.<a href="#section-12.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.1-3">
   Proactive negotiation has serious disadvantages:<a href="#section-12.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-12.1-4.1">
    It is impossible for the server to accurately determine what
    might be "best" for any given user, since that would require
    complete knowledge of both the capabilities of the user agent
    and the intended use for the response (e.g., does the user want
    to view it on screen or print it on paper?);<a href="#section-12.1-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li class="normal" id="section-12.1-4.2">
    Having the user agent describe its capabilities in every
    request can be both very inefficient (given that only a small
    percentage of responses have multiple representations) and a
    potential risk to the user's privacy;<a href="#section-12.1-4.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li class="normal" id="section-12.1-4.3">
    It complicates the implementation of an origin server and the
    algorithms for generating responses to a request; and,<a href="#section-12.1-4.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li class="normal" id="section-12.1-4.4">
    It limits the reusability of responses for shared caching.<a href="#section-12.1-4.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        </ul>
<p id="section-12.1-5">
   A user agent cannot rely on proactive negotiation preferences being
   consistently honored, since the origin server might not implement proactive
   negotiation for the requested resource or might decide that sending a
   response that doesn't conform to the user agent's preferences is better
   than sending a <a href="#status.406" class="xref">406 (Not Acceptable)</a> response.<a href="#section-12.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.1-6">
   A <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Vary</a> header field (<a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Section 12.5.5</a>) is
   often sent in a response subject to proactive negotiation to indicate what
   parts of the request information were used in the selection algorithm.<a href="#section-12.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.1-7">
   The request header fields <a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Accept</a>,
   <a href="#field.accept-charset" class="xref">Accept-Charset</a>, <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a>, and
   <a href="#field.accept-language" class="xref">Accept-Language</a> are defined below for a user agent to engage
   in <a href="#proactive.negotiation" class="xref">proactive negotiation</a> of the response content.
   The preferences sent in these
   fields apply to any content in the response, including representations of
   the target resource, representations of error or processing status, and
   potentially even the miscellaneous text strings that might appear within
   the protocol.<a href="#section-12.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="reactive.negotiation">
<section id="section-12.2">
        <h3 id="name-reactive-negotiation">
<a href="#section-12.2" class="section-number selfRef">12.2. </a><a href="#name-reactive-negotiation" class="section-name selfRef">Reactive Negotiation</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-12.2-1">
   With "reactive negotiation" (a.k.a., "agent-driven negotiation"), selection of
   content (regardless of the status code) is performed by
   the user agent after receiving an initial response. The mechanism for
   reactive negotiation might be as simple as a list of references to
   alternative representations.<a href="#section-12.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.2-2">
   If the user agent is not satisfied by the initial response content,
   it can perform a GET request on one or more of the alternative resources
   to obtain a different representation. Selection of such alternatives might
   be performed automatically (by the user agent) or manually (e.g., by the
   user selecting from a hypertext menu).<a href="#section-12.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.2-3">
   A server might choose not to send an initial representation, other than
   the list of alternatives, and thereby indicate that reactive
   negotiation by the user agent is preferred. For example, the alternatives
   listed in responses with the <a href="#status.300" class="xref">300 (Multiple Choices)</a> and
   <a href="#status.406" class="xref">406 (Not Acceptable)</a> status codes include information about
   available representations so that the user or user agent can react by
   making a selection.<a href="#section-12.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.2-4">
   Reactive negotiation is advantageous when the response would vary
   over commonly used dimensions (such as type, language, or encoding),
   when the origin server is unable to determine a user agent's
   capabilities from examining the request, and generally when public
   caches are used to distribute server load and reduce network usage.<a href="#section-12.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.2-5">
   Reactive negotiation suffers from the disadvantages of transmitting
   a list of alternatives to the user agent, which degrades user-perceived
   latency if transmitted in the header section, and needing a second request
   to obtain an alternate representation. Furthermore, this specification
   does not define a mechanism for supporting automatic selection, though it
   does not prevent such a mechanism from being developed.<a href="#section-12.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="request.content.negotiation">
<section id="section-12.3">
        <h3 id="name-request-content-negotiation">
<a href="#section-12.3" class="section-number selfRef">12.3. </a><a href="#name-request-content-negotiation" class="section-name selfRef">Request Content Negotiation</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-12.3-1">
   When content negotiation preferences are sent in a server's response, the
   listed preferences are called "request content negotiation"
   because they intend to influence selection of an appropriate content for
   subsequent requests to that resource. For example,
   the <a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Accept</a> (<a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Section 12.5.1</a>) and
   <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a> (<a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Section 12.5.3</a>)
   header fields can be sent in a response to indicate preferred media types
   and content codings for subsequent requests to that resource.<a href="#section-12.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.3-2">
   Similarly, <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5789#section-3.1" class="relref">Section 3.1</a> of [<a href="#RFC5789" class="xref">RFC5789</a>]</span> defines
   the "Accept-Patch" response header field, which allows discovery of
   which content types are accepted in PATCH requests.<a href="#section-12.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="conneg.features">
<section id="section-12.4">
        <h3 id="name-content-negotiation-field-f">
<a href="#section-12.4" class="section-number selfRef">12.4. </a><a href="#name-content-negotiation-field-f" class="section-name selfRef">Content Negotiation Field Features</a>
        </h3>
<div id="conneg.absent">
<section id="section-12.4.1">
          <h4 id="name-absence">
<a href="#section-12.4.1" class="section-number selfRef">12.4.1. </a><a href="#name-absence" class="section-name selfRef">Absence</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-12.4.1-1">
   For each of the content negotiation fields, a request that does not contain
   the field implies that the sender has no preference on that dimension of
   negotiation.<a href="#section-12.4.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.4.1-2">
   If a content negotiation header field is present in a request and none of
   the available
   representations for the response can be considered acceptable according to
   it, the origin server can either honor the header field by sending a
   <a href="#status.406" class="xref">406 (Not Acceptable)</a> response or disregard the header field
   by treating the response as if it is not subject to content negotiation
   for that request header field. This does not imply, however, that the
   client will be able to use the representation.<a href="#section-12.4.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-12.4.1-3">
            <p id="section-12.4.1-3.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> A user agent sending these header fields makes it easier for a
   server to identify an individual by virtue of the user agent's request
   characteristics (<a href="#fingerprinting" class="xref">Section 17.13</a>).<a href="#section-12.4.1-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="quality.values">
<section id="section-12.4.2">
          <h4 id="name-quality-values">
<a href="#section-12.4.2" class="section-number selfRef">12.4.2. </a><a href="#name-quality-values" class="section-name selfRef">Quality Values</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-12.4.2-1">
   The content negotiation fields defined by this specification
   use a common parameter, named "q" (case-insensitive), to assign a relative
   "weight" to the preference for that associated kind of content.
   This weight is referred to as a "quality value" (or "qvalue") because
   the same parameter name is often used within server configurations to
   assign a weight to the relative quality of the various representations
   that can be selected for a resource.<a href="#section-12.4.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.4.2-2">
   The weight is normalized to a real number in the range 0 through 1,
   where 0.001 is the least preferred and 1 is the most preferred;
   a value of 0 means "not acceptable". If no "q" parameter is present,
   the default weight is 1.<a href="#section-12.4.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-weight-309" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-qvalue-310" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-12.4.2-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  weight = OWS ";" OWS "q=" qvalue
  qvalue = ( "0" [ "." 0*3DIGIT ] )
         / ( "1" [ "." 0*3("0") ] )
</pre><a href="#section-12.4.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.4.2-4">
   A sender of qvalue <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate more than three digits after the
   decimal point. User configuration of these values ought to be limited in
   the same fashion.<a href="#section-12.4.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="wildcard.values">
<section id="section-12.4.3">
          <h4 id="name-wildcard-values">
<a href="#section-12.4.3" class="section-number selfRef">12.4.3. </a><a href="#name-wildcard-values" class="section-name selfRef">Wildcard Values</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-12.4.3-1">
   Most of these header fields, where indicated, define a wildcard value ("*")
   to select unspecified values. If no wildcard is present, values that are
   not explicitly mentioned in the field are considered unacceptable.
   Within <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Vary</a>, the wildcard value means that the variance
   is unlimited.<a href="#section-12.4.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-12.4.3-2">
            <p id="section-12.4.3-2.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> In practice, using wildcards in content negotiation has limited
   practical value because it is seldom useful to say, for example, "I
   prefer image/* more or less than (some other specific value)". By sending Accept: */*;q=0, clients can
   explicitly request a <a href="#status.406" class="xref">406 (Not Acceptable)</a> response if a
   more preferred format is not available, but
   they still need to be able to handle a different response since the
   server is allowed to ignore their preference.<a href="#section-12.4.3-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="conneg.fields">
<section id="section-12.5">
        <h3 id="name-content-negotiation-fields">
<a href="#section-12.5" class="section-number selfRef">12.5. </a><a href="#name-content-negotiation-fields" class="section-name selfRef">Content Negotiation Fields</a>
        </h3>
<div id="field.accept">
<section id="section-12.5.1">
          <h4 id="name-accept">
<a href="#section-12.5.1" class="section-number selfRef">12.5.1. </a><a href="#name-accept" class="section-name selfRef">Accept</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-accept-311" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-accept-312" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-accept-header-field-313" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-12.5.1-1">
   The "Accept" header field can be used by user agents to specify their
   preferences regarding response media types. For example, Accept header
   fields can be used to indicate that the request is specifically limited to
   a small set of desired types, as in the case of a request for an in-line
   image.<a href="#section-12.5.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.1-2">
   When sent by a server in a response, Accept provides information
   about which content types are preferred in the content of a subsequent
   request to the same resource.<a href="#section-12.5.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-accept-314" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-media-range-315" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-12.5.1-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Accept = #( media-range [ weight ] )

  media-range    = ( "*/*"
                     / ( type "/" "*" )
                     / ( type "/" subtype )
                   ) parameters
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.1-4">
   The asterisk "*" character is used to group media types into ranges,
   with "*/*" indicating all media types and "type/*" indicating all
   subtypes of that type. The media-range can include media type
   parameters that are applicable to that range.<a href="#section-12.5.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.1-5">
   Each media-range might be followed by optional applicable media type
   parameters (e.g., <a href="#charset" class="xref">charset</a>), followed by an optional "q"
   parameter for indicating a relative weight (<a href="#quality.values" class="xref">Section 12.4.2</a>).<a href="#section-12.5.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.1-6">
   Previous specifications allowed additional extension parameters to appear
   after the weight parameter. The accept extension grammar (accept-params, accept-ext) has
   been removed because it had a complicated definition, was not being used in
   practice, and is more easily deployed through new header fields. Senders
   using weights <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send "q" last (after all media-range parameters).
   Recipients <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> process any parameter named "q" as weight, regardless of
   parameter ordering.<a href="#section-12.5.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-12.5.1-7">
            <p id="section-12.5.1-7.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Use of the "q" parameter name to control content negotiation
    would interfere with any media type parameter having the same name.
    Hence, the media type registry disallows parameters named "q".<a href="#section-12.5.1-7.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<p id="section-12.5.1-8">
   The example<a href="#section-12.5.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.1-9">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept: audio/*; q=0.2, audio/basic
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.1-10">
   is interpreted as "I prefer audio/basic, but send me any audio
   type if it is the best available after an 80% markdown in quality".<a href="#section-12.5.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.1-11">
   A more elaborate example is<a href="#section-12.5.1-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.1-12">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept: text/plain; q=0.5, text/html,
       text/x-dvi; q=0.8, text/x-c
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.1-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.1-13">
   Verbally, this would be interpreted as "text/html and text/x-c are
   the equally preferred media types, but if they do not exist, then send the
   text/x-dvi representation, and if that does not exist, send the text/plain
   representation".<a href="#section-12.5.1-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.1-14">
   Media ranges can be overridden by more specific media ranges or
   specific media types. If more than one media range applies to a given
   type, the most specific reference has precedence. For example,<a href="#section-12.5.1-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.1-15">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept: text/*, text/plain, text/plain;format=flowed, */*
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.1-15" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.1-16">
   have the following precedence:<a href="#section-12.5.1-16" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-12.5.1-17">
                  <li id="section-12.5.1-17.1">text/plain;format=flowed<a href="#section-12.5.1-17.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-12.5.1-17.2">text/plain<a href="#section-12.5.1-17.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-12.5.1-17.3">text/*<a href="#section-12.5.1-17.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-12.5.1-17.4">*/*<a href="#section-12.5.1-17.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ol>
<p id="section-12.5.1-18">
   The media type quality factor associated with a given type is
   determined by finding the media range with the highest precedence
   that matches the type. For example,<a href="#section-12.5.1-18" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.1-19">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept: text/*;q=0.3, text/plain;q=0.7, text/plain;format=flowed,
       text/plain;format=fixed;q=0.4, */*;q=0.5
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.1-19" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.1-20">
   would cause the following values to be associated:<a href="#section-12.5.1-20" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<table class="left" id="table-5">
            <caption><a href="#table-5" class="selfRef">Table 5</a></caption>
<thead>
              <tr>
                <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Media Type</th>
                <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Quality Value</th>
              </tr>
            </thead>
            <tbody>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">text/plain;format=flowed</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">1</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">text/plain</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">0.7</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">text/html</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">0.3</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">image/jpeg</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">0.5</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">text/plain;format=fixed</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">0.4</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">text/html;level=3</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">0.7</td>
              </tr>
            </tbody>
          </table>
<aside id="section-12.5.1-22">
            <p id="section-12.5.1-22.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> A user agent might be provided with a default set of quality
   values for certain media ranges. However, unless the user agent is
   a closed system that cannot interact with other rendering agents,
   this default set ought to be configurable by the user.<a href="#section-12.5.1-22.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.accept-charset">
<section id="section-12.5.2">
          <h4 id="name-accept-charset">
<a href="#section-12.5.2" class="section-number selfRef">12.5.2. </a><a href="#name-accept-charset" class="section-name selfRef">Accept-Charset</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-accept-charset-316" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-accept-charse" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-accept-charset-header-field" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-12.5.2-1">
   The "Accept-Charset" header field can be sent by a user agent to indicate
   its preferences for charsets in textual response content. For example,
   this field allows user agents capable of understanding more comprehensive
   or special-purpose charsets to signal that capability to an origin server
   that is capable of representing information in those charsets.<a href="#section-12.5.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-accept-charset-319" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-12.5.2-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Accept-Charset = #( ( token / "*" ) [ weight ] )
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.2-3">
   Charset names are defined in <a href="#charset" class="xref">Section 8.3.2</a>.
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> associate a quality value with each charset to indicate
   the user's relative preference for that charset, as defined in <a href="#quality.values" class="xref">Section 12.4.2</a>.
   An example is<a href="#section-12.5.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.2-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept-Charset: iso-8859-5, unicode-1-1;q=0.8
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.2-5">
   The special value "*", if present in the Accept-Charset header field,
   matches every charset that is not mentioned elsewhere in the
   field.<a href="#section-12.5.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-12.5.2-6">
            <p id="section-12.5.2-6.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Accept-Charset is deprecated because UTF-8 has become nearly
   ubiquitous and sending a detailed list of user-preferred charsets wastes
   bandwidth, increases latency, and makes passive fingerprinting far too
   easy (<a href="#fingerprinting" class="xref">Section 17.13</a>). Most general-purpose user agents
   do not send Accept-Charset unless specifically configured to do so.<a href="#section-12.5.2-6.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.accept-encoding">
<section id="section-12.5.3">
          <h4 id="name-accept-encoding">
<a href="#section-12.5.3" class="section-number selfRef">12.5.3. </a><a href="#name-accept-encoding" class="section-name selfRef">Accept-Encoding</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-accept-encoding-320" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-accept-encodi" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-accept-encoding-header-fiel" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-12.5.3-1">
   The "Accept-Encoding" header field can be used to indicate preferences
   regarding the use of content codings (<a href="#content.codings" class="xref">Section 8.4.1</a>).<a href="#section-12.5.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-2">
   When sent by a user agent in a request, Accept-Encoding indicates the
   content codings acceptable in a response.<a href="#section-12.5.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-3">
   When sent by a server in a response, Accept-Encoding provides information
   about which content codings are preferred in the content of a subsequent
   request to the same resource.<a href="#section-12.5.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-4">
   An "identity" token is used as a synonym for
   "no encoding" in order to communicate when no encoding is preferred.<a href="#section-12.5.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-accept-encoding-323" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-codings-324" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-12.5.3-5">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Accept-Encoding  = #( codings [ weight ] )
  codings          = content-coding / "identity" / "*"
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.3-6">
   Each codings value <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> be given an associated quality value (weight)
   representing the preference for that encoding, as defined in <a href="#quality.values" class="xref">Section 12.4.2</a>.
   The asterisk "*" symbol in an Accept-Encoding field matches any available
   content coding not explicitly listed in the field.<a href="#section-12.5.3-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-7">
   Examples:<a href="#section-12.5.3-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.3-8">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept-Encoding: compress, gzip
Accept-Encoding:
Accept-Encoding: *
Accept-Encoding: compress;q=0.5, gzip;q=1.0
Accept-Encoding: gzip;q=1.0, identity; q=0.5, *;q=0
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.3-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.3-9">
   A server tests whether a content coding for a given representation is
   acceptable using these rules:<a href="#section-12.5.3-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-12.5.3-10">
                  <li id="section-12.5.3-10.1">If no Accept-Encoding header field is in the request, any content coding is
       considered acceptable by the user agent.<a href="#section-12.5.3-10.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-12.5.3-10.2">If the representation has no content coding, then it is acceptable
       by default unless specifically excluded by the Accept-Encoding header field
       stating either "identity;q=0" or "*;q=0" without a more specific
       entry for "identity".<a href="#section-12.5.3-10.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-12.5.3-10.3">If the representation's content coding is one of the content codings
       listed in the Accept-Encoding field value, then it is acceptable unless
       it is accompanied by a qvalue of 0. (As defined in <a href="#quality.values" class="xref">Section 12.4.2</a>, a
       qvalue of 0 means "not acceptable".)<a href="#section-12.5.3-10.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ol>
<p id="section-12.5.3-11">
   A representation could be encoded with multiple content codings. However, most
   content codings are alternative ways to accomplish the same purpose
   (e.g., data compression). When selecting between multiple content codings that
   have the same purpose, the acceptable content coding with the highest
   non-zero qvalue is preferred.<a href="#section-12.5.3-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-12">
   An Accept-Encoding header field with a field value that is empty
   implies that the user agent does not want any content coding in response.
   If a non-empty Accept-Encoding header field is present in a request and none of the
   available representations for the response have a content coding that
   is listed as acceptable, the origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a response
   without any content coding unless the identity coding is indicated as unacceptable.<a href="#section-12.5.3-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-13">
   When the Accept-Encoding header field is present in a response, it indicates
   what content codings the resource was willing to accept in the associated
   request. The field value is evaluated the same way as in a request.<a href="#section-12.5.3-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-14">
   Note that this information is specific to the associated request; the set of
   supported encodings might be different for other resources on the same
   server and could change over time or depend on other aspects of the request
   (such as the request method).<a href="#section-12.5.3-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-15">
   Servers that fail a request due to an unsupported content coding ought to
   respond with a <a href="#status.415" class="xref">415 (Unsupported Media Type)</a> status and
   include an Accept-Encoding header field in that response, allowing
   clients to distinguish between issues related to content codings and media
   types. In order to avoid confusion with issues related to media types,
   servers that fail a request with a 415 status for reasons unrelated to
   content codings <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> include the Accept-Encoding header
   field.<a href="#section-12.5.3-15" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.3-16">
   The most common use of Accept-Encoding is in responses with a
   <a href="#status.415" class="xref">415 (Unsupported Media Type)</a> status code, in response to
   optimistic use of a content coding by clients. However, the header field
   can also be used to indicate to clients that content codings are supported in order
   to optimize future interactions. For example, a resource might include it
   in a <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a> response when the request content was
   big enough to justify use of a compression coding but the client failed do
   so.<a href="#section-12.5.3-16" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.accept-language">
<section id="section-12.5.4">
          <h4 id="name-accept-language">
<a href="#section-12.5.4" class="section-number selfRef">12.5.4. </a><a href="#name-accept-language" class="section-name selfRef">Accept-Language</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-accept-language-325" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-accept-langua" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-accept-language-header-fiel" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-12.5.4-1">
   The "Accept-Language" header field can be used by user agents to
   indicate the set of natural languages that are preferred in the response.
   Language tags are defined in <a href="#language.tags" class="xref">Section 8.5.1</a>.<a href="#section-12.5.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-accept-language-328" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-grammar-language-range-329" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-12.5.4-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Accept-Language = #( language-range [ weight ] )
  language-range  =
            <language-range, see [RFC4647], Section 2.1>
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.4-3">
   Each language-range can be given an associated quality value
   representing an estimate of the user's preference for the languages
   specified by that range, as defined in <a href="#quality.values" class="xref">Section 12.4.2</a>. For example,<a href="#section-12.5.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.4-4">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Accept-Language: da, en-gb;q=0.8, en;q=0.7
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.4-5">
   would mean: "I prefer Danish, but will accept British English and
   other types of English".<a href="#section-12.5.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.4-6">
   Note that some recipients treat the order in which language tags are listed
   as an indication of descending priority, particularly for tags that are
   assigned equal quality values (no value is the same as q=1). However, this
   behavior cannot be relied upon. For consistency and to maximize
   interoperability, many user agents assign each language tag a unique
   quality value while also listing them in order of decreasing quality.
   Additional discussion of language priority lists can be found in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4647#section-2.3" class="relref">Section 2.3</a> of [<a href="#RFC4647" class="xref">RFC4647</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-12.5.4-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.4-7">
   For matching, <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4647#section-3" class="relref">Section 3</a> of [<a href="#RFC4647" class="xref">RFC4647</a>]</span> defines
   several matching schemes. Implementations can offer the most appropriate
   matching scheme for their requirements. The "Basic Filtering" scheme
   (<span>[<a href="#RFC4647" class="xref">RFC4647</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4647#section-3.3.1" class="relref">Section 3.3.1</a></span>) is identical to the
   matching scheme that was previously defined for HTTP in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2616#section-14.4" class="relref">Section 14.4</a> of [<a href="#RFC2616" class="xref">RFC2616</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-12.5.4-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.4-8">
   It might be contrary to the privacy expectations of the user to send
   an Accept-Language header field with the complete linguistic preferences of
   the user in every request (<a href="#fingerprinting" class="xref">Section 17.13</a>).<a href="#section-12.5.4-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.4-9">
   Since intelligibility is highly dependent on the individual user, user
   agents need to allow user control over the linguistic preference (either
   through configuration of the user agent itself or by defaulting to a user
   controllable system setting).
   A user agent that does not provide such control to the user <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span>
   send an Accept-Language header field.<a href="#section-12.5.4-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-12.5.4-10">
            <p id="section-12.5.4-10.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> User agents ought to provide guidance to users when setting a
    preference, since users are rarely familiar with the details of language
    matching as described above. For example, users might assume that on
    selecting "en-gb", they will be served any kind of English document if
    British English is not available. A user agent might suggest, in such a
    case, to add "en" to the list for better matching behavior.<a href="#section-12.5.4-10.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.vary">
<section id="section-12.5.5">
          <h4 id="name-vary">
<a href="#section-12.5.5" class="section-number selfRef">12.5.5. </a><a href="#name-vary" class="section-name selfRef">Vary</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-vary-330" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-vary-331" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-vary-header-field-332" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-12.5.5-1">
   The "Vary" header field in a response describes what parts of a request
   message, aside from the method and target URI, might have influenced the
   origin server's process for selecting the content of this response.<a href="#section-12.5.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-vary-333" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-12.5.5-2">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  Vary = #( "*" / field-name )
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.5-3">
   A Vary field value is either the wildcard member "*" or a list of
   request field names, known as the selecting header fields, that might
   have had a role in selecting the representation for this response.
   Potential selecting header fields are not limited to fields defined by
   this specification.<a href="#section-12.5.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.5-4">
   A list containing the member "*" signals that other aspects of the
   request might have played a role in selecting the response representation,
   possibly including aspects outside the message syntax (e.g., the
   client's network address).
   A recipient will not be able to determine whether this response is
   appropriate for a later request without forwarding the request to the
   origin server. A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate "*" in a Vary field value.<a href="#section-12.5.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.5-5">
   For example, a response that contains<a href="#section-12.5.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-12.5.5-6">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">Vary: accept-encoding, accept-language
</pre><a href="#section-12.5.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-12.5.5-7">
   indicates that the origin server might have used the request's
   <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a> and <a href="#field.accept-language" class="xref">Accept-Language</a>
   header fields (or lack thereof) as determining factors while choosing
   the content for this response.<a href="#section-12.5.5-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.5-8">
   A Vary field containing a list of field names has two purposes:<a href="#section-12.5.5-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-12.5.5-9">
                  <li id="section-12.5.5-9.1">
              <p id="section-12.5.5-9.1.1">
       To inform cache recipients that they <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> use this response
       to satisfy a later request unless the later request has the
       same values for the listed header fields as the original request
       (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.1" class="relref">Section 4.1</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>) or reuse of the
       response has been validated by the origin server.
       In other words, Vary expands the cache key
       required to match a new request to the stored cache entry.<a href="#section-12.5.5-9.1.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li id="section-12.5.5-9.2">
              <p id="section-12.5.5-9.2.1">
       To inform user agent recipients that this response was subject to
       content negotiation (<a href="#content.negotiation" class="xref">Section 12</a>) and a
       different representation might be sent in a subsequent request if
       other values are provided in the listed header fields
       (<a href="#proactive.negotiation" class="xref">proactive negotiation</a>).<a href="#section-12.5.5-9.2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
          </ol>
<p id="section-12.5.5-10">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a Vary header field on a cacheable
   response when it wishes that response to be selectively reused for
   subsequent requests. Generally, that is the case when the response
   content has been tailored to better fit the preferences expressed by
   those selecting header fields, such as when an origin server has
   selected the response's language based on the request's
   <a href="#field.accept-language" class="xref">Accept-Language</a> header field.<a href="#section-12.5.5-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.5-11">
   Vary might be elided when an origin server considers variance in
   content selection to be less significant than Vary's performance impact
   on caching, particularly when reuse is already limited by cache
   response directives (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2" class="relref">Section 5.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-12.5.5-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-12.5.5-12">
   There is no need to send the Authorization field name in Vary because
   reuse of that response for a different user is prohibited by the field
   definition (<a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Section 11.6.2</a>).
   Likewise, if the response content has been selected or influenced by
   network region, but the origin server wants the cached response to be
   reused even if recipients move from one region to another, then there
   is no need for the origin server to indicate such variance in Vary.<a href="#section-12.5.5-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="conditional.requests">
<section id="section-13">
      <h2 id="name-conditional-requests">
<a href="#section-13" class="section-number selfRef">13. </a><a href="#name-conditional-requests" class="section-name selfRef">Conditional Requests</a>
      </h2>
<span id="iref-conditional-request-334" class="iref"></span>
      <p id="section-13-1">
   A conditional request is an HTTP request with one or more request header
   fields that indicate a precondition to be tested before
   applying the request method to the target resource.
   <a href="#evaluation" class="xref">Section 13.2</a> defines when to evaluate preconditions and
   their order of precedence when more than one precondition is present.<a href="#section-13-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13-2">
   Conditional GET requests are the most efficient mechanism for HTTP
   cache updates <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>.  Conditionals can also be
   applied to state-changing methods, such as PUT and DELETE, to prevent
   the "lost update" problem: one client accidentally overwriting
   the work of another client that has been acting in parallel.<a href="#section-13-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="preconditions">
<section id="section-13.1">
        <h3 id="name-preconditions">
<a href="#section-13.1" class="section-number selfRef">13.1. </a><a href="#name-preconditions" class="section-name selfRef">Preconditions</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-selected-representation-335" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-13.1-1">
   Preconditions are usually defined with respect to a state of the target
   resource as a whole (its current value set) or the state as observed in a
   previously obtained representation (one value in that set). If a resource
   has multiple current representations, each with its own observable state,
   a precondition will assume that the mapping of each request to a
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> (<a href="#representations" class="xref">Section 3.2</a>)
   is consistent over time.
   Regardless, if the mapping is inconsistent or the server is unable to
   select an appropriate representation, then no harm will result when the
   precondition evaluates to false.<a href="#section-13.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1-2">
   Each precondition defined below consists of a comparison between a
   set of validators obtained from prior representations of the target
   resource to the current state of validators for the selected
   representation (<a href="#response.validator" class="xref">Section 8.8</a>). Hence, these
   preconditions evaluate whether the state of the target resource has
   changed since a given state known by the client. The effect of such an
   evaluation depends on the method semantics and choice of conditional, as
   defined in <a href="#evaluation" class="xref">Section 13.2</a>.<a href="#section-13.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1-3">
   Other preconditions, defined by other specifications as extension fields,
   might place conditions on all recipients, on the state of the target
   resource in general, or on a group of resources. For instance, the "If"
   header field in WebDAV can make a request conditional on various aspects
   of multiple resources, such as locks, if the recipient understands and
   implements that field (<span>[<a href="#WEBDAV" class="xref">WEBDAV</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4918#section-10.4" class="relref">Section 10.4</a></span>).<a href="#section-13.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1-4">
   Extensibility of preconditions is only possible when the precondition can
   be safely ignored if unknown (like <a href="#field.if-modified-since" class="xref">If-Modified-Since</a>), when
   deployment can be assumed for a given use case, or when implementation
   is signaled by some other property of the target resource. This encourages
   a focus on mutually agreed deployment of common standards.<a href="#section-13.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="field.if-match">
<section id="section-13.1.1">
          <h4 id="name-if-match">
<a href="#section-13.1.1" class="section-number selfRef">13.1.1. </a><a href="#name-if-match" class="section-name selfRef">If-Match</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-if-match-336" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-if-match-337" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-if-match-header-field-338" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-13.1.1-1">
   The "If-Match" header field makes the request method conditional on the
   recipient origin server either having at least one current
   representation of the target resource, when the field value is "*", or
   having a current representation of the target resource that has an
   entity tag matching a member of the list of entity tags provided in the
   field value.<a href="#section-13.1.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-2">
   An origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> use the strong comparison function when comparing
   entity tags for If-Match (<a href="#entity.tag.comparison" class="xref">Section 8.8.3.2</a>), since
   the client intends this precondition to prevent the method from being
   applied if there have been any changes to the representation data.<a href="#section-13.1.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-if-match-339" class="iref"></span>
          <div id="section-13.1.1-3">
<pre class="lang-abnf9110 sourcecode">  If-Match = "*" / #entity-tag
</pre><a href="#section-13.1.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-13.1.1-4">
   Examples:<a href="#section-13.1.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-13.1.1-5">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">If-Match: "xyzzy"
If-Match: "xyzzy", "r2d2xxxx", "c3piozzzz"
If-Match: *
</pre><a href="#section-13.1.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-13.1.1-6">
   If-Match is most often used with state-changing methods (e.g., POST, PUT,
   DELETE) to prevent accidental overwrites when multiple user agents might be
   acting in parallel on the same resource (i.e., to prevent the "lost update"
   problem). In general, it can be used with any method that involves the
   selection or modification of a representation to abort the request if the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>'s current entity tag is not a
   member within the If-Match field value.<a href="#section-13.1.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-7">
   When an origin server receives a request that selects a representation
   and that request includes an If-Match header field,
   the origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> evaluate the If-Match condition per
   <a href="#evaluation" class="xref">Section 13.2</a> prior to performing the method.<a href="#section-13.1.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-8">
   To evaluate a received If-Match header field:<a href="#section-13.1.1-8" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-13.1.1-9">
                  <li id="section-13.1.1-9.1">
      If the field value is "*", the condition is true if the origin server
      has a current representation for the target resource.<a href="#section-13.1.1-9.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-13.1.1-9.2">
      If the field value is a list of entity tags, the condition is true if
      any of the listed tags match the entity tag of the selected representation.<a href="#section-13.1.1-9.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li id="section-13.1.1-9.3">
      Otherwise, the condition is false.<a href="#section-13.1.1-9.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ol>
<p id="section-13.1.1-10">
   An origin server that evaluates an If-Match condition <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> perform
   the requested method if the condition evaluates to false. Instead,
   the origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span>
   indicate that the conditional request failed by responding with a
   <a href="#status.412" class="xref">412 (Precondition Failed)</a> status code. Alternatively,
   if the request is a state-changing operation that appears to have already
   been applied to the selected representation, the origin server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> respond
   with a <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a> status code
   (i.e., the change requested by the user agent has already succeeded, but
   the user agent might not be aware of it, perhaps because the prior response
   was lost or an equivalent change was made by some other user agent).<a href="#section-13.1.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-11">
   Allowing an origin server to send a success response when a change request
   appears to have already been applied is more efficient for many authoring
   use cases, but comes with some risk if multiple user agents are making
   change requests that are very similar but not cooperative.
   For example, multiple user agents writing to a common resource as a
   semaphore (e.g., a nonatomic increment) are likely to collide and
   potentially lose important state transitions. For those kinds of resources,
   an origin server is better off being stringent in sending 412 for every
   failed precondition on an unsafe method.
   In other cases, excluding the ETag field from a success response might
   encourage the user agent to perform a GET as its next request to eliminate
   confusion about the resource's current state.<a href="#section-13.1.1-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-12">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send an If-Match header field in a
   <a href="#GET" class="xref">GET</a> request to indicate that it would prefer a
   <a href="#status.412" class="xref">412 (Precondition Failed)</a> response if the selected
   representation does not match. However, this is only useful in range
   requests (<a href="#range.requests" class="xref">Section 14</a>) for completing a previously
   received partial representation when there is no desire for a new
   representation. <a href="#field.if-range" class="xref">If-Range</a> (<a href="#field.if-range" class="xref">Section 13.1.5</a>)
   is better suited for range requests when the client prefers to receive a
   new representation.<a href="#section-13.1.1-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-13">
   A cache or intermediary <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> ignore If-Match because its
   interoperability features are only necessary for an origin server.<a href="#section-13.1.1-13" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.1-14">
   Note that an If-Match header field with a list value containing "*" and
   other values (including other instances of "*") is syntactically
   invalid (therefore not allowed to be generated) and furthermore is
   unlikely to be interoperable.<a href="#section-13.1.1-14" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="field.if-none-match">
<section id="section-13.1.2">
          <h4 id="name-if-none-match">
<a href="#section-13.1.2" class="section-number selfRef">13.1.2. </a><a href="#name-if-none-match" class="section-name selfRef">If-None-Match</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-fields-if-none-match-340" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-header-fields-if-none-match" class="iref"></span>
          <span id="iref-if-none-match-header-field-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-13.1.2-1">
   The "If-None-Match" header field makes the request method conditional on
   a recipient cache or origin server either not having any current
   representation of the target resource, when the field value is "*", or
   having a <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> with an entity tag that does not match any
   of those listed in the field value.<a href="#section-13.1.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-13.1.2-2">
   A recipient <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> use the weak comparison function when comparing
   entity tags for If-None-Match (<a href="#entity.tag.comparison" class="xref">Section 8.8.3.2</a>),
   since weak entity tags can be used for cache validation even if there have
   been changes to the representation data.<a href="#section-13.1.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span id="iref-grammar-if-none-match-343" class="iref"></span>
