          <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-14.6-8.30">See Authors' Addresses section.<a href="#section-14.6-8.30" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
          <dd class="break"></dd>
<dt id="section-14.6-8.31">Change controller:</dt>
          <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-14.6-8.32">IESG<a href="#section-14.6-8.32" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
        <dd class="break"></dd>
</dl>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.codes">
<section id="section-15">
      <h2 id="name-status-codes">
<a href="#section-15" class="section-number selfRef">15. </a><a href="#name-status-codes" class="section-name selfRef">Status Codes</a>
      </h2>
<span id="iref-status-code-394" class="iref"></span>
      <p id="section-15-1">
   The status code of a response is a three-digit integer code that describes
   the result of the request and the semantics of the response, including
   whether the request was successful and what content is enclosed (if any).
   All valid status codes are within the range of 100 to 599, inclusive.<a href="#section-15-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15-2">
   The first digit of the status code defines the class of response. The
   last two digits do not have any categorization role. There are five
   values for the first digit:<a href="#section-15-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-15-3.1">
          <a href="#status.1xx" class="xref">1xx (Informational)</a>: The request was received, continuing
    process<a href="#section-15-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-15-3.2">
          <a href="#status.2xx" class="xref">2xx (Successful)</a>: The request was successfully received,
    understood, and accepted<a href="#section-15-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-15-3.3">
          <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">3xx (Redirection)</a>: Further action needs to be taken in order to
    complete the request<a href="#section-15-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-15-3.4">
          <a href="#status.4xx" class="xref">4xx (Client Error)</a>: The request contains bad syntax or cannot
    be fulfilled<a href="#section-15-3.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        <li class="normal" id="section-15-3.5">
          <a href="#status.5xx" class="xref">5xx (Server Error)</a>: The server failed to fulfill an apparently
    valid request<a href="#section-15-3.5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
      </ul>
<p id="section-15-4">
   HTTP status codes are extensible. A client is not required to understand
   the meaning of all registered status codes, though such understanding is
   obviously desirable. However, a client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> understand the class of any
   status code, as indicated by the first digit, and treat an unrecognized
   status code as being equivalent to the x00 status code of that class.<a href="#section-15-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15-5">
   For example, if a client receives an unrecognized status code of 471,
   it can see from the first digit that there was something wrong with its
   request and treat the response as if it had received a
   <a href="#status.400" class="xref">400 (Bad Request)</a> status code. The response
   message will usually contain a representation that explains the status.<a href="#section-15-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15-6">
   Values outside the range 100..599 are invalid. Implementations often use
   three-digit integer values outside of that range (i.e., 600..999) for
   internal communication of non-HTTP status (e.g., library errors). A client
   that receives a response with an invalid status code <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> process the
   response as if it had a <a href="#status.5xx" class="xref">5xx (Server Error)</a> status code.<a href="#section-15-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="final.interim">
<p id="section-15-7">
            <span id="iref-status-codes-final-395" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-interim-396" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-informational-" class="iref"></span>
  A single request can have multiple associated responses: zero or more
  "interim" (non-final) responses with status codes in the
  "informational" (<a href="#status.1xx" class="xref">1xx</a>) range, followed by exactly one
  "final" response with a status code in one of the other ranges.<a href="#section-15-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</div>
<div id="overview.of.status.codes">
<section id="section-15.1">
        <h3 id="name-overview-of-status-codes">
<a href="#section-15.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.1. </a><a href="#name-overview-of-status-codes" class="section-name selfRef">Overview of Status Codes</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-15.1-1">
   The status codes listed below are defined in this specification.
   The reason phrases listed here are only recommendations -- they can be
   replaced by local equivalents or left out altogether without affecting the
   protocol.<a href="#section-15.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.1-2">
   Responses with status codes that are defined as heuristically cacheable
   (e.g., 200, 203, 204, 206, 300, 301, 308, 404, 405, 410, 414, and 501 in this
   specification) can be reused by a cache with heuristic expiration unless
   otherwise indicated by the method definition or explicit cache controls
   <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>; all other status codes are not heuristically cacheable.<a href="#section-15.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.1-3">
   Additional status codes, outside the scope of this specification, have been
   specified for use in HTTP. All such status codes ought to be registered
   within the "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Status Code Registry",
   as described in <a href="#status.code.extensibility" class="xref">Section 16.2</a>.<a href="#section-15.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.1xx">
<section id="section-15.2">
        <h3 id="name-informational-1xx">
<a href="#section-15.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.2. </a><a href="#name-informational-1xx" class="section-name selfRef">Informational 1xx</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-1xx-informational-status-co" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-classes-1xx-in" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-15.2-1">
   The 1xx (Informational) class of status code indicates an
   interim response for communicating connection status or request progress
   prior to completing the requested action and sending a final response.
   Since HTTP/1.0 did not define any 1xx status codes, a server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> send
   a 1xx response to an HTTP/1.0 client.<a href="#section-15.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.2-2">
   A 1xx response is terminated by the end of the header section;
   it cannot contain content or trailers.<a href="#section-15.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.2-3">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> be able to parse one or more 1xx responses received
   prior to a final response, even if the client does not expect one.
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> ignore unexpected 1xx responses.<a href="#section-15.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.2-4">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> forward 1xx responses unless the proxy itself
   requested the generation of the 1xx response. For example, if a
   proxy adds an "Expect: 100-continue" header field when it forwards a request,
   then it need not forward the corresponding <a href="#status.100" class="xref">100 (Continue)</a>
   response(s).<a href="#section-15.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="status.100">
<section id="section-15.2.1">
          <h4 id="name-100-continue">
<a href="#section-15.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.2.1. </a><a href="#name-100-continue" class="section-name selfRef">100 Continue</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-100-continue-status-code-40" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.2.1-1">
   The 100 (Continue) status code indicates that the initial
   part of a request has been received and has not yet been rejected by the
   server. The server intends to send a final response after the request has
   been fully received and acted upon.<a href="#section-15.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.2.1-2">
   When the request contains an <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Expect</a> header field that
   includes a <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">100-continue</a> expectation, the 100 response
   indicates that the server wishes to receive the request content,
   as described in <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Section 10.1.1</a>.  The client
   ought to continue sending the request and discard the 100 response.<a href="#section-15.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.2.1-3">
   If the request did not contain an <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Expect</a> header field
   containing the <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">100-continue</a> expectation,
   the client can simply discard this interim response.<a href="#section-15.2.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.101">
<section id="section-15.2.2">
          <h4 id="name-101-switching-protocols">
<a href="#section-15.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.2.2. </a><a href="#name-101-switching-protocols" class="section-name selfRef">101 Switching Protocols</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-101-switching-protocols-sta" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.2.2-1">
   The 101 (Switching Protocols) status code indicates that the
   server understands and is willing to comply with the client's request,
   via the <a href="#field.upgrade" class="xref">Upgrade</a> header field (<a href="#field.upgrade" class="xref">Section 7.8</a>), for
   a change in the application protocol being used on this connection.
   The server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate an Upgrade header field in the response that
   indicates which protocol(s) will be in effect after this response.<a href="#section-15.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.2.2-2">
   It is assumed that the server will only agree to switch protocols when
   it is advantageous to do so. For example, switching to a newer version of
   HTTP might be advantageous over older versions, and switching to a
   real-time, synchronous protocol might be advantageous when delivering
   resources that use such features.<a href="#section-15.2.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.2xx">
<section id="section-15.3">
        <h3 id="name-successful-2xx">
<a href="#section-15.3" class="section-number selfRef">15.3. </a><a href="#name-successful-2xx" class="section-name selfRef">Successful 2xx</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-2xx-successful-status-code-" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-classes-2xx-su" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-15.3-1">
   The 2xx (Successful) class of status code indicates that
   the client's request was successfully received, understood, and accepted.<a href="#section-15.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="status.200">
<section id="section-15.3.1">
          <h4 id="name-200-ok">
<a href="#section-15.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.1. </a><a href="#name-200-ok" class="section-name selfRef">200 OK</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-200-ok-status-code-404" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.1-1">
   The 200 (OK) status code indicates that the request has
   succeeded. The content sent in a 200 response depends on the request
   method. For the methods defined by this specification, the intended meaning
   of the content can be summarized as:<a href="#section-15.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<table class="left" id="table-6">
            <caption><a href="#table-6" class="selfRef">Table 6</a></caption>
<thead>
              <tr>
                <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Request Method</th>
                <th class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">Response content is a representation of:</th>
              </tr>
            </thead>
            <tbody>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">GET</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>
                </td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">HEAD</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>, like GET, but without
          transferring the representation data</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">POST</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">the status of, or results obtained from, the action</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">PUT, DELETE</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">the status of the action</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">OPTIONS</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">communication options for the target resource</td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">TRACE</td>
                <td class="text-left" rowspan="1" colspan="1">the request message as received by the server returning the
          trace</td>
              </tr>
            </tbody>
          </table>
<p id="section-15.3.1-3">
   Aside from responses to CONNECT, a 200 response is expected to contain
   message content unless the message framing explicitly indicates that the
   content has zero length. If some aspect of the request indicates a
   preference for no content upon success, the origin server ought to send a
   204 (No Content) response instead.
   For CONNECT, there is no content because the successful result is a
   tunnel, which begins immediately after the 200 response header section.<a href="#section-15.3.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.1-4">
   A 200 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.3.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.1-5">
   In 200 responses to GET or HEAD, an origin server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send any
   available validator fields (<a href="#response.validator" class="xref">Section 8.8</a>) for the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>, with both a strong entity tag and
   a <a href="#field.last-modified" class="xref">Last-Modified</a> date being preferred.<a href="#section-15.3.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.1-6">
   In 200 responses to state-changing methods, any validator fields
   (<a href="#response.validator" class="xref">Section 8.8</a>) sent in the response convey the
   current validators for the new representation formed as a result of
   successfully applying the request semantics. Note that the PUT method
   (<a href="#PUT" class="xref">Section 9.3.4</a>) has additional requirements that might preclude
   sending such validators.<a href="#section-15.3.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.201">
<section id="section-15.3.2">
          <h4 id="name-201-created">
<a href="#section-15.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.2. </a><a href="#name-201-created" class="section-name selfRef">201 Created</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-201-created-status-code-405" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.2-1">
   The 201 (Created) status code indicates that the request has
   been fulfilled and has resulted in one or more new resources being created.
   The primary resource created by the request is identified by either a
   <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field in the response or, if no
   <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field is received, by the target URI.<a href="#section-15.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.2-2">
   The 201 response content typically describes and links to the resource(s)
   created. Any validator fields (<a href="#response.validator" class="xref">Section 8.8</a>)
   sent in the response convey the current validators for a new
   representation created by the request. Note that the PUT method
   (<a href="#PUT" class="xref">Section 9.3.4</a>) has additional requirements that might preclude
   sending such validators.<a href="#section-15.3.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.202">
<section id="section-15.3.3">
          <h4 id="name-202-accepted">
<a href="#section-15.3.3" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.3. </a><a href="#name-202-accepted" class="section-name selfRef">202 Accepted</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-202-accepted-status-code-40" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.3-1">
   The 202 (Accepted) status code indicates that the request
   has been accepted for processing, but the processing has not been
   completed. The request might or might not eventually be acted upon, as it
   might be disallowed when processing actually takes place. There is no
   facility in HTTP for re-sending a status code from an asynchronous
   operation.<a href="#section-15.3.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.3-2">
   The 202 response is intentionally noncommittal. Its purpose is to
   allow a server to accept a request for some other process (perhaps a
   batch-oriented process that is only run once per day) without
   requiring that the user agent's connection to the server persist
   until the process is completed. The representation sent with this
   response ought to describe the request's current status and point to
   (or embed) a status monitor that can provide the user with an estimate of
   when the request will be fulfilled.<a href="#section-15.3.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.203">
<section id="section-15.3.4">
          <h4 id="name-203-non-authoritative-infor">
<a href="#section-15.3.4" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.4. </a><a href="#name-203-non-authoritative-infor" class="section-name selfRef">203 Non-Authoritative Information</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-203-non-authoritative-infor" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.4-1">
   The 203 (Non-Authoritative Information) status code
   indicates that the request was successful but the enclosed content has been
   modified from that of the origin server's <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response
   by a transforming proxy (<a href="#message.transformations" class="xref">Section 7.7</a>). This status code allows the
   proxy to notify recipients when a transformation has been applied, since
   that knowledge might impact later decisions regarding the content. For
   example, future cache validation requests for the content might only be
   applicable along the same request path (through the same proxies).<a href="#section-15.3.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.4-2">
   A 203 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.3.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.204">
<section id="section-15.3.5">
          <h4 id="name-204-no-content">
<a href="#section-15.3.5" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.5. </a><a href="#name-204-no-content" class="section-name selfRef">204 No Content</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-204-no-content-status-code-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.5-1">
   The 204 (No Content) status code indicates that the server
   has successfully fulfilled the request and that there is no additional
   content to send in the response content. Metadata in the response
   header fields refer to the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> and its
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a> after the requested action was applied.<a href="#section-15.3.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.5-2">
   For example, if a 204 status code is received in response to a PUT
   request and the response contains an <a href="#field.etag" class="xref">ETag</a> field, then
   the PUT was successful and the ETag field value contains the entity tag for
   the new representation of that target resource.<a href="#section-15.3.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.5-3">
   The 204 response allows a server to indicate that the action has been
   successfully applied to the target resource, while implying that the
   user agent does not need to traverse away from its current "document view"
   (if any).  The server assumes that the user agent will provide some
   indication of the success to its user, in accord with its own interface,
   and apply any new or updated metadata in the response to its active
   representation.<a href="#section-15.3.5-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.5-4">
   For example, a 204 status code is commonly used with document editing
   interfaces corresponding to a "save" action, such that the document
   being saved remains available to the user for editing. It is also
   frequently used with interfaces that expect automated data transfers
   to be prevalent, such as within distributed version control systems.<a href="#section-15.3.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.5-5">
   A 204 response is terminated by the end of the header section;
   it cannot contain content or trailers.<a href="#section-15.3.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.5-6">
   A 204 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.3.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.205">
<section id="section-15.3.6">
          <h4 id="name-205-reset-content">
<a href="#section-15.3.6" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.6. </a><a href="#name-205-reset-content" class="section-name selfRef">205 Reset Content</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-205-reset-content-status-co" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.6-1">
   The 205 (Reset Content) status code indicates that the
   server has fulfilled the request and desires that the user agent reset the
   "document view", which caused the request to be sent, to its original state
   as received from the origin server.<a href="#section-15.3.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.6-2">
   This response is intended to support a common data entry use case where
   the user receives content that supports data entry (a form, notepad,
   canvas, etc.), enters or manipulates data in that space, causes the entered
   data to be submitted in a request, and then the data entry mechanism is
   reset for the next entry so that the user can easily initiate another
   input action.<a href="#section-15.3.6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.6-3">
   Since the 205 status code implies that no additional content will be
   provided, a server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate content in a 205 response.<a href="#section-15.3.6-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.206">
<section id="section-15.3.7">
          <h4 id="name-206-partial-content">
<a href="#section-15.3.7" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.7. </a><a href="#name-206-partial-content" class="section-name selfRef">206 Partial Content</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-206-partial-content-status-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.3.7-1">
   The 206 (Partial Content) status code indicates that the
   server is successfully fulfilling a range request for the target resource
   by transferring one or more parts of the
   <a href="#selected.representation" class="xref">selected representation</a>.<a href="#section-15.3.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7-2">
   A server that supports range requests (<a href="#range.requests" class="xref">Section 14</a>) will
   usually attempt to satisfy all of the requested ranges, since sending
   less data will likely result in another client request for the remainder.
   However, a server might want to send only a subset of the data requested
   for reasons of its own, such as temporary unavailability, cache efficiency,
   load balancing, etc. Since a 206 response is self-descriptive, the client
   can still understand a response that only partially satisfies its range
   request.<a href="#section-15.3.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7-3">
   A client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> inspect a 206 response's <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> and
   <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> field(s) to determine what parts are enclosed
   and whether additional requests are needed.<a href="#section-15.3.7-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7-4">
   A server that generates a 206 response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate the following
   header fields, in addition to those required in the subsections below,
   if the field would
   have been sent in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response to the same request:
   <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a>, Cache-Control, <a href="#field.etag" class="xref">ETag</a>,
   Expires, <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a>, and
   <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Vary</a>.<a href="#section-15.3.7-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7-5">
   A <a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Content-Length</a> header field present in a 206 response
   indicates the number of octets in the content of this message, which is
   usually not the complete length of the selected representation.
   Each <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header field includes information about the
   selected representation's complete length.<a href="#section-15.3.7-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7-6">
   A sender that generates a 206 response to a request with an <a href="#field.if-range" class="xref">If-Range</a>
   header field <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate other representation header
   fields beyond those required because the client
   already has a prior response containing those header fields.
   Otherwise, a sender <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate all of the representation header
   fields that would have been sent in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response
   to the same request.<a href="#section-15.3.7-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7-7">
   A 206 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.3.7-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="partial.single">
<section id="section-15.3.7.1">
            <h5 id="name-single-part">
<a href="#section-15.3.7.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.7.1. </a><a href="#name-single-part" class="section-name selfRef">Single Part</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-15.3.7.1-1">
   If a single part is being transferred, the server generating the 206
   response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate a <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header field,
   describing what range of the selected representation is enclosed, and a
   content consisting of the range. For example:<a href="#section-15.3.7.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-15.3.7.1-2">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 206 Partial Content
Date: Wed, 15 Nov 1995 06:25:24 GMT
Last-Modified: Wed, 15 Nov 1995 04:58:08 GMT
Content-Range: bytes 21010-47021/47022
Content-Length: 26012
Content-Type: image/gif

... 26012 bytes of partial image data ...
</pre><a href="#section-15.3.7.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="partial.multipart">
<section id="section-15.3.7.2">
            <h5 id="name-multiple-parts">
<a href="#section-15.3.7.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.7.2. </a><a href="#name-multiple-parts" class="section-name selfRef">Multiple Parts</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-15.3.7.2-1">
   If multiple parts are being transferred, the server generating the 206
   response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate "multipart/byteranges" content, as defined
   in <a href="#multipart.byteranges" class="xref">Section 14.6</a>, and a
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> header field containing the
   "multipart/byteranges" media type and its required boundary parameter.
   To avoid confusion with single-part responses, a server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate
   a <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header field in the HTTP header section of a
   multiple part response (this field will be sent in each part instead).<a href="#section-15.3.7.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.2-2">
   Within the header area of each body part in the multipart content, the
   server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate a <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header field
   corresponding to the range being enclosed in that body part.
   If the selected representation would have had a <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a>
   header field in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response, the server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span>
   generate that same <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> header field in the header area of
   each body part. For example:<a href="#section-15.3.7.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-15.3.7.2-3">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 206 Partial Content
Date: Wed, 15 Nov 1995 06:25:24 GMT
Last-Modified: Wed, 15 Nov 1995 04:58:08 GMT
Content-Length: 1741
Content-Type: multipart/byteranges; boundary=THIS_STRING_SEPARATES

--THIS_STRING_SEPARATES
Content-Type: application/pdf
Content-Range: bytes 500-999/8000

...the first range...
--THIS_STRING_SEPARATES
Content-Type: application/pdf
Content-Range: bytes 7000-7999/8000

...the second range
--THIS_STRING_SEPARATES--
</pre><a href="#section-15.3.7.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<p id="section-15.3.7.2-4">
   When multiple ranges are requested, a server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> coalesce any of the
   ranges that overlap, or that are separated by a gap that is smaller than the
   overhead of sending multiple parts, regardless of the order in which the
   corresponding range-spec appeared in the received <a href="#field.range" class="xref">Range</a>
   header field. Since the typical overhead between each part of a
   "multipart/byteranges" is around 80 bytes, depending on the selected
   representation's media type and the chosen boundary parameter length, it
   can be less efficient to transfer many small disjoint parts than it is to
   transfer the entire selected representation.<a href="#section-15.3.7.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.2-5">
   A server <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a multipart response to a request for a single
   range, since a client that does not request multiple parts might not
   support multipart responses. However, a server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> generate a
   "multipart/byteranges" response with only a single body part if multiple
   ranges were requested and only one range was found to be satisfiable or
   only one range remained after coalescing.
   A client that cannot process a "multipart/byteranges" response <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span>
   generate a request that asks for multiple ranges.<a href="#section-15.3.7.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.2-6">
   A server that generates a multipart response <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send
   the parts in the same order that the corresponding range-spec appeared
   in the received <a href="#field.range" class="xref">Range</a> header field, excluding those ranges
   that were deemed unsatisfiable or that were coalesced into other ranges.
   A client that receives a multipart response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> inspect the
   <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header field present in each body part in
   order to determine which range is contained in that body part; a client
   cannot rely on receiving the same ranges that it requested, nor the same
   order that it requested.<a href="#section-15.3.7.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="combining.byte.ranges">
<section id="section-15.3.7.3">
            <h5 id="name-combining-parts">
<a href="#section-15.3.7.3" class="section-number selfRef">15.3.7.3. </a><a href="#name-combining-parts" class="section-name selfRef">Combining Parts</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-15.3.7.3-1">
   A response might transfer only a subrange of a representation if the
   connection closed prematurely or if the request used one or more Range
   specifications.  After several such transfers, a client might have
   received several ranges of the same representation.  These ranges can only
   be safely combined if they all have in common the same strong validator
   (<a href="#weak.and.strong.validators" class="xref">Section 8.8.1</a>).<a href="#section-15.3.7.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.3-2">
   A client that has received multiple partial responses to GET requests on a
   target resource <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> combine those responses into a larger continuous
   range if they share the same strong validator.<a href="#section-15.3.7.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.3-3">
   If the most recent response is an incomplete <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a>
   response, then the header fields of that response are used for any
   combined response and replace those of the matching stored responses.<a href="#section-15.3.7.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.3-4">
   If the most recent response is a <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a>
   response and at least one of the matching stored responses is a
   <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a>, then the combined response header fields consist
   of the most recent 200 response's header fields. If all of the matching
   stored responses are 206 responses, then the stored response with the most
   recent header fields is used as the source of header fields for the
   combined response, except that the client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> use other header fields
   provided in the new response, aside from <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a>, to
   replace all instances of the corresponding header fields in the stored
   response.<a href="#section-15.3.7.3-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.3.7.3-5">
   The combined response content consists of the union of partial content
   ranges within the new response and all of the matching stored responses.
   If the union consists of the entire range of the representation, then the
   client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> process the combined response as if it were a complete
   <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response, including a <a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Content-Length</a>
   header field that reflects the complete length.
   Otherwise, the client <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> process the set of continuous ranges as one of
   the following:
   an incomplete <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response if the combined response is
   a prefix of the representation,
   a single <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a> response containing
   "multipart/byteranges" content, or
   multiple <a href="#status.206" class="xref">206 (Partial Content)</a> responses, each with one
   continuous range that is indicated by a <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header
   field.<a href="#section-15.3.7.3-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.3xx">
<section id="section-15.4">
        <h3 id="name-redirection-3xx">
<a href="#section-15.4" class="section-number selfRef">15.4. </a><a href="#name-redirection-3xx" class="section-name selfRef">Redirection 3xx</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-3xx-redirection-status-code" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-classes-3xx-re" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-15.4-1">
   The 3xx (Redirection) class of status code indicates that
   further action needs to be taken by the user agent in order to fulfill the
   request. There are several types of redirects:<a href="#section-15.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-15.4-2">
               <li id="section-15.4-2.1">
    Redirects that indicate this resource might be available at a
    different URI, as provided by the <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field,
    as in the status codes <a href="#status.301" class="xref">301 (Moved Permanently)</a>,
    <a href="#status.302" class="xref">302 (Found)</a>, <a href="#status.307" class="xref">307 (Temporary Redirect)</a>, and
    <a href="#status.308" class="xref">308 (Permanent Redirect)</a>.<a href="#section-15.4-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-2.2">
    Redirection that offers a choice among matching resources capable
    of representing this resource, as in the
    <a href="#status.300" class="xref">300 (Multiple Choices)</a> status code.<a href="#section-15.4-2.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-2.3">
    Redirection to a different resource, identified by the
    <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field, that can represent an indirect
    response to the request, as in the <a href="#status.303" class="xref">303 (See Other)</a>
    status code.<a href="#section-15.4-2.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-2.4">
    Redirection to a previously stored result, as in the
    <a href="#status.304" class="xref">304 (Not Modified)</a> status code.<a href="#section-15.4-2.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        </ol>
<aside id="section-15.4-3">
          <p id="section-15.4-3.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> In HTTP/1.0, the status codes <a href="#status.301" class="xref">301 (Moved Permanently)</a>
    and <a href="#status.302" class="xref">302 (Found)</a> were originally defined as method-preserving
    (<span>[<a href="#HTTP10" class="xref">HTTP/1.0</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1945#section-9.3" class="relref">Section 9.3</a></span>) to match their implementation
    at CERN; <a href="#status.303" class="xref">303 (See Other)</a> was defined for a redirection that
    changed its method to GET. However, early user agents split on whether to
    redirect POST requests as POST (according to then-current specification)
    or as GET (the safer alternative when redirected to a different site).
    Prevailing practice eventually converged on changing the method to GET.
    <a href="#status.307" class="xref">307 (Temporary Redirect)</a> and
    <a href="#status.308" class="xref">308 (Permanent Redirect)</a>
            <span>[<a href="#RFC7538" class="xref">RFC7538</a>]</span> were
    later added to unambiguously indicate method-preserving redirects, and status codes
    <a href="#status.301" class="xref">301</a> and <a href="#status.302" class="xref">302</a> have been adjusted to allow a POST
    request to be redirected as GET.<a href="#section-15.4-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<p id="section-15.4-4">
   If a <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.location" class="xref">Section 10.2.2</a>) is provided, the user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span>
   automatically redirect its request to the URI referenced by the Location
   field value, even if the specific status code is not understood.
   Automatic redirection needs to be done with care for methods not known to be
   <a href="#safe.methods" class="xref">safe</a>, as defined in <a href="#safe.methods" class="xref">Section 9.2.1</a>, since
   the user might not wish to redirect an unsafe request.<a href="#section-15.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4-5">
   When automatically following a redirected request, the user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span>
   resend the original request message with the following modifications:<a href="#section-15.4-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-15.4-6">
               <li id="section-15.4-6.1">
            <p id="section-15.4-6.1.1">
      Replace the target URI with the URI referenced by the redirection response's
      <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field value after resolving it relative to the original
      request's target URI.<a href="#section-15.4-6.1.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-6.2">
            <p id="section-15.4-6.2.1">
      Remove header fields that were automatically generated by the implementation,
      replacing them with updated values as appropriate to the new request. This
      includes:<a href="#section-15.4-6.2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-15.4-6.2.2">
                     <li id="section-15.4-6.2.2.1">Connection-specific header fields (see <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Section 7.6.1</a>),<a href="#section-15.4-6.2.2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li id="section-15.4-6.2.2.2">Header fields specific to the client's proxy configuration,
       including (but not limited to) <a href="#field.proxy-authorization" class="xref">Proxy-Authorization</a>,<a href="#section-15.4-6.2.2.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li id="section-15.4-6.2.2.3">Origin-specific header fields (if any), including (but not
       limited to) <a href="#field.host" class="xref">Host</a>,<a href="#section-15.4-6.2.2.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li id="section-15.4-6.2.2.4">Validating header fields that were added by the implementation's
       cache (e.g., <a href="#field.if-none-match" class="xref">If-None-Match</a>,
       <a href="#field.if-modified-since" class="xref">If-Modified-Since</a>), and<a href="#section-15.4-6.2.2.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
              <li id="section-15.4-6.2.2.5">Resource-specific header fields, including (but not limited to)
       <a href="#field.referer" class="xref">Referer</a>, Origin,
       <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a>, and Cookie.<a href="#section-15.4-6.2.2.5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            </ol>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-6.3">
            <p id="section-15.4-6.3.1">
      Consider removing header fields that were not automatically generated by the
      implementation (i.e., those present in the request because they were added
      by the calling context) where there are security implications; this
      includes but is not limited to <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> and Cookie.<a href="#section-15.4-6.3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-6.4">
            <p id="section-15.4-6.4.1">
      Change the request method according to the redirecting status code's
      semantics, if applicable.<a href="#section-15.4-6.4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
          <li id="section-15.4-6.5">
            <p id="section-15.4-6.5.1">
      If the request method has been changed to GET or HEAD, remove
      content-specific header fields, including (but not limited to)
      <a href="#field.content-encoding" class="xref">Content-Encoding</a>,
      <a href="#field.content-language" class="xref">Content-Language</a>, <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a>,
      <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a>, <a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Content-Length</a>,
      Digest, <a href="#field.last-modified" class="xref">Last-Modified</a>.<a href="#section-15.4-6.5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
        </ol>
<p id="section-15.4-7">
   A client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> detect and intervene in cyclical redirections (i.e.,
   "infinite" redirection loops).<a href="#section-15.4-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-15.4-8">
          <p id="section-15.4-8.1">
                  <strong>Note:</strong> An earlier version of this specification recommended a
    maximum of five redirections (<span>[<a href="#RFC2068" class="xref">RFC2068</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2068#section-10.3" class="relref">Section 10.3</a></span>).
    Content developers need to be aware that some clients might
    implement such a fixed limitation.<a href="#section-15.4-8.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<div id="status.300">
<section id="section-15.4.1">
          <h4 id="name-300-multiple-choices">
<a href="#section-15.4.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.1. </a><a href="#name-300-multiple-choices" class="section-name selfRef">300 Multiple Choices</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-300-multiple-choices-status" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.1-1">
   The 300 (Multiple Choices) status code indicates that the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> has more than one representation, each with
   its own more specific identifier, and information about the alternatives is
   being provided so that the user (or user agent) can select a preferred
   representation by redirecting its request to one or more of those
   identifiers. In other words, the server desires that the user agent engage
   in reactive negotiation to select the most appropriate representation(s)
   for its needs (<a href="#content.negotiation" class="xref">Section 12</a>).<a href="#section-15.4.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.1-2">
   If the server has a preferred choice, the server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a
   <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field containing a preferred choice's URI
   reference. The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use the Location field value for automatic
   redirection.<a href="#section-15.4.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.1-3">
   For request methods other than HEAD, the server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate content
   in the 300 response containing a list of representation metadata and URI
   reference(s) from which the user or user agent can choose the one most
   preferred. The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> make a selection from that list
   automatically if it understands the provided media type. A specific format
   for automatic selection is not defined by this specification because HTTP
   tries to remain orthogonal to the definition of its content.
   In practice, the representation is provided in some easily parsed format
   believed to be acceptable to the user agent, as determined by shared design
   or content negotiation, or in some commonly accepted hypertext format.<a href="#section-15.4.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.1-4">
   A 300 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.4.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-15.4.1-5">
            <p id="section-15.4.1-5.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> The original proposal for the 300 status code defined the URI header field as
   providing a list of alternative representations, such that it would be
   usable for 200, 300, and 406 responses and be transferred in responses to
   the HEAD method. However, lack of deployment and disagreement over syntax
   led to both URI and Alternates (a subsequent proposal) being dropped from
   this specification. It is possible to communicate the list as a
   Link header field value <span>[<a href="#RFC8288" class="xref">RFC8288</a>]</span> whose members have a relationship of
   "alternate", though deployment is a chicken-and-egg problem.<a href="#section-15.4.1-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.301">
<section id="section-15.4.2">
          <h4 id="name-301-moved-permanently">
<a href="#section-15.4.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.2. </a><a href="#name-301-moved-permanently" class="section-name selfRef">301 Moved Permanently</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-301-moved-permanently-statu" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.2-1">
   The 301 (Moved Permanently) status code indicates that the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> has been assigned a new permanent URI and
   any future references to this resource ought to use one of the enclosed
   URIs. The server is suggesting that a user agent with link-editing capability
   can permanently replace references to the target URI with one of the
   new references sent by the server. However, this suggestion is usually
   ignored unless the user agent is actively editing references
   (e.g., engaged in authoring content), the connection is secured, and
   the origin server is a trusted authority for the content being edited.<a href="#section-15.4.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.2-2">
   The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field in the
   response containing a preferred URI reference for the new permanent URI.
   The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use the Location field value for automatic redirection.
   The server's response content usually contains a short hypertext note with
   a hyperlink to the new URI(s).<a href="#section-15.4.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-15.4.2-3">
            <p id="section-15.4.2-3.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> For historical reasons, a user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> change the
    request method from POST to GET for the subsequent request. If this
    behavior is undesired, the <a href="#status.308" class="xref">308 (Permanent Redirect)</a>
    status code can be used instead.<a href="#section-15.4.2-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
<p id="section-15.4.2-4">
   A 301 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.4.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.302">
<section id="section-15.4.3">
          <h4 id="name-302-found">
<a href="#section-15.4.3" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.3. </a><a href="#name-302-found" class="section-name selfRef">302 Found</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-302-found-status-code-415" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.3-1">
   The 302 (Found) status code indicates that the target
   resource resides temporarily under a different URI. Since the redirection
   might be altered on occasion, the client ought to continue to use the
   target URI for future requests.<a href="#section-15.4.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.3-2">
   The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field in the
   response containing a URI reference for the different URI.
   The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use the Location field value for automatic redirection.
   The server's response content usually contains a short hypertext note with
   a hyperlink to the different URI(s).<a href="#section-15.4.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-15.4.3-3">
            <p id="section-15.4.3-3.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> For historical reasons, a user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> change the
    request method from POST to GET for the subsequent request. If this
    behavior is undesired, the <a href="#status.307" class="xref">307 (Temporary Redirect)</a>
    status code can be used instead.<a href="#section-15.4.3-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.303">
<section id="section-15.4.4">
          <h4 id="name-303-see-other">
<a href="#section-15.4.4" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.4. </a><a href="#name-303-see-other" class="section-name selfRef">303 See Other</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-303-see-other-status-code-4" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.4-1">
   The 303 (See Other) status code indicates that the server is
   redirecting the user agent to a different resource, as indicated by a URI
   in the <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field, which is intended to provide
   an indirect response to the original request. A user agent can perform a
   retrieval request targeting that URI (a GET or HEAD request if using HTTP),
   which might also be redirected, and present the eventual result as an
   answer to the original request. Note that the new URI in the Location
   header field is not considered equivalent to the target URI.<a href="#section-15.4.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.4-2">
   This status code is applicable to any HTTP method.  It is
   primarily used to allow the output of a POST action to redirect
   the user agent to a different resource, since doing so provides the
   information corresponding to the POST response as a resource that
   can be separately identified, bookmarked, and cached.<a href="#section-15.4.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.4-3">
   A 303 response to a GET request indicates that the origin server does not
   have a representation of the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> that can be
   transferred by the server over HTTP. However, the
   <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> field value refers to a resource that is
   descriptive of the target resource, such that making a retrieval request
   on that other resource might result in a representation that is useful to
   recipients without implying that it represents the original target resource.
   Note that answers to the questions of what can be represented, what
   representations are adequate, and what might be a useful description are
   outside the scope of HTTP.<a href="#section-15.4.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.4-4">
   Except for responses to a HEAD request, the representation of a 303
   response ought to contain a short hypertext note with a hyperlink to the
   same URI reference provided in the <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field.<a href="#section-15.4.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.304">
<section id="section-15.4.5">
          <h4 id="name-304-not-modified">
<a href="#section-15.4.5" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.5. </a><a href="#name-304-not-modified" class="section-name selfRef">304 Not Modified</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-304-not-modified-status-cod" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.5-1">
   The 304 (Not Modified) status code indicates that a
   conditional GET or HEAD request has been
   received and would have resulted in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response
   if it were not for the fact that the condition evaluated to false.
   In other words, there is no need for the server to transfer a
   representation of the target resource because the request indicates that
   the client, which made the request conditional, already has a valid
   representation; the server is therefore redirecting the client to make
   use of that stored representation as if it were the content of a
   <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response.<a href="#section-15.4.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.5-2">
   The server generating a 304 response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate any of the following
   header fields that would have been sent in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a>
   response to the same request:<a href="#section-15.4.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-15.4.5-3.1">
              <a href="#field.content-location" class="xref">Content-Location</a>, <a href="#field.date" class="xref">Date</a>, <a href="#field.etag" class="xref">ETag</a>,
    and <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Vary</a><a href="#section-15.4.5-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-15.4.5-3.2">
    Cache-Control and Expires (see
    <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>)<a href="#section-15.4.5-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-15.4.5-4">
   Since the goal of a 304 response is to minimize information transfer
   when the recipient already has one or more cached representations,
   a sender <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> generate representation metadata other
   than the above listed fields unless said metadata exists for the
   purpose of guiding cache updates (e.g., <a href="#field.last-modified" class="xref">Last-Modified</a> might
   be useful if the response does not have an <a href="#field.etag" class="xref">ETag</a> field).<a href="#section-15.4.5-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.5-5">
   Requirements on a cache that receives a 304 response are defined in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.3.4" class="relref">Section 4.3.4</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>. If the conditional request originated with an
   outbound client, such as a user agent with its own cache sending a
   conditional GET to a shared proxy, then the proxy <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> forward the
   304 response to that client.<a href="#section-15.4.5-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.5-6">
   A 304 response is terminated by the end of the header section;
   it cannot contain content or trailers.<a href="#section-15.4.5-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.305">
<section id="section-15.4.6">
          <h4 id="name-305-use-proxy">
<a href="#section-15.4.6" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.6. </a><a href="#name-305-use-proxy" class="section-name selfRef">305 Use Proxy</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-305-use-proxy-status-code-4" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.6-1">
   The 305 (Use Proxy) status code was defined in a previous
   version of this specification and is now deprecated (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7231#appendix-B" class="relref">Appendix B</a> of [<a href="#RFC7231" class="xref">RFC7231</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.4.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.306">
<section id="section-15.4.7">
          <h4 id="name-306-unused">
<a href="#section-15.4.7" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.7. </a><a href="#name-306-unused" class="section-name selfRef">306 (Unused)</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-306-unused-status-code-419" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.7-1">
   The 306 status code was defined in a previous version of this
   specification, is no longer used, and the code is reserved.<a href="#section-15.4.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.307">
<section id="section-15.4.8">
          <h4 id="name-307-temporary-redirect">
<a href="#section-15.4.8" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.8. </a><a href="#name-307-temporary-redirect" class="section-name selfRef">307 Temporary Redirect</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-307-temporary-redirect-stat" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.8-1">
   The 307 (Temporary Redirect) status code indicates that the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> resides temporarily under a different URI
   and the user agent <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> change the request method if it performs an
   automatic redirection to that URI.
   Since the redirection can change over time, the client ought to continue
   using the original target URI for future requests.<a href="#section-15.4.8-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.8-2">
   The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field in the
   response containing a URI reference for the different URI.
   The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use the Location field value for automatic redirection.
   The server's response content usually contains a short hypertext note with
   a hyperlink to the different URI(s).<a href="#section-15.4.8-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.308">
<section id="section-15.4.9">
          <h4 id="name-308-permanent-redirect">
<a href="#section-15.4.9" class="section-number selfRef">15.4.9. </a><a href="#name-308-permanent-redirect" class="section-name selfRef">308 Permanent Redirect</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-308-permanent-redirect-stat" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.4.9-1">
   The 308 (Permanent Redirect) status code indicates that the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> has been assigned a new permanent URI and
   any future references to this resource ought to use one of the enclosed
   URIs. The server is suggesting that a user agent with link-editing capability
   can permanently replace references to the target URI with one of the
   new references sent by the server. However, this suggestion is usually
   ignored unless the user agent is actively editing references
   (e.g., engaged in authoring content), the connection is secured, and
   the origin server is a trusted authority for the content being edited.<a href="#section-15.4.9-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.9-2">
   The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> header field in the
   response containing a preferred URI reference for the new permanent URI.
   The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> use the Location field value for automatic redirection.
   The server's response content usually contains a short hypertext note with
   a hyperlink to the new URI(s).<a href="#section-15.4.9-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.4.9-3">
   A 308 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.4.9-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-15.4.9-4">
            <p id="section-15.4.9-4.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> This status code is much younger (June 2014) than its sibling codes and thus
    might not be recognized everywhere. See <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7538#section-4" class="relref">Section 4</a> of [<a href="#RFC7538" class="xref">RFC7538</a>]</span>
    for deployment considerations.<a href="#section-15.4.9-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.4xx">
<section id="section-15.5">
        <h3 id="name-client-error-4xx">
<a href="#section-15.5" class="section-number selfRef">15.5. </a><a href="#name-client-error-4xx" class="section-name selfRef">Client Error 4xx</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-4xx-client-error-status-cod" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-classes-4xx-cl" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-15.5-1">
   The 4xx (Client Error) class of status code indicates that
   the client seems to have erred. Except when responding to a HEAD request,
   the server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a representation containing an explanation of
   the error situation, and whether it is a temporary or permanent condition.
   These status codes are applicable to any request method. User agents
   <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> display any included representation to the user.<a href="#section-15.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="status.400">
<section id="section-15.5.1">
          <h4 id="name-400-bad-request">
<a href="#section-15.5.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.1. </a><a href="#name-400-bad-request" class="section-name selfRef">400 Bad Request</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-400-bad-request-status-code" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.1-1">
   The 400 (Bad Request) status code indicates that the server
   cannot or will not process the request due to something that is perceived
   to be a client error (e.g., malformed request syntax, invalid request
   message framing, or deceptive request routing).<a href="#section-15.5.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.401">
<section id="section-15.5.2">
          <h4 id="name-401-unauthorized">
<a href="#section-15.5.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.2. </a><a href="#name-401-unauthorized" class="section-name selfRef">401 Unauthorized</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-401-unauthorized-status-cod" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.2-1">
   The 401 (Unauthorized) status code indicates that the
   request has not been applied because it lacks valid authentication
   credentials for the target resource.
   The server generating a 401 response <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send a
   <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">Section 11.6.1</a>)
   containing at least one challenge applicable to the target resource.<a href="#section-15.5.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.2-2">
   If the request included authentication credentials, then the 401 response
   indicates that authorization has been refused for those credentials.
   The user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> repeat the request with a new or replaced
   <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> header field (<a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Section 11.6.2</a>).
   If the 401 response contains the same challenge as the prior response, and
   the user agent has already attempted authentication at least once, then the
   user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> present the enclosed representation to the user, since
   it usually contains relevant diagnostic information.<a href="#section-15.5.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.402">
<section id="section-15.5.3">
          <h4 id="name-402-payment-required">
<a href="#section-15.5.3" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.3. </a><a href="#name-402-payment-required" class="section-name selfRef">402 Payment Required</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-402-payment-required-status" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.3-1">
   The 402 (Payment Required) status code is reserved for
   future use.<a href="#section-15.5.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.403">
<section id="section-15.5.4">
          <h4 id="name-403-forbidden">
<a href="#section-15.5.4" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.4. </a><a href="#name-403-forbidden" class="section-name selfRef">403 Forbidden</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-403-forbidden-status-code-4" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.4-1">
   The 403 (Forbidden) status code indicates that the
   server understood the request but refuses to fulfill it.
   A server that wishes to make public why the request has been forbidden
   can describe that reason in the response content (if any).<a href="#section-15.5.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.4-2">
   If authentication credentials were provided in the request, the
   server considers them insufficient to grant access.
   The client <span class="bcp14">SHOULD NOT</span> automatically repeat the request with the same
   credentials.
   The client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> repeat the request with new or different credentials.
   However, a request might be forbidden for reasons unrelated to the
   credentials.<a href="#section-15.5.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.4-3">
   An origin server that wishes to "hide" the current existence of a forbidden
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>
            <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> instead respond with a status
   code of <a href="#status.404" class="xref">404 (Not Found)</a>.<a href="#section-15.5.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.404">
<section id="section-15.5.5">
          <h4 id="name-404-not-found">
<a href="#section-15.5.5" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.5. </a><a href="#name-404-not-found" class="section-name selfRef">404 Not Found</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-404-not-found-status-code-4" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.5-1">
   The 404 (Not Found) status code indicates that the origin
   server did not find a current representation for the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> or is not willing to disclose that one
   exists. A 404 status code does not indicate whether this lack of representation
   is temporary or permanent; the <a href="#status.410" class="xref">410 (Gone)</a> status code is
   preferred over 404 if the origin server knows, presumably through some
   configurable means, that the condition is likely to be permanent.<a href="#section-15.5.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.5-2">
   A 404 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.5.5-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.405">
<section id="section-15.5.6">
          <h4 id="name-405-method-not-allowed">
<a href="#section-15.5.6" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.6. </a><a href="#name-405-method-not-allowed" class="section-name selfRef">405 Method Not Allowed</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-405-method-not-allowed-stat" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.6-1">
   The 405 (Method Not Allowed) status code indicates that the
   method received in the request-line is known by the origin server but
   not supported by the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>.
   The origin server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> generate an <a href="#field.allow" class="xref">Allow</a> header field in
   a 405 response containing a list of the target resource's currently
   supported methods.<a href="#section-15.5.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.6-2">
   A 405 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.5.6-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.406">
<section id="section-15.5.7">
          <h4 id="name-406-not-acceptable">
<a href="#section-15.5.7" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.7. </a><a href="#name-406-not-acceptable" class="section-name selfRef">406 Not Acceptable</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-406-not-acceptable-status-c" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.7-1">
   The 406 (Not Acceptable) status code indicates that the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> does not have a current representation that
   would be acceptable to the user agent, according to the
   <a href="#proactive.negotiation" class="xref">proactive negotiation</a> header fields received in the request
   (<a href="#proactive.negotiation" class="xref">Section 12.1</a>), and the server is unwilling to supply a
   default representation.<a href="#section-15.5.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.7-2">
   The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate content containing a list of available
   representation characteristics and corresponding resource identifiers from
   which the user or user agent can choose the one most appropriate.
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> automatically select the most appropriate choice from
   that list. However, this specification does not define any standard for
   such automatic selection, as described in <a href="#status.300" class="xref">Section 15.4.1</a>.<a href="#section-15.5.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.407">
<section id="section-15.5.8">
          <h4 id="name-407-proxy-authentication-re">
<a href="#section-15.5.8" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.8. </a><a href="#name-407-proxy-authentication-re" class="section-name selfRef">407 Proxy Authentication Required</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-407-proxy-authentication-re" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.8-1">
   The 407 (Proxy Authentication Required) status code is
   similar to <a href="#status.401" class="xref">401 (Unauthorized)</a>, but it indicates that the client
   needs to authenticate itself in order to use a proxy for this request.
   The proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send a <a href="#field.proxy-authenticate" class="xref">Proxy-Authenticate</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.proxy-authenticate" class="xref">Section 11.7.1</a>) containing a challenge
   applicable to that proxy for the request. The client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> repeat
   the request with a new or replaced <a href="#field.proxy-authorization" class="xref">Proxy-Authorization</a>
   header field (<a href="#field.proxy-authorization" class="xref">Section 11.7.2</a>).<a href="#section-15.5.8-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.408">
<section id="section-15.5.9">
          <h4 id="name-408-request-timeout">
<a href="#section-15.5.9" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.9. </a><a href="#name-408-request-timeout" class="section-name selfRef">408 Request Timeout</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-408-request-timeout-status-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.9-1">
   The 408 (Request Timeout) status code indicates
   that the server did not receive a complete request message within the time
   that it was prepared to wait.<a href="#section-15.5.9-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.9-2">
   If the client has an outstanding request in transit, it <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> repeat that
   request. If the current connection is not usable (e.g., as it would be in
   HTTP/1.1 because request delimitation is lost), a new connection will be
   used.<a href="#section-15.5.9-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.409">
<section id="section-15.5.10">
          <h4 id="name-409-conflict">
<a href="#section-15.5.10" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.10. </a><a href="#name-409-conflict" class="section-name selfRef">409 Conflict</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-409-conflict-status-code-43" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.10-1">
   The 409 (Conflict) status code indicates that the request
   could not be completed due to a conflict with the current state of the target
   resource. This code is used in situations where the user might be able to
   resolve the conflict and resubmit the request. The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate
   content that includes enough information for a user to recognize the
   source of the conflict.<a href="#section-15.5.10-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.10-2">
   Conflicts are most likely to occur in response to a PUT request. For
   example, if versioning were being used and the representation being PUT
   included changes to a resource that conflict with those made by an
   earlier (third-party) request, the origin server might use a 409 response
   to indicate that it can't complete the request. In this case, the response
   representation would likely contain information useful for merging the
   differences based on the revision history.<a href="#section-15.5.10-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.410">
<section id="section-15.5.11">
          <h4 id="name-410-gone">
<a href="#section-15.5.11" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.11. </a><a href="#name-410-gone" class="section-name selfRef">410 Gone</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-410-gone-status-code-434" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.11-1">
   The 410 (Gone) status code indicates that access to the
   <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a> is no longer available at the origin
   server and that this condition is likely to be permanent. If the origin
   server does not know, or has no facility to determine, whether or not the
   condition is permanent, the status code <a href="#status.404" class="xref">404 (Not Found)</a>
   ought to be used instead.<a href="#section-15.5.11-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.11-2">
   The 410 response is primarily intended to assist the task of web
   maintenance by notifying the recipient that the resource is
   intentionally unavailable and that the server owners desire that
   remote links to that resource be removed. Such an event is common for
   limited-time, promotional services and for resources belonging to
   individuals no longer associated with the origin server's site. It is not
   necessary to mark all permanently unavailable resources as "gone" or
   to keep the mark for any length of time -- that is left to the
   discretion of the server owner.<a href="#section-15.5.11-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.11-3">
   A 410 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.5.11-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.411">
<section id="section-15.5.12">
