          <h4 id="name-411-length-required">
<a href="#section-15.5.12" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.12. </a><a href="#name-411-length-required" class="section-name selfRef">411 Length Required</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-411-length-required-status-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.12-1">
   The 411 (Length Required) status code indicates that the
   server refuses to accept the request without a defined
   <a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Content-Length</a> (<a href="#field.content-length" class="xref">Section 8.6</a>).
   The client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> repeat the request if it adds a valid Content-Length
   header field containing the length of the request content.<a href="#section-15.5.12-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.412">
<section id="section-15.5.13">
          <h4 id="name-412-precondition-failed">
<a href="#section-15.5.13" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.13. </a><a href="#name-412-precondition-failed" class="section-name selfRef">412 Precondition Failed</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-412-precondition-failed-sta" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.13-1">
   The 412 (Precondition Failed) status code indicates that one
   or more conditions given in the request header fields evaluated to false
   when tested on the server (<a href="#conditional.requests" class="xref">Section 13</a>). This
   response status code allows the client to place preconditions on the
   current resource state (its current representations and metadata) and,
   thus, prevent the request method from being applied if the target resource
   is in an unexpected state.<a href="#section-15.5.13-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.413">
<section id="section-15.5.14">
          <h4 id="name-413-content-too-large">
<a href="#section-15.5.14" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.14. </a><a href="#name-413-content-too-large" class="section-name selfRef">413 Content Too Large</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-413-content-too-large-statu" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.14-1">
   The 413 (Content Too Large) status code indicates
   that the server is refusing to process a request because the request
   content is larger than the server is willing or able to process.
   The server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> terminate the request, if the protocol version in use
   allows it; otherwise, the server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> close the connection.<a href="#section-15.5.14-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.14-2">
   If the condition is temporary, the server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a
   <a href="#field.retry-after" class="xref">Retry-After</a> header field to indicate that it is temporary
   and after what time the client <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> try again.<a href="#section-15.5.14-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.414">
<section id="section-15.5.15">
          <h4 id="name-414-uri-too-long">
<a href="#section-15.5.15" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.15. </a><a href="#name-414-uri-too-long" class="section-name selfRef">414 URI Too Long</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-414-uri-too-long-status-cod" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.15-1">
   The 414 (URI Too Long) status code indicates that the server
   is refusing to service the request because the
   target URI is longer than the server is willing to
   interpret. This rare condition is only likely to occur when a client has
   improperly converted a POST request to a GET request with long query
   information, when the client has descended into an infinite loop of
   redirection (e.g., a redirected URI prefix that points to a suffix of
   itself) or when the server is under attack by a client attempting to
   exploit potential security holes.<a href="#section-15.5.15-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.15-2">
   A 414 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.5.15-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.415">
<section id="section-15.5.16">
          <h4 id="name-415-unsupported-media-type">
<a href="#section-15.5.16" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.16. </a><a href="#name-415-unsupported-media-type" class="section-name selfRef">415 Unsupported Media Type</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-415-unsupported-media-type-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.16-1">
   The 415 (Unsupported Media Type) status code indicates that
   the origin server is refusing to service the request because the content is
   in a format not supported by this method on the <a href="#target.resource" class="xref">target resource</a>.<a href="#section-15.5.16-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.16-2">
   The format problem might be due to the request's indicated
   <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> or <a href="#field.content-encoding" class="xref">Content-Encoding</a>, or as a
   result of inspecting the data directly.<a href="#section-15.5.16-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.16-3">
   If the problem was caused by an unsupported content coding, the
   <a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Accept-Encoding</a> response header field
   (<a href="#field.accept-encoding" class="xref">Section 12.5.3</a>) ought to be
   used to indicate which (if any) content codings would have been accepted
   in the request.<a href="#section-15.5.16-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.16-4">
   On the other hand, if the cause was an unsupported media type, the
   <a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Accept</a> response header field (<a href="#field.accept" class="xref">Section 12.5.1</a>)
   can be used to indicate which media types would have been accepted
   in the request.<a href="#section-15.5.16-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.416">
<section id="section-15.5.17">
          <h4 id="name-416-range-not-satisfiable">
<a href="#section-15.5.17" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.17. </a><a href="#name-416-range-not-satisfiable" class="section-name selfRef">416 Range Not Satisfiable</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-416-range-not-satisfiable-s" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.17-1">
   The 416 (Range Not Satisfiable) status code indicates that
   the set of ranges in the request's <a href="#field.range" class="xref">Range</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.range" class="xref">Section 14.2</a>) has been rejected either because none of
   the requested ranges are satisfiable or because the client has requested
   an excessive number of small or overlapping ranges (a potential denial of
   service attack).<a href="#section-15.5.17-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.17-2">
   Each range unit defines what is required for its own range sets to be
   satisfiable. For example, <a href="#byte.ranges" class="xref">Section 14.1.2</a> defines what makes
   a bytes range set satisfiable.<a href="#section-15.5.17-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.17-3">
   A server that generates a 416 response to a byte-range request <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span>
   generate a <a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Content-Range</a> header field
   specifying the current length of the selected representation
   (<a href="#field.content-range" class="xref">Section 14.4</a>).<a href="#section-15.5.17-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.17-4">
   For example:<a href="#section-15.5.17-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-15.5.17-5">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 416 Range Not Satisfiable
Date: Fri, 20 Jan 2012 15:41:54 GMT
Content-Range: bytes */47022
</pre><a href="#section-15.5.17-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
<aside id="section-15.5.17-6">
            <p id="section-15.5.17-6.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Because servers are free to ignore <a href="#field.range" class="xref">Range</a>, many
    implementations will respond with the entire selected representation
    in a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> response. That is partly because
    most clients are prepared to receive a <a href="#status.200" class="xref">200 (OK)</a> to
    complete the task (albeit less efficiently) and partly because clients
    might not stop making an invalid range request until they have received
    a complete representation. Thus, clients cannot depend on receiving a
    <a href="#status.416" class="xref">416 (Range Not Satisfiable)</a> response even when it is most
    appropriate.<a href="#section-15.5.17-6.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.417">
<section id="section-15.5.18">
          <h4 id="name-417-expectation-failed">
<a href="#section-15.5.18" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.18. </a><a href="#name-417-expectation-failed" class="section-name selfRef">417 Expectation Failed</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-417-expectation-failed-stat" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.18-1">
   The 417 (Expectation Failed) status code indicates that the
   expectation given in the request's <a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Expect</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.expect" class="xref">Section 10.1.1</a>) could not be met by at least one of the
   inbound servers.<a href="#section-15.5.18-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.418">
<section id="section-15.5.19">
          <h4 id="name-418-unused">
<a href="#section-15.5.19" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.19. </a><a href="#name-418-unused" class="section-name selfRef">418 (Unused)</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-418-unused-status-code-442" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.19-1">
                  <span>[<a href="#RFC2324" class="xref">RFC2324</a>]</span> was an April 1 RFC that lampooned the various ways
   HTTP was abused; one such abuse was the definition of an
   application-specific 418 status code, which has been deployed as a joke
   often enough for the code to be unusable for any future use.<a href="#section-15.5.19-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.19-2">
   Therefore, the 418 status code is reserved in the IANA HTTP Status Code
   Registry. This indicates that the status code cannot be assigned to other
   applications currently. If future circumstances require its use (e.g.,
   exhaustion of 4NN status codes), it can be re-assigned to another use.<a href="#section-15.5.19-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.421">
<section id="section-15.5.20">
          <h4 id="name-421-misdirected-request">
<a href="#section-15.5.20" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.20. </a><a href="#name-421-misdirected-request" class="section-name selfRef">421 Misdirected Request</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-421-misdirected-request-sta" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.20-1">
   The 421 (Misdirected Request) status code indicates that the request was
   directed at a server that is unable or unwilling to produce an
   authoritative response for the target URI. An origin server (or gateway
   acting on behalf of the origin server) sends 421 to reject a target URI
   that does not match an <a href="#origin" class="xref">origin</a> for which the server has been
   configured (<a href="#origin" class="xref">Section 4.3.1</a>) or does not match the connection
   context over which the request was received
   (<a href="#routing.reject" class="xref">Section 7.4</a>).<a href="#section-15.5.20-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.20-2">
   A client that receives a 421 (Misdirected Request) response <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> retry the
   request, whether or not the request method is idempotent, over a different
   connection, such as a fresh connection specific to the target resource's
   origin, or via an alternative service <span>[<a href="#ALTSVC" class="xref">ALTSVC</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-15.5.20-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.20-3">
   A proxy <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> generate a 421 response.<a href="#section-15.5.20-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.422">
<section id="section-15.5.21">
          <h4 id="name-422-unprocessable-content">
<a href="#section-15.5.21" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.21. </a><a href="#name-422-unprocessable-content" class="section-name selfRef">422 Unprocessable Content</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-422-unprocessable-content-s" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.21-1">
   The 422 (Unprocessable Content) status code indicates that the server
   understands the content type of the request content (hence a
   <a href="#status.415" class="xref">415 (Unsupported Media Type)</a> status code is inappropriate),
   and the syntax of the request content is correct, but it was unable to process
   the contained instructions. For example, this status code can be sent if
   an XML request content contains well-formed (i.e., syntactically correct), but
   semantically erroneous XML instructions.<a href="#section-15.5.21-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.426">
<section id="section-15.5.22">
          <h4 id="name-426-upgrade-required">
<a href="#section-15.5.22" class="section-number selfRef">15.5.22. </a><a href="#name-426-upgrade-required" class="section-name selfRef">426 Upgrade Required</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-426-upgrade-required-status" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.5.22-1">
   The 426 (Upgrade Required) status code indicates that the
   server refuses to perform the request using the current protocol but might
   be willing to do so after the client upgrades to a different protocol.
   The server <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> send an <a href="#field.upgrade" class="xref">Upgrade</a> header field in a 426
   response to indicate the required protocol(s) (<a href="#field.upgrade" class="xref">Section 7.8</a>).<a href="#section-15.5.22-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.5.22-2">
   Example:<a href="#section-15.5.22-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="section-15.5.22-3">
<pre class="lang-http-message sourcecode">HTTP/1.1 426 Upgrade Required
Upgrade: HTTP/3.0
Connection: Upgrade
Content-Length: 53
Content-Type: text/plain

This service requires use of the HTTP/3.0 protocol.
</pre><a href="#section-15.5.22-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.5xx">
<section id="section-15.6">
        <h3 id="name-server-error-5xx">
<a href="#section-15.6" class="section-number selfRef">15.6. </a><a href="#name-server-error-5xx" class="section-name selfRef">Server Error 5xx</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-5xx-server-error-status-cod" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-status-codes-classes-5xx-se" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-15.6-1">
   The 5xx (Server Error) class of status code indicates that
   the server is aware that it has erred or is incapable of performing the
   requested method.
   Except when responding to a HEAD request, the server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> send a
   representation containing an explanation of the error situation, and
   whether it is a temporary or permanent condition.
   A user agent <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> display any included representation to the user.
   These status codes are applicable to any request method.<a href="#section-15.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="status.500">
<section id="section-15.6.1">
          <h4 id="name-500-internal-server-error">
<a href="#section-15.6.1" class="section-number selfRef">15.6.1. </a><a href="#name-500-internal-server-error" class="section-name selfRef">500 Internal Server Error</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-500-internal-server-error-s" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.6.1-1">
   The 500 (Internal Server Error) status code indicates that
   the server encountered an unexpected condition that prevented it from
   fulfilling the request.<a href="#section-15.6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.501">
<section id="section-15.6.2">
          <h4 id="name-501-not-implemented">
<a href="#section-15.6.2" class="section-number selfRef">15.6.2. </a><a href="#name-501-not-implemented" class="section-name selfRef">501 Not Implemented</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-501-not-implemented-status-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.6.2-1">
   The 501 (Not Implemented) status code indicates that the
   server does not support the functionality required to fulfill the request.
   This is the appropriate response when the server does not recognize the
   request method and is not capable of supporting it for any resource.<a href="#section-15.6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-15.6.2-2">
   A 501 response is heuristically cacheable; i.e., unless otherwise indicated by
   the method definition or explicit cache controls (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-4.2.2" class="relref">Section 4.2.2</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-15.6.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.502">
<section id="section-15.6.3">
          <h4 id="name-502-bad-gateway">
<a href="#section-15.6.3" class="section-number selfRef">15.6.3. </a><a href="#name-502-bad-gateway" class="section-name selfRef">502 Bad Gateway</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-502-bad-gateway-status-code" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.6.3-1">
   The 502 (Bad Gateway) status code indicates that the server,
   while acting as a gateway or proxy, received an invalid response from an
   inbound server it accessed while attempting to fulfill the request.<a href="#section-15.6.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.503">
<section id="section-15.6.4">
          <h4 id="name-503-service-unavailable">
<a href="#section-15.6.4" class="section-number selfRef">15.6.4. </a><a href="#name-503-service-unavailable" class="section-name selfRef">503 Service Unavailable</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-503-service-unavailable-sta" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.6.4-1">
   The 503 (Service Unavailable) status code indicates that the
   server is currently unable to handle the request due to a temporary overload
   or scheduled maintenance, which will likely be alleviated after some delay.
   The server <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> send a <a href="#field.retry-after" class="xref">Retry-After</a> header field
   (<a href="#field.retry-after" class="xref">Section 10.2.3</a>) to suggest an appropriate
   amount of time for the client to wait before retrying the request.<a href="#section-15.6.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-15.6.4-2">
            <p id="section-15.6.4-2.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> The existence of the 503 status code does not imply that a
    server has to use it when becoming overloaded. Some servers might
    simply refuse the connection.<a href="#section-15.6.4-2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.504">
<section id="section-15.6.5">
          <h4 id="name-504-gateway-timeout">
<a href="#section-15.6.5" class="section-number selfRef">15.6.5. </a><a href="#name-504-gateway-timeout" class="section-name selfRef">504 Gateway Timeout</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-504-gateway-timeout-status-" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.6.5-1">
   The 504 (Gateway Timeout) status code indicates that the
   server, while acting as a gateway or proxy, did not receive a timely
   response from an upstream server it needed to access in order to
   complete the request.<a href="#section-15.6.5-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.505">
<section id="section-15.6.6">
          <h4 id="name-505-http-version-not-suppor">
<a href="#section-15.6.6" class="section-number selfRef">15.6.6. </a><a href="#name-505-http-version-not-suppor" class="section-name selfRef">505 HTTP Version Not Supported</a>
          </h4>
<span id="iref-505-http-version-not-suppor" class="iref"></span>
          <p id="section-15.6.6-1">
   The 505 (HTTP Version Not Supported) status code indicates
   that the server does not support, or refuses to support, the major version
   of HTTP that was used in the request message. The server is indicating that
   it is unable or unwilling to complete the request using the same major
   version as the client, as described in <a href="#protocol.version" class="xref">Section 2.5</a>, other than with this
   error message. The server <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> generate a representation for the 505
   response that describes why that version is not supported and what other
   protocols are supported by that server.<a href="#section-15.6.6-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="extending">
<section id="section-16">
      <h2 id="name-extending-http">
<a href="#section-16" class="section-number selfRef">16. </a><a href="#name-extending-http" class="section-name selfRef">Extending HTTP</a>
      </h2>
<p id="section-16-1">
   HTTP defines a number of generic extension points that can be used to
   introduce capabilities to the protocol without introducing a new version,
   including methods, status codes, field names, and further extensibility
   points within defined fields, such as authentication schemes and
   cache directives (see Cache-Control extensions in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2.3" class="relref">Section 5.2.3</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>). Because the semantics of HTTP are
   not versioned, these extension points are persistent; the version of the
   protocol in use does not affect their semantics.<a href="#section-16-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16-2">
   Version-independent extensions are discouraged from depending on or
   interacting with the specific version of the protocol in use. When this is
   unavoidable, careful consideration needs to be given to how the extension
   can interoperate across versions.<a href="#section-16-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16-3">
   Additionally, specific versions of HTTP might have their own extensibility
   points, such as transfer codings in HTTP/1.1 (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-6.1" class="relref">Section 6.1</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>) and HTTP/2 SETTINGS or frame types
   (<span>[<a href="#HTTP2" class="xref">HTTP/2</a>]</span>). These extension points are specific to the
   version of the protocol they occur within.<a href="#section-16-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16-4">
   Version-specific extensions cannot override or modify the semantics of
   a version-independent mechanism or extension point (like a method or
   header field) without explicitly being allowed by that protocol element. For
   example, the CONNECT method (<a href="#CONNECT" class="xref">Section 9.3.6</a>) allows this.<a href="#section-16-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16-5">
   These guidelines assure that the protocol operates correctly and
   predictably, even when parts of the path implement different versions of
   HTTP.<a href="#section-16-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="method.extensibility">
<section id="section-16.1">
        <h3 id="name-method-extensibility">
<a href="#section-16.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.1. </a><a href="#name-method-extensibility" class="section-name selfRef">Method Extensibility</a>
        </h3>
<div id="method.registry">
<section id="section-16.1.1">
          <h4 id="name-method-registry">
<a href="#section-16.1.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.1.1. </a><a href="#name-method-registry" class="section-name selfRef">Method Registry</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.1.1-1">
  The "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Method Registry", maintained by
  IANA at <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-methods">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-methods</a>></span>,
  registers <a href="#method.overview" class="xref">method</a> names.<a href="#section-16.1.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.1.1-2">
  HTTP method registrations <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> include the following fields:<a href="#section-16.1.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.1.1-3.1">Method Name (see <a href="#methods" class="xref">Section 9</a>)<a href="#section-16.1.1-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.1.1-3.2">Safe ("yes" or "no", see <a href="#safe.methods" class="xref">Section 9.2.1</a>)<a href="#section-16.1.1-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.1.1-3.3">Idempotent ("yes" or "no", see <a href="#idempotent.methods" class="xref">Section 9.2.2</a>)<a href="#section-16.1.1-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.1.1-3.4">Pointer to specification text<a href="#section-16.1.1-3.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-16.1.1-4">
  Values to be added to this namespace require IETF Review
  (see <span>[<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.8" class="relref">Section 4.8</a></span>).<a href="#section-16.1.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.methods">
<section id="section-16.1.2">
          <h4 id="name-considerations-for-new-meth">
<a href="#section-16.1.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.1.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-meth" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Methods</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.1.2-1">
   Standardized methods are generic; that is, they are potentially
   applicable to any resource, not just one particular media type, kind of
   resource, or application. As such, it is preferred that new methods
   be registered in a document that isn't specific to a single application or
   data format, since orthogonal technologies deserve orthogonal specification.<a href="#section-16.1.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.1.2-2">
   Since message parsing (<a href="#message.abstraction" class="xref">Section 6</a>) needs to be
   independent of method
   semantics (aside from responses to HEAD), definitions of new methods
   cannot change the parsing algorithm or prohibit the presence of content
   on either the request or the response message.
   Definitions of new methods can specify that only a zero-length content
   is allowed by requiring a Content-Length header field with a value of "0".<a href="#section-16.1.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.1.2-3">
   Likewise, new methods cannot use the special host:port and asterisk forms of
   request target that are allowed for <a href="#CONNECT" class="xref">CONNECT</a> and
   <a href="#OPTIONS" class="xref">OPTIONS</a>, respectively (<a href="#target.resource" class="xref">Section 7.1</a>).
   A full URI in absolute form is needed for the target URI, which means either
   the request target needs to be sent in absolute form or the target URI will
   be reconstructed from the request context in the same way it is for other
   methods.<a href="#section-16.1.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.1.2-4">
   A new method definition needs to indicate whether it is safe (<a href="#safe.methods" class="xref">Section 9.2.1</a>), idempotent (<a href="#idempotent.methods" class="xref">Section 9.2.2</a>),
   cacheable (<a href="#cacheable.methods" class="xref">Section 9.2.3</a>), what
   semantics are to be associated with the request content (if any), and what
   refinements the method makes to header field or status code semantics.
   If the new method is cacheable, its definition ought to describe how, and
   under what conditions, a cache can store a response and use it to satisfy a
   subsequent request.
   The new method ought to describe whether it can be made conditional
   (<a href="#preconditions" class="xref">Section 13.1</a>) and, if so, how a server responds
   when the condition is false.
   Likewise, if the new method might have some use for partial response
   semantics (<a href="#field.range" class="xref">Section 14.2</a>), it ought to document this, too.<a href="#section-16.1.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<aside id="section-16.1.2-5">
            <p id="section-16.1.2-5.1">
                     <strong>Note:</strong> Avoid defining a method name that starts with "M-", since that
    prefix might be misinterpreted as having the semantics assigned to it
    by <span>[<a href="#RFC2774" class="xref">RFC2774</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-16.1.2-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</aside>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="status.code.extensibility">
<section id="section-16.2">
        <h3 id="name-status-code-extensibility">
<a href="#section-16.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.2. </a><a href="#name-status-code-extensibility" class="section-name selfRef">Status Code Extensibility</a>
        </h3>
<div id="status.code.registry">
<section id="section-16.2.1">
          <h4 id="name-status-code-registry">
<a href="#section-16.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.2.1. </a><a href="#name-status-code-registry" class="section-name selfRef">Status Code Registry</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.2.1-1">
   The "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Status Code Registry", maintained
   by IANA at <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-status-codes">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-status-codes</a>></span>,
   registers status code numbers.<a href="#section-16.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.1-2">
   A registration <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> include the following fields:<a href="#section-16.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.2.1-3.1">Status Code (3 digits)<a href="#section-16.2.1-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.2.1-3.2">Short Description<a href="#section-16.2.1-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.2.1-3.3">Pointer to specification text<a href="#section-16.2.1-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-16.2.1-4">
   Values to be added to the HTTP status code namespace require IETF Review
   (see <span>[<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.8" class="relref">Section 4.8</a></span>).<a href="#section-16.2.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.status.codes">
<section id="section-16.2.2">
          <h4 id="name-considerations-for-new-stat">
<a href="#section-16.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.2.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-stat" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Status Codes</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.2.2-1">
   When it is necessary to express semantics for a response that are not
   defined by current status codes, a new status code can be registered.
   Status codes are generic; they are potentially applicable to any resource,
   not just one particular media type, kind of resource, or application of
   HTTP. As such, it is preferred that new status codes be registered in a
   document that isn't specific to a single application.<a href="#section-16.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.2-2">
   New status codes are required to fall under one of the categories
   defined in <a href="#status.codes" class="xref">Section 15</a>. To allow existing parsers to
   process the response message, new status codes cannot disallow content,
   although they can mandate a zero-length content.<a href="#section-16.2.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.2-3">
   Proposals for new status codes that are not yet widely deployed ought to
   avoid allocating a specific number for the code until there is clear
   consensus that it will be registered; instead, early drafts can use a
   notation such as "4NN", or "3N0" .. "3N9", to indicate the class
   of the proposed status code(s) without consuming a number prematurely.<a href="#section-16.2.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.2-4">
   The definition of a new status code ought to explain the request
   conditions that would cause a response containing that status code (e.g.,
   combinations of request header fields and/or method(s)) along with any
   dependencies on response header fields (e.g., what fields are required,
   what fields can modify the semantics, and what field semantics are
   further refined when used with the new status code).<a href="#section-16.2.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.2-5">
   By default, a status code applies only to the request corresponding to the
   response it occurs within. If a status code applies to a larger scope of
   applicability -- for example, all requests to the resource in question or
   all requests to a server -- this must be explicitly specified. When doing
   so, it should be noted that not all clients can be expected to
   consistently apply a larger scope because they might not understand the
   new status code.<a href="#section-16.2.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.2-6">
   The definition of a new final status code ought to specify whether or not it
   is heuristically cacheable. Note that any response with a final status code
   can be cached if the response has explicit freshness information. A status
   code defined as heuristically cacheable is allowed to be cached without
   explicit freshness information.
   Likewise, the definition of a status code can place
   constraints upon cache behavior if the must-understand cache
   directive is used. See <span>[<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span> for more information.<a href="#section-16.2.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.2.2-7">
   Finally, the definition of a new status code ought to indicate whether the
   content has any implied association with an identified resource (<a href="#identifying.content" class="xref">Section 6.4.2</a>).<a href="#section-16.2.2-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="fields.extensibility">
<section id="section-16.3">
        <h3 id="name-field-extensibility">
<a href="#section-16.3" class="section-number selfRef">16.3. </a><a href="#name-field-extensibility" class="section-name selfRef">Field Extensibility</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-16.3-1">
  HTTP's most widely used extensibility point is the definition of new header and
  trailer fields.<a href="#section-16.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3-2">
  New fields can be defined such that, when they are understood by a
  recipient, they override or enhance the interpretation of previously
  defined fields, define preconditions on request evaluation, or
  refine the meaning of responses.<a href="#section-16.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3-3">
   However, defining a field doesn't guarantee its deployment or recognition
   by recipients. Most fields are designed with the expectation that a recipient
   can safely ignore (but forward downstream) any field not recognized.
   In other cases, the sender's ability to understand a given field might be
   indicated by its prior communication, perhaps in the protocol version
   or fields that it sent in prior messages, or its use of a specific media type.
   Likewise, direct inspection of support might be possible through an
   OPTIONS request or by interacting with a defined well-known URI
   <span>[<a href="#RFC8615" class="xref">RFC8615</a>]</span> if such inspection is defined along with
   the field being introduced.<a href="#section-16.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="fields.registry">
<section id="section-16.3.1">
          <h4 id="name-field-name-registry">
<a href="#section-16.3.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.3.1. </a><a href="#name-field-name-registry" class="section-name selfRef">Field Name Registry</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.3.1-1">
   The "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Field Name Registry" defines the
   namespace for HTTP field names.<a href="#section-16.3.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-2">
   Any party can request registration of an HTTP field. See <a href="#considerations.for.new.fields" class="xref">Section 16.3.2</a> for considerations to take
   into account when creating a new HTTP field.<a href="#section-16.3.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-3">
   The "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Field Name Registry" is located at
   <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-fields/">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-fields/</a>></span>.
   Registration requests can be made by following the instructions located
   there or by sending an email to the "ietf-http-wg@w3.org" mailing list.<a href="#section-16.3.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-4">
   Field names are registered on the advice of a designated expert
   (appointed by the IESG or their delegate). Fields with the status
   'permanent' are Specification Required
   (<span>[<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.6" class="relref">Section 4.6</a></span>).<a href="#section-16.3.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-5">
   Registration requests consist of the following information:<a href="#section-16.3.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span class="break"></span><dl class="dlNewline" id="section-16.3.1-6">
            <dt id="section-16.3.1-6.1">Field name:</dt>
            <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-16.3.1-6.2">
    The requested field name. It <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> conform to the
    field-name syntax defined in <a href="#fields.names" class="xref">Section 5.1</a>, and it <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> be
    restricted to just letters, digits, and hyphen ('-')
    characters, with the first character being a letter.<a href="#section-16.3.1-6.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
            <dd class="break"></dd>
<dt id="section-16.3.1-6.3">Status:</dt>
            <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-16.3.1-6.4">
    "permanent", "provisional", "deprecated", or "obsoleted".<a href="#section-16.3.1-6.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
            <dd class="break"></dd>
<dt id="section-16.3.1-6.5">Specification document(s):</dt>
            <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-16.3.1-6.6">
    Reference to the document that specifies
    the field, preferably including a URI that can be used to retrieve
    a copy of the document. Optional but encouraged for provisional registrations.
    An indication of the relevant section(s) can also be included, but is not required.<a href="#section-16.3.1-6.6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
          <dd class="break"></dd>
</dl>
<p id="section-16.3.1-7">
   And optionally:<a href="#section-16.3.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<span class="break"></span><dl class="dlParallel" id="section-16.3.1-8">
            <dt id="section-16.3.1-8.1">Comments:</dt>
            <dd style="margin-left: 1.5em" id="section-16.3.1-8.2">
    Additional information, such as about reserved entries.<a href="#section-16.3.1-8.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</dd>
          <dd class="break"></dd>
</dl>
<p id="section-16.3.1-9">
   The expert(s) can define additional fields to be collected in the
   registry, in consultation with the community.<a href="#section-16.3.1-9" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-10">
   Standards-defined names have a status of "permanent". Other names can also
   be registered as permanent if the expert(s) finds that they are in use, in
   consultation with the community. Other names should be registered as
   "provisional".<a href="#section-16.3.1-10" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-11">
   Provisional entries can be removed by the expert(s) if -- in consultation
   with the community -- the expert(s) find that they are not in use. The
   expert(s) can change a provisional entry's status to permanent at any time.<a href="#section-16.3.1-11" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.1-12">
   Note that names can be registered by third parties (including the
   expert(s)) if the expert(s) determines that an unregistered name is widely
   deployed and not likely to be registered in a timely manner otherwise.<a href="#section-16.3.1-12" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.fields">
<section id="section-16.3.2">
          <h4 id="name-considerations-for-new-fiel">
<a href="#section-16.3.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.3.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-fiel" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Fields</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.3.2-1">
   HTTP header and trailer fields are a widely used extension point for the protocol.
   While they can be used in an ad hoc fashion, fields that are intended for
   wider use need to be carefully documented to ensure interoperability.<a href="#section-16.3.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2-2">
   In particular, authors of specifications defining new fields are advised to consider
   and, where appropriate, document the following aspects:<a href="#section-16.3.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.1">Under what conditions the field can be used; e.g., only in
     responses or requests, in all messages, only on responses to a
     particular request method, etc.<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.2">Whether the field semantics are further refined by their context,
     such as their use with certain request methods or status codes.<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.3">The scope of applicability for the information conveyed.
     By default, fields apply only to the message they are
     associated with, but some response fields are designed to apply to all
     representations of a resource, the resource itself, or an even broader
     scope. Specifications that expand the scope of a response field will
     need to carefully consider issues such as content negotiation, the time
     period of applicability, and (in some cases) multi-tenant server
     deployments.<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.4">Under what conditions intermediaries are allowed to insert,
     delete, or modify the field's value.<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.5">If the field is allowable in trailers; by
     default, it will not be (see <a href="#trailers.limitations" class="xref">Section 6.5.1</a>).<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.6">Whether it is appropriate or even required to list the field name in the
     <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Connection</a> header field (i.e., if the field is to
     be hop-by-hop; see <a href="#field.connection" class="xref">Section 7.6.1</a>).<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-3.7">Whether the field introduces any additional security considerations, such
     as disclosure of privacy-related data.<a href="#section-16.3.2-3.7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-16.3.2-4">
   Request header fields have additional considerations that need to be documented
   if the default behavior is not appropriate:<a href="#section-16.3.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-5.1">If it is appropriate to list the field name in a
     <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Vary</a> response header field (e.g., when the request header
     field is used by an origin server's content selection algorithm; see
     <a href="#field.vary" class="xref">Section 12.5.5</a>).<a href="#section-16.3.2-5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-5.2">If the field is intended to be stored when received in a PUT
   request (see <a href="#PUT" class="xref">Section 9.3.4</a>).<a href="#section-16.3.2-5.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.3.2-5.3">If the field ought to be removed when automatically redirecting a
   request due to security concerns (see <a href="#status.3xx" class="xref">Section 15.4</a>).<a href="#section-16.3.2-5.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<div id="considerations.for.new.field.names">
<section id="section-16.3.2.1">
            <h5 id="name-considerations-for-new-field">
<a href="#section-16.3.2.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.3.2.1. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-field" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Field Names</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-16.3.2.1-1">
   Authors of specifications defining new fields are advised to choose a short
   but descriptive field name. Short names avoid needless data transmission;
   descriptive names avoid confusion and "squatting" on names that might have
   broader uses.<a href="#section-16.3.2.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.1-2">
   To that end, limited-use fields (such as a header confined to a single
   application or use case) are encouraged to use a name that includes that use
   (or an abbreviation) as a prefix; for example, if the Foo Application needs
   a Description field, it might use "Foo-Desc"; "Description" is too generic,
   and "Foo-Description" is needlessly long.<a href="#section-16.3.2.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.1-3">
   While the field-name syntax is defined to allow any token character, in
   practice some implementations place limits on the characters they accept
   in field-names. To be interoperable, new field names <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> constrain
   themselves to alphanumeric characters, "-", and ".", and <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span>
   begin with a letter. For example, the underscore
   ("_") character can be problematic when passed through non-HTTP
   gateway interfaces (see <a href="#underscore.in.fields" class="xref">Section 17.10</a>).<a href="#section-16.3.2.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.1-4">
   Field names ought not be prefixed with "X-"; see
   <span>[<a href="#BCP178" class="xref">BCP178</a>]</span> for further information.<a href="#section-16.3.2.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.1-5">
   Other prefixes are sometimes used in HTTP field names; for example,
   "Accept-" is used in many content negotiation headers, and "Content-" is used
   as explained in <a href="#content" class="xref">Section 6.4</a>. These prefixes are
   only an aid to recognizing the purpose of a field and do not
   trigger automatic processing.<a href="#section-16.3.2.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.field.values">
<section id="section-16.3.2.2">
            <h5 id="name-considerations-for-new-field-">
<a href="#section-16.3.2.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.3.2.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-field-" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Field Values</a>
            </h5>
<p id="section-16.3.2.2-1">
   A major task in the definition of a new HTTP field is the specification of
   the field value syntax: what senders should generate, and how recipients
   should infer semantics from what is received.<a href="#section-16.3.2.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.2-2">
   Authors are encouraged (but not required) to use either the ABNF rules in
   this specification or those in <span>[<a href="#RFC8941" class="xref">RFC8941</a>]</span> to define the syntax
   of new field values.<a href="#section-16.3.2.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.2-3">
   Authors are advised to carefully consider how the combination of multiple
   field lines will impact them (see <a href="#fields.order" class="xref">Section 5.3</a>). Because
   senders might erroneously send multiple values, and both intermediaries
   and HTTP libraries can perform combination automatically, this applies to
   all field values -- even when only a single value is anticipated.<a href="#section-16.3.2.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.2-4">
   Therefore, authors are advised to delimit or encode values that contain
   commas (e.g., with the <a href="#rule.quoted-string" class="xref">quoted-string</a> rule of
   <a href="#quoted.strings" class="xref">Section 5.6.4</a>, the String data type of
   <span>[<a href="#RFC8941" class="xref">RFC8941</a>]</span>, or a field-specific encoding).
   This ensures that commas within field data are not confused
   with the commas that delimit a list value.<a href="#section-16.3.2.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.2-5">
   For example, the <a href="#field.content-type" class="xref">Content-Type</a> field value only allows commas
   inside quoted strings, which can be reliably parsed even when multiple
   values are present. The <a href="#field.location" class="xref">Location</a> field value provides a
   counter-example that should not be emulated: because URIs can include
   commas, it is not possible to reliably distinguish between a single value
   that includes a comma from two values.<a href="#section-16.3.2.2-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.3.2.2-6">
   Authors of fields with a singleton value (see <a href="#fields.values" class="xref">Section 5.5</a>) are additionally advised to document how to treat
   messages where the multiple members are present (a sensible default would
   be to ignore the field, but this might not always be the right choice).<a href="#section-16.3.2.2-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="auth.scheme.extensibility">
<section id="section-16.4">
        <h3 id="name-authentication-scheme-exten">
<a href="#section-16.4" class="section-number selfRef">16.4. </a><a href="#name-authentication-scheme-exten" class="section-name selfRef">Authentication Scheme Extensibility</a>
        </h3>
<div id="auth.scheme.registry">
<section id="section-16.4.1">
          <h4 id="name-authentication-scheme-regis">
<a href="#section-16.4.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.4.1. </a><a href="#name-authentication-scheme-regis" class="section-name selfRef">Authentication Scheme Registry</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.4.1-1">
   The "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Authentication Scheme Registry"
   defines the namespace for the authentication schemes in challenges and
   credentials. It is maintained
   at <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-authschemes">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-authschemes</a>></span>.<a href="#section-16.4.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.4.1-2">
  Registrations <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> include the following fields:<a href="#section-16.4.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.4.1-3.1">Authentication Scheme Name<a href="#section-16.4.1-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.1-3.2">Pointer to specification text<a href="#section-16.4.1-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.1-3.3">Notes (optional)<a href="#section-16.4.1-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-16.4.1-4">
  Values to be added to this namespace require IETF Review
  (see <span>[<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.8" class="relref">Section 4.8</a></span>).<a href="#section-16.4.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.auth.schemes">
<section id="section-16.4.2">
          <h4 id="name-considerations-for-new-auth">
<a href="#section-16.4.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.4.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-auth" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Authentication Schemes</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.4.2-1">
  There are certain aspects of the HTTP Authentication framework that put
  constraints on how new authentication schemes can work:<a href="#section-16.4.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.1">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.1.1">
    HTTP authentication is presumed to be stateless: all of the information
    necessary to authenticate a request <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> be provided in the request,
    rather than be dependent on the server remembering prior requests.
    Authentication based on, or bound to, the underlying connection is
    outside the scope of this specification and inherently flawed unless
    steps are taken to ensure that the connection cannot be used by any
    party other than the authenticated user
    (see <a href="#connections" class="xref">Section 3.3</a>).<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.1.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.2">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.2.1">
      The authentication parameter "realm" is reserved for defining protection
      spaces as described in <a href="#protection.space" class="xref">Section 11.5</a>. New schemes
      <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> use it in a way incompatible with that definition.<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.2.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.3">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.3.1">
      The "token68" notation was introduced for compatibility with existing
      authentication schemes and can only be used once per challenge or credential.
      Thus, new schemes ought to use the auth-param syntax instead, because
      otherwise future extensions will be impossible.<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.4">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.4.1">
      The parsing of challenges and credentials is defined by this specification
      and cannot be modified by new authentication schemes. When the auth-param
      syntax is used, all parameters ought to support both token and
      quoted-string syntax, and syntactical constraints ought to be defined on
      the field value after parsing (i.e., quoted-string processing). This is
      necessary so that recipients can use a generic parser that applies to
      all authentication schemes.<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.4.2-2.4.2">
                        <strong>Note:</strong> The fact that the value syntax for the "realm" parameter
      is restricted to quoted-string was a bad design choice not to be repeated
      for new parameters.<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.4.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.5">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.5.1">
      Definitions of new schemes ought to define the treatment of unknown
      extension parameters. In general, a "must-ignore" rule is preferable
      to a "must-understand" rule, because otherwise it will be hard to introduce
      new parameters in the presence of legacy recipients. Furthermore,
      it's good to describe the policy for defining new parameters (such
      as "update the specification" or "use this registry").<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.5.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.6">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.6.1">
      Authentication schemes need to document whether they are usable in
      origin-server authentication (i.e., using <a href="#field.www-authenticate" class="xref">WWW-Authenticate</a>),
      and/or proxy authentication (i.e., using <a href="#field.proxy-authenticate" class="xref">Proxy-Authenticate</a>).<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.6.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.7">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.7.1">
      The credentials carried in an <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> header field are specific to
      the user agent and, therefore, have the same effect on HTTP caches as the
      "private" cache response directive (<span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-5.2.2.7" class="relref">Section 5.2.2.7</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>),
      within the scope of the request in which they appear.<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.7.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.4.2-2.7.2">
      Therefore, new authentication schemes that choose not to carry
      credentials in the <a href="#field.authorization" class="xref">Authorization</a> header field (e.g., using a newly defined
      header field) will need to explicitly disallow caching, by mandating the use of
      cache response directives (e.g., "private").<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.7.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.4.2-2.8">
              <p id="section-16.4.2-2.8.1">
      Schemes using <a href="#field.authentication-info" class="xref">Authentication-Info</a>, <a href="#field.proxy-authentication-info" class="xref">Proxy-Authentication-Info</a>,
      or any other authentication related response header field need to
      consider and document the related security considerations (see
      <a href="#security.auth.add.resp" class="xref">Section 17.16.4</a>).<a href="#section-16.4.2-2.8.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</li>
          </ul>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="range.unit.extensibility">
<section id="section-16.5">
        <h3 id="name-range-unit-extensibility">
<a href="#section-16.5" class="section-number selfRef">16.5. </a><a href="#name-range-unit-extensibility" class="section-name selfRef">Range Unit Extensibility</a>
        </h3>
<div id="range.unit.registry">
<section id="section-16.5.1">
          <h4 id="name-range-unit-registry">
<a href="#section-16.5.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.5.1. </a><a href="#name-range-unit-registry" class="section-name selfRef">Range Unit Registry</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.5.1-1">
   The "HTTP Range Unit Registry" defines the namespace for the range
   unit names and refers to their corresponding specifications.
   It is maintained at
   <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-parameters">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-parameters</a>></span>.<a href="#section-16.5.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.5.1-2">
   Registration of an HTTP Range Unit <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> include the following fields:<a href="#section-16.5.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.5.1-3.1">Name<a href="#section-16.5.1-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.5.1-3.2">Description<a href="#section-16.5.1-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.5.1-3.3">Pointer to specification text<a href="#section-16.5.1-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-16.5.1-4">
  Values to be added to this namespace require IETF Review
  (see <span>[<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>], <a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.8" class="relref">Section 4.8</a></span>).<a href="#section-16.5.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.range.units">
<section id="section-16.5.2">
          <h4 id="name-considerations-for-new-rang">
<a href="#section-16.5.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.5.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-rang" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Range Units</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.5.2-1">
   Other range units, such as format-specific boundaries like pages,
   sections, records, rows, or time, are potentially usable in HTTP for
   application-specific purposes, but are not commonly used in practice.
   Implementors of alternative range units ought to consider how they would
   work with content codings and general-purpose intermediaries.<a href="#section-16.5.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="content.coding.extensibility">
<section id="section-16.6">
        <h3 id="name-content-coding-extensibilit">
<a href="#section-16.6" class="section-number selfRef">16.6. </a><a href="#name-content-coding-extensibilit" class="section-name selfRef">Content Coding Extensibility</a>
        </h3>
<div id="content.coding.registry">
<section id="section-16.6.1">
          <h4 id="name-content-coding-registry">
<a href="#section-16.6.1" class="section-number selfRef">16.6.1. </a><a href="#name-content-coding-registry" class="section-name selfRef">Content Coding Registry</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.6.1-1">
   The "HTTP Content Coding Registry", maintained by
   IANA at <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-parameters/">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-parameters/</a>></span>,
   registers <a href="#content.codings" class="xref">content-coding</a> names.<a href="#section-16.6.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.6.1-2">
   Content coding registrations <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> include the following fields:<a href="#section-16.6.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ul class="normal">
<li class="normal" id="section-16.6.1-3.1">Name<a href="#section-16.6.1-3.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.6.1-3.2">Description<a href="#section-16.6.1-3.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
            <li class="normal" id="section-16.6.1-3.3">Pointer to specification text<a href="#section-16.6.1-3.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          </ul>
<p id="section-16.6.1-4">
   Names of content codings <span class="bcp14">MUST NOT</span> overlap with names of transfer codings
   (per the "HTTP Transfer Coding Registry" located at
   <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-parameters/">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-parameters/</a>></span>) unless
   the encoding transformation is
   identical (as is the case for the compression codings defined in
   <a href="#content.codings" class="xref">Section 8.4.1</a>).<a href="#section-16.6.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.6.1-5">
   Values to be added to this namespace require IETF Review
   (see <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.8" class="relref">Section 4.8</a> of [<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>]</span>) and <span class="bcp14">MUST</span>
   conform to the purpose of content coding defined in
   <a href="#content.codings" class="xref">Section 8.4.1</a>.<a href="#section-16.6.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="considerations.for.new.content.codings">
<section id="section-16.6.2">
          <h4 id="name-considerations-for-new-cont">
<a href="#section-16.6.2" class="section-number selfRef">16.6.2. </a><a href="#name-considerations-for-new-cont" class="section-name selfRef">Considerations for New Content Codings</a>
          </h4>
<p id="section-16.6.2-1">
   New content codings ought to be self-descriptive whenever possible, with
   optional parameters discoverable within the coding format itself, rather
   than rely on external metadata that might be lost during transit.<a href="#section-16.6.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="upgrade.token.registry">
<section id="section-16.7">
        <h3 id="name-upgrade-token-registry">
<a href="#section-16.7" class="section-number selfRef">16.7. </a><a href="#name-upgrade-token-registry" class="section-name selfRef">Upgrade Token Registry</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-16.7-1">
   The "Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) Upgrade Token Registry" defines
   the namespace for protocol-name tokens used to identify protocols in the
   <a href="#field.upgrade" class="xref">Upgrade</a> header field. The registry is maintained at
   <span><<a href="https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-upgrade-tokens">https://www.iana.org/assignments/http-upgrade-tokens</a>></span>.<a href="#section-16.7-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.7-2">
   Each registered protocol name is associated with contact information
   and an optional set of specifications that details how the connection
   will be processed after it has been upgraded.<a href="#section-16.7-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-16.7-3">
   Registrations happen on a "First Come First Served" basis (see
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8126#section-4.4" class="relref">Section 4.4</a> of [<a href="#RFC8126" class="xref">RFC8126</a>]</span>) and are subject to the
   following rules:<a href="#section-16.7-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<ol start="1" type="1" class="normal type-1" id="section-16.7-4">
               <li id="section-16.7-4.1">A protocol-name token, once registered, stays registered forever.<a href="#section-16.7-4.1" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.2">A protocol-name token is case-insensitive and registered with the
       preferred case to be generated by senders.<a href="#section-16.7-4.2" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.3">The registration <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> name a responsible party for the
       registration.<a href="#section-16.7-4.3" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.4">The registration <span class="bcp14">MUST</span> name a point of contact.<a href="#section-16.7-4.4" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.5">The registration <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> name a set of specifications associated with
       that token. Such specifications need not be publicly available.<a href="#section-16.7-4.5" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.6">The registration <span class="bcp14">SHOULD</span> name a set of expected "protocol-version"
       tokens associated with that token at the time of registration.<a href="#section-16.7-4.6" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.7">The responsible party <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> change the registration at any time.
       The IANA will keep a record of all such changes, and make them
       available upon request.<a href="#section-16.7-4.7" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
          <li id="section-16.7-4.8">The IESG <span class="bcp14">MAY</span> reassign responsibility for a protocol token.
       This will normally only be used in the case when a
       responsible party cannot be contacted.<a href="#section-16.7-4.8" class="pilcrow">¶</a>
</li>
        </ol>
</section>
</div>
</section>
</div>
<div id="security.considerations">
<section id="section-17">
      <h2 id="name-security-considerations">
<a href="#section-17" class="section-number selfRef">17. </a><a href="#name-security-considerations" class="section-name selfRef">Security Considerations</a>
      </h2>
<p id="section-17-1">
   This section is meant to inform developers, information providers, and
   users of known security concerns relevant to HTTP semantics and its
   use for transferring information over the Internet. Considerations related
   to caching are discussed in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-7" class="relref">Section 7</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>,
   and considerations related to HTTP/1.1 message syntax and parsing are
   discussed in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112#section-11" class="relref">Section 11</a> of [<a href="#HTTP11" class="xref">HTTP/1.1</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-17-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17-2">
   The list of considerations below is not exhaustive. Most security concerns
   related to HTTP semantics are about securing server-side applications (code
   behind the HTTP interface), securing user agent processing of content
   received via HTTP, or secure use of the Internet in general, rather than
   security of the protocol. The security considerations for URIs, which
   are fundamental to HTTP operation, are discussed in
   <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-7" class="relref">Section 7</a> of [<a href="#URI" class="xref">URI</a>]</span>. Various organizations maintain
   topical information and links to current research on Web application
   security (e.g., <span>[<a href="#OWASP" class="xref">OWASP</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-17-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<div id="establishing.authority">
<section id="section-17.1">
        <h3 id="name-establishing-authority">
<a href="#section-17.1" class="section-number selfRef">17.1. </a><a href="#name-establishing-authority" class="section-name selfRef">Establishing Authority</a>
        </h3>
<span id="iref-authoritative-response-454" class="iref"></span>
        <span id="iref-phishing-455" class="iref"></span>
        <p id="section-17.1-1">
   HTTP relies on the notion of an "authoritative response": a
   response that has been determined by (or at the direction of) the origin
   server identified within the target URI to be the most appropriate response
   for that request given the state of the target resource at the time of
   response message origination.<a href="#section-17.1-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.1-2">
   When a registered name is used in the authority component, the "http" URI
   scheme (<a href="#http.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.1</a>) relies on the user's local name
   resolution service to determine where it can find authoritative responses.
   This means that any attack on a user's network host table, cached names,
   or name resolution libraries becomes an avenue for attack on establishing
   authority for "http" URIs. Likewise, the user's choice of server for
   Domain Name Service (DNS), and the hierarchy of servers from which it
   obtains resolution results, could impact the authenticity of address
   mappings; DNS Security Extensions (DNSSEC, <span>[<a href="#RFC4033" class="xref">RFC4033</a>]</span>) are
   one way to improve authenticity, as are the various mechanisms for making
   DNS requests over more secure transfer protocols.<a href="#section-17.1-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.1-3">
   Furthermore, after an IP address is obtained, establishing authority for
   an "http" URI is vulnerable to attacks on Internet Protocol routing.<a href="#section-17.1-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.1-4">
   The "https" scheme (<a href="#https.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.2</a>) is intended to prevent
   (or at least reveal) many of these potential attacks on establishing
   authority, provided that the negotiated connection is secured and
   the client properly verifies that the communicating server's identity
   matches the target URI's authority component
   (<a href="#https.verify" class="xref">Section 4.3.4</a>). Correctly implementing such verification
   can be difficult (see <span>[<a href="#Georgiev" class="xref">Georgiev</a>]</span>).<a href="#section-17.1-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.1-5">
   Authority for a given origin server can be delegated through protocol
   extensions; for example, <span>[<a href="#ALTSVC" class="xref">ALTSVC</a>]</span>. Likewise, the set of
   servers for which a connection is considered authoritative can be changed
   with a protocol extension like <span>[<a href="#RFC8336" class="xref">RFC8336</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-17.1-5" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.1-6">
   Providing a response from a non-authoritative source, such as a shared
   proxy cache, is often useful to improve performance and availability, but
   only to the extent that the source can be trusted or the distrusted
   response can be safely used.<a href="#section-17.1-6" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.1-7">
   Unfortunately, communicating authority to users can be difficult.
   For example, "phishing" is an attack on the user's perception
   of authority, where that perception can be misled by presenting similar
   branding in hypertext, possibly aided by userinfo obfuscating the authority
   component (see <a href="#http.uri" class="xref">Section 4.2.1</a>).
   User agents can reduce the impact of phishing attacks by enabling users to
   easily inspect a target URI prior to making an action, by prominently
   distinguishing (or rejecting) userinfo when present, and by not sending
   stored credentials and cookies when the referring document is from an
   unknown or untrusted source.<a href="#section-17.1-7" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="risks.intermediaries">
<section id="section-17.2">
        <h3 id="name-risks-of-intermediaries">
<a href="#section-17.2" class="section-number selfRef">17.2. </a><a href="#name-risks-of-intermediaries" class="section-name selfRef">Risks of Intermediaries</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-17.2-1">
   HTTP intermediaries are inherently situated for on-path attacks.
   Compromise of
   the systems on which the intermediaries run can result in serious security
   and privacy problems. Intermediaries might have access to security-related
   information, personal information about individual users and
   organizations, and proprietary information belonging to users and
   content providers. A compromised intermediary, or an intermediary
   implemented or configured without regard to security and privacy
   considerations, might be used in the commission of a wide range of
   potential attacks.<a href="#section-17.2-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.2-2">
   Intermediaries that contain a shared cache are especially vulnerable
   to cache poisoning attacks, as described in <span><a href="https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111#section-7" class="relref">Section 7</a> of [<a href="#CACHING" class="xref">CACHING</a>]</span>.<a href="#section-17.2-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.2-3">
   Implementers need to consider the privacy and security
   implications of their design and coding decisions, and of the
   configuration options they provide to operators (especially the
   default configuration).<a href="#section-17.2-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.2-4">
   Intermediaries are no more trustworthy than the people and policies
   under which they operate; HTTP cannot solve this problem.<a href="#section-17.2-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="attack.pathname">
<section id="section-17.3">
        <h3 id="name-attacks-based-on-file-and-p">
<a href="#section-17.3" class="section-number selfRef">17.3. </a><a href="#name-attacks-based-on-file-and-p" class="section-name selfRef">Attacks Based on File and Path Names</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-17.3-1">
   Origin servers frequently make use of their local file system to manage the
   mapping from target URI to resource representations.
   Most file systems are not designed to protect against malicious file
   or path names. Therefore, an origin server needs to avoid accessing
   names that have a special significance to the system when mapping the
   target resource to files, folders, or directories.<a href="#section-17.3-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.3-2">
   For example, UNIX, Microsoft Windows, and other operating systems use ".."
   as a path component to indicate a directory level above the current one,
   and they use specially named paths or file names to send data to system devices.
   Similar naming conventions might exist within other types of storage
   systems. Likewise, local storage systems have an annoying tendency to
   prefer user-friendliness over security when handling invalid or unexpected
   characters, recomposition of decomposed characters, and case-normalization
   of case-insensitive names.<a href="#section-17.3-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.3-3">
   Attacks based on such special names tend to focus on either denial-of-service
   (e.g., telling the server to read from a COM port) or disclosure
   of configuration and source files that are not meant to be served.<a href="#section-17.3-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="attack.injection">
<section id="section-17.4">
        <h3 id="name-attacks-based-on-command-co">
<a href="#section-17.4" class="section-number selfRef">17.4. </a><a href="#name-attacks-based-on-command-co" class="section-name selfRef">Attacks Based on Command, Code, or Query Injection</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-17.4-1">
   Origin servers often use parameters within the URI as a
   means of identifying system services, selecting database entries, or
   choosing a data source. However, data received in a request cannot be
   trusted. An attacker could construct any of the request data elements
   (method, target URI, header fields, or content) to contain data that might
   be misinterpreted as a command, code, or query when passed through a
   command invocation, language interpreter, or database interface.<a href="#section-17.4-1" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.4-2">
   For example, SQL injection is a common attack wherein additional query
   language is inserted within some part of the target URI or header
   fields (e.g., <a href="#field.host" class="xref">Host</a>, <a href="#field.referer" class="xref">Referer</a>, etc.).
   If the received data is used directly within a SELECT statement, the
   query language might be interpreted as a database command instead of a
   simple string value. This type of implementation vulnerability is extremely
   common, in spite of being easy to prevent.<a href="#section-17.4-2" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.4-3">
   In general, resource implementations ought to avoid use of request data
   in contexts that are processed or interpreted as instructions.  Parameters
   ought to be compared to fixed strings and acted upon as a result of that
   comparison, rather than passed through an interface that is not prepared
   for untrusted data. Received data that isn't based on fixed parameters
   ought to be carefully filtered or encoded to avoid being misinterpreted.<a href="#section-17.4-3" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
<p id="section-17.4-4">
   Similar considerations apply to request data when it is stored and later
   processed, such as within log files, monitoring tools, or when included
   within a data format that allows embedded scripts.<a href="#section-17.4-4" class="pilcrow">¶</a></p>
</section>
</div>
<div id="attack.protocol.element.length">
<section id="section-17.5">
        <h3 id="name-attacks-via-protocol-elemen">
<a href="#section-17.5" class="section-number selfRef">17.5. </a><a href="#name-attacks-via-protocol-elemen" class="section-name selfRef">Attacks via Protocol Element Length</a>
        </h3>
<p id="section-17.5-1">
   Because HTTP uses mostly textual, character-delimited fields, parsers are
   often vulnerable to attacks based on sending very long (or very slow)
   streams of data, particularly where an implementation is expecting a
